structure.rationale: The episode meanders from John Cena's hair transplant into Supreme Court commentary without a clear transition, and the discussion drifts loosely between transgender sports, gay marriage history, and bathroom policy with no discernible segment structure.

relevance.rationale: The episode directly engages a specific Supreme Court ruling on transgender issues and related state-vs-federal jurisdiction questions, making it substantively on-topic for News/Politics, though the opening segment is off-topic celebrity chatter.

clipability.rationale: The slippery-slope argument and the 'we have to differentiate' passage have some standalone potential, but most moments require surrounding context or are embedded in rambling tangents that undercut clean extraction.

card.tagline: Supreme Court's transgender ruling: what it means for every state's laws
card.description: Chuck Douglas breaks down the Supreme Court's transgender decision and argues that erasing gender distinctions threatens the legal foundation of civil rights legislation nationwide.
card.reason: Because this episode covers a major Supreme Court ruling on gender and identity

scored.feedTitle: The 405 Coffee Break with O.K. Solberg
scored.description:
Greetings and Welcome to The 405 Coffee Break with O.K. Solberg

New episodes tend to air over the local KMMR radio station @ 5 minutes past 4PM each M-F. And have been doing so, nearly every week since Sept 2018.

I'm D.J. Rasmussen, O.K.s friend since junior high, possibly your neighbor & this websites maintainer, whom strives to get each episode's show notes written, the transcript proofed and the audio posted to the internet within a few hours of that days KMMR air time. NOTE: recently been publishing most new releases by 4:30PM.

Thanks for visiting and I hope you enjoy the time we can spend together.

latestEpisode.title: The 405 Aired July 8th, 2026

But if you take 37 and multiply it times 6 you get 2-2-2. And if you take 37 times 9 you get 3-3-3. And if you take 37 times 12 you get 4-4-4 all the way up. 00:02:14.395 --> 00:02:28.260 37 times 27 equals 9-9-9. And I don't care who you are. That's just fun. 111-222-333-444 and on it goes. I like to learn. 00:02:28.260 --> 00:02:46.060 It's fun to learn. So always remember and never forget the thing that I learned way back. If you need something done, get a busy person to do it. That concept is so important. And there are other sayings that mirror the same meaning. 00:02:46.060 --> 00:03:10.820 Know guys, a concept that gets used a lot and is very meaningful gets worded in different ways in different places. People who get things done don't wait for perfect conditions. That's another one that's out there or this one. The more you do, the more you can do. And finally, excellence is a habit, not an event. 00:03:10.980 --> 00:03:31.505 Excellence is a habit, not an event. But listen one last time to our saying for today. And as you listen, notice how at first it seems backwards. If you need something done, get a busy person to do it. Now, pray tell, why does that hold true most of the time? 00:03:31.505 --> 00:03:51.905 Why is that? Well, the deeper meaning is not that busy people have more free time. They usually have less. But it's inferring that people who are already accomplishing things tend to have qualities that make them reliable. They know how to prioritize. 00:03:52.705 --> 00:04:11.560 They distinguish between what is important and what can wait. They have momentum. People who are already active often find it easier to keep moving than someone who is inactive. They have developed discipline. Their schedule isn't necessarily lighter. 00:04:11.640 --> 00:04:38.350 They're simply better at managing it. They tend to keep commitments as well. A busy person often has a reputation to protect. So when they say yes, they usually follow through and they keep that good reputation going. Now two questions. Two questions for you. We're almost done. Sit up. Pay attention. Do you admire a person who gets things done? 00:04:40.270 --> 00:05:11.550 And 2ndly, are you that kind of person? Listen to this bible verse, Proverbs 22:29 Do you see a man skilled in his work? He will stand before kings. He will not stand before obscure men. Proverbs 22 verse 29 Busy people don't necessarily have more hours in the day. They simply have fewer wasted hours. 00:05:12.116 --> 00:05:18.676 So until next time, as you go out there, remember now, don't be bitter.

relevance.rationale: This episode covers commodity prices and motivational life philosophy with no connection to current news or political events.

clipability.rationale: The '37 times 3 equals 111' segment and the Proverbs 22:29 quote are self-contained moments, but most of the episode requires the warm, conversational setup to land with any impact.

card.tagline: Wheat prices, magic math, and why busy people always get the call
card.description: O.K. Solberg opens with commodity prices then unpacks why the most overloaded person in the room is always the right one to ask.

structure.rationale: Conversational but follows a clear throughline moving from convention energy to Black voter outreach to Crockett's absence to party chair dynamics, though transitions are loose and pacing is uneven.

relevance.rationale: Directly covers the Texas Democratic Party convention, a live intraparty unity fight between Talerico and Crockett, and a contested party chair re-election — all timely, specific Texas political developments.

clipability.rationale: The Crockett-as-afterthought exchange and the Cornyn/Crockett 'sour grapes' parallel are quotable standalone moments, but most insights require the surrounding conversation to land fully.

card.tagline: Texas Dems claim unity — but Jasmine Crockett wasn't even invited
card.description: TribCast breaks down the Texas Democratic convention: Talerico's Black voter problem, Crockett's conspicuous absence, and a party chair who won 75% despite internal revolt.
card.reason: Because this episode covers the Texas Democratic Party's biggest internal fault lines right now

scored.description:
The Diddy Diaries: The Downfall of Sean Combs is a raw, unflinching look at the dramatic collapse of one of hip-hop’s biggest moguls. For decades, Sean 'Diddy' Combs seemed untouchable—a figure who rose from the streets to become a titan of the music, fashion, and entertainment industries. His Bad Boy Entertainment label defined a generation of hip-hop, and his entrepreneurial spirit made him a household name. But beneath the surface of this glittering success lay darker currents—currents that have now surged into the spotlight as the empire he built threatens to crumble beneath the weight of scandal and serious criminal allegations.

This podcast dives deep into the allegations that have sent shockwaves through the entertainment world. At the center of it all are the accusers—those who have come forward with shocking claims of misconduct, manipulation, and abuse. These aren’t just sensational headlines—they are stories that expose a side of Sean Combs the public never saw. The Diddy Diaries explores how these accusations began to surface, what drove his accusers to finally speak out, and how the legal system has responded.

But this isn’t just about Diddy’s public image taking a hit—The Diddy Diaries zeroes in on the high-stakes battle that Diddy now faces to stay out of jail. Each episode unpacks the legal drama as it unfolds in real-time: the charges, the court proceedings, and the fight for his freedom. Once a man who had it all—fame, fortune, and influence—Diddy now stands at the center of a legal storm that threatens to strip him of everything, including his freedom.


We trace the events that led up to this moment, looking at how Diddy’s larger-than-life persona masked a pattern of behavior that would ultimately bring him to this point. Listeners will get a front-row seat to the high-stakes legal battles, where the possibility of incarceration looms large. The tension is palpable as Diddy’s legal team works to combat the mounting evidence against him, while his accusers push for justice.

Through riveting storytelling, The Diddy Diaries paints a full picture of Diddy’s downfall, from the heights of his stardom to the depths of his legal fight for survival. This is not a story about maintaining power or influence—it’s a fight to avoid the ultimate consequence: prison. As the allegations mount and the legal drama intensifies, The Diddy Diaries delivers a compelling narrative of a man grappling with the very real possibility of losing everything he once held dear.

With every episode, listeners are drawn deeper into this unfolding saga of celebrity, power, and justice. The Diddy Diaries not only explores the high-profile case but also delves into the human cost behind the headlines. As Diddy’s world unravels, so too does the carefully constructed image he spent decades building. This podcast is a must-listen for anyone who wants to understand the full scope of Sean Combs’ downfall, where the stakes couldn’t be higher and the outcome remains uncertain.

Prepare for a rollercoaster ride through the most shocking and consequential scandal in recent entertainment history. Tune in to The Diddy Diaries: The Downfall of Sean Combs to witness the dramatic fall of a man who once ruled the music world but now fights for his freedom in the face of life-altering allegations.

relevance.rationale: The episode substantively engages with Prince Andrew's alibi in the Epstein-connected trafficking scandal, a real political and institutional accountability story, but the show's framing around celebrity scandal (Diddy Diaries) and its rhetorical rather than reportorial approach keeps it from the top tier.

clipability.rationale: Multiple passages are genuinely self-contained and punchy — particularly the 'survivor gets a microscope, a prince gets a cushion' line and the Pizza Express committee bit — requiring minimal setup to land as standalone previews.

structure.rationale: The episode has loose named segments (e.g., 'war over democracy') but conversation meanders between topics with inconsistent pacing, frequent tangents, and no clear signposting between subjects.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers specific current political developments including Trump DOJ threats to state election officials, federal grant extortion allegations, a federal judge blocking DOJ access to 2020 election worker names, and Trump's diplomatic friction with Italy's Prime Minister — substantive and timely but delivered in casual bar-chat format.

clipability.rationale: A few punchy lines exist — notably the extortion framing of federal grant conditions and the 'no high school student council has nuclear weapons' quip — but most moments require surrounding context from the multi-speaker bar-format conversation to land.

card.tagline: Trump's DOJ is threatening all 50 states — is that extortion?
card.description: Three political commentators break down Trump DOJ election threats, federal grant coercion, a judge blocking access to Georgia election workers, and Rubio's Cuba policy.
card.reason: Because this episode digs into this week's most alarming election oversight story

latestEpisode.title: Barely Possible - 2026-07-09

clip.text: of dependence — companies getting cut off from models, access getting rationed, vendors changing terms. And here's one of the most prominent researchers in the field saying, out loud, that the whole risk is concentration, and the whole answer is openness. Whether you buy his solution or not — and reasonable people fight about it — the diagnosis is the one that keeps showing up from every direction this week. The professor's in-person exam, the Lovable moat question, the efficiency arms race, LeCun's tweet — they're all circling the same anxiety: how much of what you depend on do you actually control? Alright, let me shift gears entirely, from the

transcript.scoringWindow: today that tells you exactly where the market's head is at. Lovable, the AI app-building company, is reportedly in talks to double its valuation to thirteen-point-two billion dollars. The round is around three hundred million, reportedly led by Menlo Ventures, and this is per a report from Sifted. Double. In one round. This is a company in the vibe-coding, build-an-app-by-describing-it space, and the market is saying it's worth twice what it was worth not long ago. I'm going to give this to you straight, because I've watched a lot of valuation cycles. A double is a signal, and it's a signal that cuts two ways. On the bull side: this whole category — describe what you want, get working software — is clearly resonating with real users and real revenue, or nobody's writing a three hundred million dollar check at that price. Menlo isn't a tourist. On the bear side: this is exactly the kind of category where the moat is the question that keeps founders up at night. When the underlying models get better, a lot of the magic that a company like this provides gets easier for anyone to provide. The thing you're building on top of is improving underneath you, which is great until it improves so much that it eats your product. We talked, not long ago on this show, about Figma acquiring the team behind a vibe-coding app, and about Vercel's CEO out there arguing about splitting models off from the agents that use them. This Lovable number is another data point in that same current: everybody with money believes the app-building layer is where a huge amount of value lands, and everybody is racing to plant a flag before the ground shifts. Thirteen billion dollars is the market betting that the interface to software creation is worth owning. Maybe it is. But if you're building in this space, the question you should be asking is not "how do I ride this wave," it's "what do I own that survives the next model release." Because the next model release is always coming. And on cue — the next model release came this week too. Let me run through the model news quickly, because there's a bunch of it and most of it doesn't need a dissertation. Grok got a new version, 4.5, out of Musk's operation. Elon's describing it as an, quote, Opus-class model, and the pitch is that it's cheaper, more token-efficient, and lower cost than the other powerful models. Now, "Opus-class" is Elon-grading-his-own-homework, so put whatever discount on that you normally put on a founder describing his own product. But the interesting part isn't the capability claim, it's the positioning. Notice he's not leading with "it's the smartest." He's leading with "it's cheaper and more efficient." That's a tell about where the whole industry's mind is at right now. Six months ago every launch was a raw-power flex. Now the flex is efficiency. Everybody figured out at roughly the same time that when you're running agents that make thousands of calls, the cost per token is the thing that decides whether your product has a business model or not. We've been circling this expense-management theme for a while on the show, and I'm not going to belabor it, but I'll just point out: even the guy who loves a superlative is now selling you on cheaper. That's the market talking. On the OpenAI side, Sam Altman announced GPT-live, a next-generation voice mode launching in ChatGPT. And his framing was personal and kind of interesting — he said he's always preferred typing to talking to an AI, and now he thinks that's going to shift. He called it magical and, quote, real. Take the marketing gloss off and there's a genuine product bet underneath. Voice has always been the interface that either feels natural or feels like you're arguing with a phone tree. If they've actually crossed the line where talking is better than typing for a meaningful chunk of tasks, that changes the shape of a lot of products, because voice pulls in the people who never wanted to type at a computer in the first place. That's a bigger addressable market than the keyboard crowd. I'm not going to tell you it's there until I hear it myself, but the direction is worth clocking. And then there's Google, quietly getting graded on its own report card. Google updated its Android developer benchmark — Android Bench — with a bunch of new models, and the headline from the coverage is a little awkward for them: their own Gemini still lags behind on it. I love this, honestly, because it's Google running an honest benchmark and then publishing the result even though the result makes their own model look middling. That's more integrity than a lot of these benchmark exercises show. For you as a builder, the takeaway is small but real: don't assume the house model is the best model for your task just because it's from the same house. Benchmark it yourself. The company that made the benchmark just showed you their own model isn't automatically winning. Now let me pull one thread out of all this model news, because Yann LeCun — Meta's chief AI scientist — dropped a line this morning that ties it together. Responding to someone, he said the biggest risk of AI is the concentration of power in a few dominant providers of proprietary AI assistants, and that the only solution to AI sovereignty is open-source foundation models. Now, I've got to give you the honest context here, because LeCun has been beating this open-source drum for years, and he works for a company whose whole AI strategy is built on releasing open weights, so this is not exactly a man discovering a new religion. Consider the source. But the reason I'm surfacing it is that it lands in a very specific moment. We've spent the last couple weeks on this show talking about the machinery of dependence — companies getting cut off from models, access getting rationed, vendors changing terms. And here's one of the most prominent researchers in the field saying, out loud, that the whole risk is concentration, and the whole answer is openness. Whether you buy his solution or not — and reasonable people fight about it — the diagnosis is the one that keeps showing up from every direction this week. The professor's in-person exam, the Lovable moat question, the efficiency arms race, LeCun's tweet — they're all circling the same anxiety: how much of what you depend on do you actually control? Alright, let me shift gears entirely, from the abstract to the concrete, because there's a cluster of stories about AI meeting the real world in ugly and messy ways, and a builder needs to see these clearly. The hardest one first, and I'll keep it brief and clinical because it deserves that. There's a lawsuit — reported by Ashley Belanger — involving Grok, Musk's image tool, alleging that a man used it to generate thousands of abuse images of a child, and that the company only flagged a tiny fraction of the offending prompts to authorities. More young girls are now suing X over AI-generated child sexual abuse material, with the platform accused of shielding predators. I'm not going to detail any of it beyond that. The reason it belongs in a builder's briefing, and not just a crime blotter, is this: if you ship a generative tool, you are shipping a machine that can be pointed at the worst thing a human can imagine, and the question of what you detect, what you report, and how fast you report it is not a nice-to-have compliance checkbox. It is core product safety, and the courts are now treating it that way. The allegation here isn't just that a bad thing happened — bad people will always try to misuse tools — it's that the company didn't report what it saw. That distinction is where the legal exposure lives. If you're building anything generative, your reporting pipeline is not an afterthought. It's load-bearing. On a much lighter note in the same "AI meets reality" bucket — Google's deepfake detection system got used this week to debunk a hoax. There was a picture going around that appeared to show Senator Mitch McConnell in a hospital bed, covered in tubes, in obvious distress. Turned out to be an AI-generated fake, and Google's detection system was used to call it. So there's your two-sided coin again: the same generative capability that produces the abuse images and the fake hospital photos is also spawning the detection tools that catch them. It's an arms race, and detection is always a step behind generation, but at least there's a defensive line forming. For anybody building in media or trust-and-safety, that detection layer is quietly becoming a product category of its own. Now let me dig into the deep dive, because there's one story that I think matters most for you, the founder, and it's the one about drones — but not the way you'd expect. Let me set it up with the flashy headline and then get to the part that actually matters. The flashy version: the U.S. is now hunting for cheaper hunter-killer drones after Iran destroyed roughly a billion dollars' worth of Reapers. The reporting, from Jeremy Hsu, is that American military drone losses in the Iran war have spurred a Pentagon call for cheap replacements. A billion dollars of these big, expensive, exquisite Reaper drones — gone. And the response isn't "build them tougher." The response is "build them cheaper." Sit with that, because it's one of the most important strategic ideas of our moment, and it applies way beyond the battlefield. For decades, the American way of building military hardware was what people call the exquisite approach. You build a small number of extraordinarily capable, extraordinarily expensive platforms. A Reaper drone is a masterpiece. It's got the sensors, the range, the payload, it's a flying luxury item. And the whole logic of that approach depended on an assumption: that your fancy thing wouldn't get shot down very often, because the enemy couldn't afford or couldn't manage to kill it. That assumption just broke. When the other side can knock a billion dollars of your exquisite platforms out of the sky, the exquisite model isn't just expensive — it's a losing trade. You're bringing a Fabergé egg to a rock fight. So the Pentagon's pivot is toward attritable systems. Ugly word, simple idea: things cheap enough that you're okay losing them. Instead of one perfect drone that costs a fortune and breaks your heart when it dies, you want a swarm of cheap ones where losing a few is just Tuesday. The math flips. Quantity becomes its own kind of quality. And the whole industrial base has to reorient — away from building a handful of perfect things slowly, toward building a flood of good-enough things fast. Now, why am I making this the centerpiece for a room full of founders and builders? Because this is the single most important cost-structure debate of the entire AI era, and it's playing out in the exact same shape everywhere you look. Think about what we just talked about all episode. The frontier model versus the cheap efficient model — that's exquisite versus attritable. The expensive, perfect, do-everything model is the Reaper. And the whole industry's pivot toward cheaper, token-efficient, good-enough models that you can run by the thousands — Grok's efficiency pitch, Microsoft's in-house cost-cutting models we talked about earlier this week, everybody suddenly bragging about being lower cost — that's the attritable swarm. The Pentagon and the AI industry independently arrived at the same conclusion in the same season: when you have to run the thing constantly, at scale, in the real world, the exquisite version bankrupts you. Cheap and plentiful beats perfect and scarce. And it goes deeper than models. Think about how

structure.rationale: The episode has a clear throughline and deliberate segment transitions — market news, model roundup, real-world AI harms, deep dive — with consistent signposting, though pacing is dense and the deep dive runs long without a visible close.

relevance.rationale: The episode is primarily a tech/startup/AI industry briefing covering venture valuations, model releases, and drone procurement strategy — not a News/Politics episode despite brief mentions of Musk, a senator, and military spending.

clipability.rationale: Several self-contained, quotable moments exist — the Fabergé egg line, the 'exquisite versus attritable' framing, and the CSAM reporting-pipeline warning — each punchy enough to stand alone as a preview.

card.tagline: Why the Pentagon's drone pivot is the AI industry's most important cost lesson
card.description: From Lovable's $13B valuation to attritable drone swarms, this episode argues cheap-and-plentiful is beating perfect-and-scarce across every frontier.
card.reason: Because this episode reframes the AI cost debate through a military strategy lens

scored.description:
"The Neutral Corner" boxing podcast covers all the latest news in the fight game. Every Monday, host Michael Montero reviews/previews all the fights from around the world and takes listener calls. He is never afraid to ruffle feathers when he gives hot takes or goes on heated rants.

Montero has contributed to numerous boxing publications in multiple countries, performed ringside commentary around the USA and is an International Boxing Hall of Fame voter.

latestEpisode.title: Episode 389: Jaron "Boots" Ennis stops Xander Zayas; Oleksandr Usyk vacates Titles

But it's perspective that counts, right, That's what it is ultimately that you have to look at. Because and this goes to something you tweeted on Monday, I think, or it was this week after this Xander fight. You said you were harping on the lack of fights that fighters are getting, and you talked about the problem and it's a huge problem, and it's become an increasingly more bigger problem. And I think maybe there is a connection there between this reactionary feeling that we're getting more than any other era that I can remember, and based on the fact that fighters aren't fighting, you know, the guys that we want to see the most aren't fighting. You had a list of about a dozen guys that are some of the biggest names in the sport that have so many different fights in front of them that they could make, and most of them haven't fought this year. In fact, I don't think any of them on that list had fought that year this year, and so there may be some correlation there. Right. I don't even know if you can blame the fight fans at this point for that kind of reactionary behavior. But ultimately, what did we learn in this fight? Coming into it? I think we had the evidence to suggest that Boots was the overall more talented fighter from athleticism to you know, you could look at his bagwork, you could look at his mitwork, you could look at pass fights, and I think you could sit a casual fan down and actually show the distinctions between Xander and Boots that would say, hey, yeah, that guy is the better fighter, or that guy looks better to me or whatever, you know what I mean, the difference between a high school hitter hitting BP versus Mike trout right, or maybe not that, but you know what I'm trying to say. So, ultimately, what we didn't know. We knew he could box, Could he fight? Yes, we've seen him hit, Yes, we've seen him you know, touched up a little more, but never again somebody that could actually give resistance, and so much has been made about whether or not Xander had the power going into it to make a difference, or the speed and all that's valid, right, but ultimately it's about performance. Once you get to this elite level of fighting, there is no more that a coach can bestow upon you to make a pretty boy Floyd become Floyd money Mayweather. That's not how it works. You have to get in the ring. No amount of sparring can ever account for you leveling up. That's not the way this sport of all sports work. I don't even think any professional sports work that way. And so ultimately you have to prove that not only can you sink those threes from behind the arc, you know, eight feet behind, ten feet behind, but now do it in front of a defender, But now do it in front of an elite defender. What do you look like? And so that was the question I think going into the fight, right, we saw what Xander was able to do with Burrow, and that ultimately for me made that fight feel more fifty to fifty, not because Xander was in the league of Boots, but more because the fight game works in a way that experience goes hand in hand with that ability once you get to those elite moments. By the way, the bright lights can absolutely shrink a fighter. Oh we've seen it happen before, right, It's like, how do you account for a fighter learning to be okay with his own blood for the first time. You don't. It's experience and all of that I think amounted. One other aspect of this

structure.rationale: The conversation follows a loose interview format with some thematic throughlines around Louisville's convention business, but pacing is uneven and transitions between topics are driven by the guest rather than deliberate editorial structure.

relevance.rationale: This episode is a local tourism and convention center promotion interview with no substantive news or political content at its core — the passing mention of partisan politics is incidental to what is fundamentally a civic boosterism segment.

clipability.rationale: Most moments require significant context about who David Beck is and what role he holds, making standalone clips difficult; the 'petty partisan politics' line is the only near-self-contained moment in the excerpt.

card.tagline: Louisville's top tourism ambassador may be pushed out by partisan politics
card.description: Terry Meiners makes the case for keeping David Beck working Louisville's convention and tourism pipeline as a political transition threatens to disrupt years of relationship-building.
card.reason: Because this episode spotlights a local leadership dispute affecting Louisville's economic future

"Tavis Smiley" is the flagship program for KBLA, America's newest progressive radio network and LA's first Black-owned talk station. Broadcast live across southern California on 1580AM and around the world on KBLA1580.com and TuneIn, this podcast presents the best of Tavis Smiley's conversations with activists, influencers, thought leaders, and cultural vanguards.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/tavis-smiley--6286410/support.

structure.rationale: There is a discernible throughline moving from intra-Democratic ideological conflict to third-party prospects to DSA victories to defund-the-police, but transitions are conversational and loose, with a mid-segment Anita Baker digression that disrupts the political flow.

relevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with live political fault lines — DSA electoral wins, Democratic Party ideological fractures, defund-the-police debates, and third-party viability — but the published date of 2026-06-30 makes some references (Bernie 2020, early DSA wins) feel temporally ambiguous rather than sharply current.

clipability.rationale: Several passages — West's 'neoliberal plantation' line, 'if all we have is the Democratic Party you're gonna lead folk into the crackhouse,' and the Bernie's babies framing — are punchy and self-contained enough to work as standalone previews without heavy setup.

card.tagline: Cornel West calls the Democratic Party a neoliberal plantation — live on air
card.description: Tavis and Dr. West debate whether the Democratic Party can survive its left-right civil war, third-party viability, and what DSA wins mean for Black political power.
card.reason: Because this episode cuts to the heart of the Democratic Party's ideological crisis

scored.description:
Live from West Virginia’s Capital City, with stories from every corner of the Mountain State. From Noon to 3 p.m., MetroNews Midday connects West Virginians with the news that matters most — hosted by 13 News and Tonight Live anchor Amanda Barren and Dave Allen of MetroNews. Amanda and Dave deliver up-to-the-minute headlines and breaking news while amplifying voices from around the state through insightful interviews and real-time conversation.

📺 Watch Hour 3 live on any device with MetroNews TV
📱 Join the conversation by texting 304-TALK-304 or call 800-765-TALK

 With featured segments like:
  • Mayor Monday – spotlighting towns and cities across the state
  • Wonderful West Virginian – honoring unsung heroes for their generosity and community spirit
MetroNews Midday is your live, local, and lively midday connection to West Virginia.

latestEpisode.title: MetroNews Midday Wednesday July 8, 2026 Hour 3

structure.rationale: The segment is a loose, rapid-fire read-through of listener texts with no clear throughline, interrupted by commercial breaks and off-topic banter about a TV show role.

relevance.rationale: The episode touches on specific current political issues — federal voter data collection, Iran ceasefire contradictions, WV school funding, and Japanese company expansion — but coverage is fragmented and listener-text-driven rather than substantively developed.

clipability.rationale: The Iran ceasefire text read-aloud ('the president says the ceasefire is over...which is it, mister president?') is the only moment with standalone punch, but most content requires surrounding context to land.

latestEpisode.title: Main character energy: qual è il potere nascosto di ogni segno zodiacale? Scopri il tuo!

clip.text: Il cancro è considerato uno dei segni zodiacali più romantici e sensibili. Chi ha cancro o ha una crash di questo segno tende a vivere le emozioni in modo profondo e cerca sempre amore e dolcezza nelle relazioni. Secondo le stelle, la compatibilità migliore in amore per il cancro si trova con lo scorpione, i pesci e il toro. Con lo scorpione nasce una connessione intensa e profonda. Vi sentite protetti e riuscite a tirar fuori il meglio l'uno dall'altro. nonostante qualche piccolo momento di gerosia. Con i pesci c'è una sintonia emotiva unica, fatta di coccole e sostegno reciproco, che vi permette di vivere una storia dolce e sognante.

transcript.scoringWindow: Il cancro è considerato uno dei segni zodiacali più romantici e sensibili. Secondo le stelle, la compatibilità migliore in amore per il cancro si trova con lo scorpione, i pesci e il toro. Con lo scorpione nasce una connessione intensa e profonda. Vi sentite protetti e riuscite a tirar fuori il meglio l'uno dall'altro. nonostante qualche piccolo momento di gerosia. Con i pesci c'è una sintonia emotiva unica, fatta di coccole e sostegno reciproco, che vi permette di vivere una storia dolce e sognante. Il toro, invece, garantisce stabilità e sicurezza. Insieme vi sentite davvero a casa e riuscite a costruire una relazione solida e duratura. Le combinazioni meno fortunate includono i gemelli, l'ariete e l'acquario. Con i gemelli è difficile trovarsi, perché voi cercate sicurezza mentre loro amano la libertà, portando spesso a incomprensioni. Con l'ariete c'è attrazione, ma le differenze nel modo di affrontare i problemi possono creare tensioni e richiedere molta pazienza. Con l'acquario vedete l'amore in modo troppo diverso. Voi volete vicinanza, loro indipendenza, e rischiate di sentirvi trascurati. Ricordate però che questi sono solo suggerimenti legati alle caratteristiche dei segni zodiacali. Ognuno è unico.

structure.rationale: The episode follows a loose but recognizable pattern — best matches, then worst matches, then a disclaimer — but it reads as a flat list with minimal transitions or deliberate pacing.

relevance.rationale: This episode covers zodiac sign romantic compatibility for Cancer, which has no connection to news or political events whatsoever.

clipability.rationale: Individual sign pairings are briefly self-contained, but they are formulaic and interchangeable, offering little that would function as a compelling standalone hook.

scored.description:
Beyond the Horizon is a project that aims to dig a bit deeper than just the surface level that we are so used to with the legacy media while at the same time attempting to side step the gaslighting and rhetoric in search of the truth. From the day to day news that dominates the headlines to more complex geopolitical issues that effect all of our lives, we will be exploring them all.

It's time to stop settling for what is force fed to us and it's time to look beyond the horizon.

First comes denial, Then comes minimization, 230 00:15:29.799 --> 00:15:32.799 Then comes the claim that the story is a distraction. 231 00:15:33.480 --> 00:15:35.679 Then comes the pivot to the greater evil on the 232 00:15:35.720 --> 00:15:39.279 other side. Then, when the facts become too heavy, comes 233 00:15:39.279 --> 00:15:42.399 a sudden statement of concern. Finally, comes the attempt to 234 00:15:42.399 --> 00:15:46.559 move on as quickly as possible. That playbook is not accountability, 235 00:15:46.960 --> 00:15:51.440 its containment, its crisis management, and it's exactly why Americans 236 00:15:51.440 --> 00:15:55.799 increasingly believe both parties use morality as a weapon, not 237 00:15:55.879 --> 00:16:00.720 a standard. What should have happened is simple. Democrats who 238 00:16:00.759 --> 00:16:03.840 knew of the red flag should have treated them as disqualifying, or, 239 00:16:03.840 --> 00:16:07.360 at a minimum, demanded a level of public accountability before 240 00:16:07.440 --> 00:16:10.879 lending their names. They should have asked whether their own 241 00:16:10.960 --> 00:16:16.000 rhetoric about survivors, abuse power, and institutional rot meant anything 242 00:16:16.159 --> 00:16:18.879 when the issue touched their side. They should have remembered 243 00:16:18.919 --> 00:16:21.679 that vetting is not an act of betrayal. They should 244 00:16:21.679 --> 00:16:25.120 have understood that moral seriousness is proven when it costs 245 00:16:25.120 --> 00:16:28.919 you something. They should have refused to let anti establishment 246 00:16:29.039 --> 00:16:33.440 romance override basic judgment. They should have stopped confusing charisma 247 00:16:33.639 --> 00:16:37.159 with credibility. They should have understood that a candidate can 248 00:16:37.200 --> 00:16:40.000 say the right things about billionaires and still be the 249 00:16:40.039 --> 00:16:43.080 wrong person to elevate. They should have lived by the 250 00:16:43.120 --> 00:16:47.639 standards they preach and folks. They did not, and that 251 00:16:47.679 --> 00:16:52.159 failure now bleeds into their Epstein posture. Every time Democrats 252 00:16:52.200 --> 00:16:55.320 demand answers about Epstein. scored.feedTitle: Good News York by Growth Mode Content
scored.description:
Your go-to podcast for inspiring stories, uplifting news, and positive vibes from across New York State!

Whether it's groundbreaking innovations, community heroes, or businesses making a difference, we bring you the stories that remind us all of the good happening around us.

Join us as we highlight the changemakers, dreamers, and everyday folks bringing positivity to the Empire State.

From local businesses thriving in Syracuse to heartwarming tales from Buffalo, Albany, and beyond—this is the news you’ll actually want to hear. Because good news deserves to be heard.

🔊 Listen in, get inspired, and spread the good! Good News York is a project of Growth Mode Content in Syracuse, NY.

Visit: GoodNewsYork.com for full video episodes, clips, links and more!

Violations of the ordinance may result in fines, 174 00:09:36.879 --> 00:09:42.840 jail time, or civil penalties. Pursuing Iowa's third congressional district seat, 175 00:09:43.000 --> 00:09:47.240 Democratic candidate Sarah Trone Garriots that improving access to healthcare 176 00:09:47.320 --> 00:09:51.480 is key to addressing Iowa's high cancer rates. Speaking Tuesday 177 00:09:51.519 --> 00:09:55.919 during an American Cancer Society Cancer Action Network Cancer coffee 178 00:09:56.000 --> 00:09:58.960 chat and West of Mooine, Drone Garriott said she supports 179 00:09:59.000 --> 00:10:03.919 expanding healthcare coverage through a national public option, restoring Medicaid funding, 180 00:10:04.159 --> 00:10:08.720 and increasing federal investment in cancer research and rural clinical trials. 181 00:10:08.919 --> 00:10:15.679 Trongarriott also signed acscim's Cancer Promise, pledging to support cancer research, funding, 182 00:10:16.039 --> 00:10:21.720 early detection and prevention programs, affordable healthcare, and expanding access 183 00:10:21.759 --> 00:10:26.039 to clinical trials. If elected. ACSCN will host a similar 184 00:10:26.080 --> 00:10:29.240 candidate event with incumbent Republican Zach Nan at noon on 185 00:10:29.360 --> 00:10:34.080 Friday at Beaverdale Books in Des Moines. More news online 186 00:10:34.120 --> 00:10:39.080 at Western Iowa Today dot com, imite bellings with KSOM 187 00:10:39.240 --> 00:10:41.720 and ks ninety five News

structure.rationale: Follows a clear multi-segment local news format with distinct stories and reporter attribution, though transitions are abrupt and pacing is uneven across segments of very different weight.

relevance.rationale: Primarily local community news (school athletics, county health agreement, festival ordinance) with one brief political segment about a congressional candidate's healthcare platform, making it loosely relevant to News/Politics but without a sharp timely political hook at its core.

clipability.rationale: The football coach's emotional quote about 'put the kids first, put your pride aside' is genuinely self-contained, but most segments require local context to land and the political segment is too brief to stand alone.

card.tagline: Small-town Iowa school fights to save its football program this fall
card.description: A rural Iowa athletic director pleads for players, proposes a broad inter-district sports merger, and a congressional candidate pushes a public healthcare option.
card.reason: Because this episode covers local Iowa governance and a congressional race

Despite advanced intelligence warnings, Ukrainian President 14 00:00:54.560 --> 00:00:59.000 Volodimir Zelenski had urgently cut short a diplomatic visit to Dublin, 15 00:00:59.280 --> 00:01:03.799 Ireland less than twenty four hours prior after receiving fresh 16 00:01:03.840 --> 00:01:08.200 intelligence reports that Moscow was staging a massive strategic strike. 17 00:01:08.719 --> 00:01:12.599 According to the Ukrainian Air Force, Russia's barrage included an 18 00:01:12.680 --> 00:01:18.120 unusually high concentration of advanced ballistic and hypersonic weapons, alongside 19 00:01:18.200 --> 00:01:22.599 jet powered Shahid drones designed to exhaust local defenses. While 20 00:01:22.640 --> 00:01:27.159 air defense systems successfully intercepted the majority of the incoming targets, 21 00:01:27.560 --> 00:01:31.719 a critical shortage of Patriot interceptor missiles allowed twenty six 22 00:01:31.799 --> 00:01:36.280 missiles and twenty attack drones to bypass the shield, impacting 23 00:01:36.319 --> 00:01:40.000 more than thirty three distinct locations across the city. The 24 00:01:40.040 --> 00:01:44.879 structural and humanitarian damage across Kiev's residential areas is extensive, 25 00:01:45.319 --> 00:01:48.959 prompting Kiev Mayor Vitali Klichko to declare a formal day 26 00:01:49.000 --> 00:01:52.560 of mourning. In the eastern Darnitsky District, on the left 27 00:01:52.599 --> 00:01:56.120 bank of the Denipro River, a direct missile strike caused 28 00:01:56.159 --> 00:01:59.680 six structural levels of a nine story residential building to 29 00:01:59.719 --> 00:02:05.079 com depletely collapse, destroying dozens of apartments and trapping families inside. 30 00:02:05.400 --> 00:02:08.919 A hotel in the city center hosting European expats. The Kremlin openly framed the massive bombardment as 36 00:02:31.360 --> 00:02:35.919 a direct retaliatory response to Ukraine's highly effective long range 37 00:02:36.000 --> 00:02:41.360 drone campaign against Russian domestic infrastructure. Russian Defense Ministry officials 38 00:02:41.400 --> 00:02:47.360 stated that the massive, coordinated strikes successfully neutralized military airfield components, 39 00:02:47.759 --> 00:02:53.879 homegrown drone assembly plants, and supporting energy facilities. With Russian President Vladimir 44 00:03:12.400 --> 00:03:16.280 Putin declaring that the mass aerial strikes must continue and 45 00:03:16.439 --> 00:03:21.280 President Zelensky fiercely vowing that Ukraine will definitely retaliate. The 46 00:03:21.319 --> 00:03:25.199 brutal exchange underscores a significant hardening of the conflict.

relevance.rationale: Directly covers a major breaking military and geopolitical event with specific casualty figures, weapon counts, diplomatic context, and escalation analysis — exactly what a News/Politics listener seeks.

clipability.rationale: Several self-contained, quotable passages — particularly the Patriot missile shortage detail and the Kremlin's retaliation framing — work as standalone previews without requiring much setup.

scored.description:
The Vault: The Epstein Files Unsealed is a deep-dive investigative podcast that pulls back the curtain on one of the most protected criminal networks in modern history. This series is built from the ground up on the actual paper trail—unsealed court records, depositions, exhibits, emails, and filings that were never meant to be read by the public. No pundit panels. No spin. Just the documents themselves, examined line by line, name by name, connection by connection—paired with precise, document-driven analysis that explains what the record truly shows.

Each episode opens the vault on newly unsealed or long-buried Epstein files and walks listeners through what they actually reveal about power, money, influence, and the systems that failed survivors at every turn. Alongside the filings themselves, informed commentary breaks down the legal strategy, the institutional behavior, the contradictions, and the implications hiding between the lines. From judges’ orders and sealed exhibits to sworn testimony and back-channel communications, the show connects the dots the media often won’t—or can’t. Patterns emerge. Timelines collapse. Excuses fall apart.

The Vault is a working archive in audio form, a living record of the Epstein case as told by the courts themselves—supplemented by rigorous analysis that provides context, challenges official narratives, and exposes where the record has been distorted, sanitized, or deliberately ignored. Every claim is grounded in filings. Every episode is anchored to the record. Listeners aren’t told what to think—they are shown what exists, what was said under oath, and what the commentary reveals about how those facts were buried, softened, or misrepresented.

If you want to understand how Jeffrey Epstein was protected, who circled him, how institutions closed ranks, and why accountability keeps slipping through the cracks, The Vault: The Epstein Files Unsealed is where the record finally speaks for itself—and where the commentary ensures the documents do what no press release ever will.

Stay informed, no matter how busy you are. Reporter Noa Levi of Israel Today: Ongoing War Report brings you the most crucial updates from Israel every hour. Sourced directly from leading news channels and top-rated RSS feeds, our team distills extensive news coverage from 'Telegram Channel Israel Today', 'Haaretz', 'JPost.com', 'The Times of Israel', 'Ynet' and more, into concise 2-3 minute summaries. From breaking headlines to political shifts and cultural happenings, get a comprehensive snapshot of the nation's heartbeat without the fluff. Subscribe now and never miss a beat from the heart of Israel.

Sources:
  • Telegram Channel Israel Today
  • Telegram Channel Stand With Us Breaking News

  • Front - Globes
  • Haaretz latest headlines
  • JPost.com - Homepage & Breaking

DISCLAIMER DISCLAIMER DISCLAIMER
This podcast's content is generated using advanced artificial intelligence techniques and aims to summarize information from various sources. It's important to note that while the AI strives for accuracy, it might not always provide an exact representation of the news. There's potential for unintended inaccuracies or omissions. The content provides an overview of discussions over the last hour and serves as a digest for those seeking a concise update. For comprehensive and direct information, please refer to primary news sources.

If Waller proceeds, it must follow the 81 00:05:04.399 --> 00:05:08.879 process laid out in its collective agreement, including furnishing financial data, 82 00:05:09.199 --> 00:05:12.720 the economic basis for cuts, the expected savings, and the 83 00:05:12.759 --> 00:05:17.480 considered alternatives. In Uplifting News, Gaza's humanitarian picture shows a 84 00:05:17.519 --> 00:05:21.240 reassuring surge in aid during the ceasefire period, with COG 85 00:05:21.360 --> 00:05:24.480 eight reporting that roughly one point seven eight million tons 86 00:05:24.480 --> 00:05:28.160 of food and other supplies entered Gaza between October twenty 87 00:05:28.199 --> 00:05:31.519 twenty five and June twenty twenty six, more than three 88 00:05:31.560 --> 00:05:35.399 times the World Food Program's stated requirement. The report notes 89 00:05:35.439 --> 00:05:38.720 a seventy two percent drop in food prices from September 90 00:05:38.720 --> 00:05:41.920 twenty twenty five to May twenty twenty six, and says 91 00:05:41.920 --> 00:05:45.879 water supply exceeded seventy thousand cubic meters per day, with 92 00:05:46.000 --> 00:05:49.800 price movements largely driven by internal market conditions and taxes. 93 00:05:50.199 --> 00:05:53.319 In the Netherlands, shop owners are preparing for tonight's World 94 00:05:53.319 --> 00:05:56.800 Cup match between Morocco and France, reflecting the global reach 95 00:05:56.839 --> 00:06:00.560 of the tournament as fans anticipate the game. structure.rationale: The episode follows a loose call-in format with host fielding questions and testimonials, but there is no discernible intro, segmentation, or deliberate pacing — it reads as an extended sales call.

relevance.rationale: This is a commercial health supplement infomercial promoting Taheebo tea with anecdotal medical claims — no connection to news or political content whatsoever.

clipability.rationale: Nearly every moment is either a product pitch or an anecdotal testimonial that requires the surrounding infomercial context to make sense as anything other than unverified health claims.

card.tagline: Taheebo tea host fields calls on kidneys, psoriasis, and celiac disease
card.description: Milan Vukovic takes live listener calls touting Taheebo tea as a remedy for kidney failure, COVID symptoms, psoriasis, and celiac disease via customer testimonials.
card.reason: Because this episode covers alternative health remedies and supplement promotion

scored.feedTitle: True Crime Podcast 2026 - Police Interrogations, 911 Calls and True Police Stories Podcast
scored.description:
True Crime Podcasts 2026 — 911 Calls, Police Interrogations & Crime Stories

This is your nonstop true crime feed — built for fans who binge for hours.

Hear authentic 911 calls, full-length police interrogations, unsolved mysteries, and shocking cases from across the country.

Episodes drop in shifts — mornings, afternoons, and nights — so there’s always fresh crime waiting, whether you’re commuting, on the job, or streaming through the night.

Perfect for shift workers, commuters, night owls, and binge listeners who want hours of true crime content — not once a week, but every single day.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/true-crime-podcast-2026-police-interrogations-911-calls-and-true-police-stories-podcast--5693470/support.

structure.rationale: The episode follows a loose interrogation transcript format with some narrative framing, but the raw Q&A structure makes it difficult to identify deliberate editorial organization or clear segments.

relevance.rationale: This is a true crime podcast focused on a criminal interrogation — pure crime/justice content with no meaningful connection to current news or political events.

clipability.rationale: A few emotionally charged moments — the blue face description, the 'I didn't have the strength to turn her over' admission — are viscerally striking but require significant case context to land as standalone clips.

card.tagline: A stepmother's story unravels as detectives reveal what the autopsy found
card.description: Detectives confront a stepmother with autopsy evidence in a child death case, exposing contradictions in her account of that night.
card.reason: Because you follow true crime cases built around real interrogation recordings

scored.description:
Melissa Unfiltered - a podcast where nothing is off limits. As a Mom with three adopted kids, I dive into foster care system failures, realities of Mom life, homeschooling, sexual abuse cover ups, domestic violence and any issues that get me fired up each week.

My perspective is unfiltered, fearless, and usually different from the mainstream narrative. 

Join me as I question and expose our cultures “accepted” truths, shine a light on the uncomfortable, and explore topics through a lens that’s without boundaries and uniquely mine.

latestEpisode.title: The Quiet War

transcript.scoringWindow: 00:15:20.399 --> 00:15:30.559 Kind of my rule of thumb now is like, if I wouldn't take this photo or I wouldn't take this and text it to every single person privately and say, here, look at this. 00:15:30.720 --> 00:15:33.519 Why would I post it up there for them to look at? 00:15:33.759 --> 00:15:34.879 Think about that. 00:15:35.120 --> 00:15:41.440 Like, if you wouldn't go to that person 20 years ago from high school and send them a text, be like, look at my new dog. 00:15:43.120 --> 00:15:44.320 What do you think? 00:15:44.720 --> 00:15:46.080 Why do they need to see it? 00:15:46.240 --> 00:15:51.600 Why do you owe them seeing that new dog or you having a baby? 00:15:51.840 --> 00:15:54.320 Like, you didn't talk to them anyway. 00:15:55.840 --> 00:15:57.360 And then, you know, politics. 00:15:57.519 --> 00:15:59.600 We're posting all about our political opinions. 00:15:59.679 --> 00:16:02.159 I did it too, super freaking guilty. 00:16:02.399 --> 00:16:04.799 Like during COVID, I am blowing up. 00:16:04.960 --> 00:16:09.360 I'm like, don't wear a mask, don't do this, and it turns out I was right. 00:16:09.519 --> 00:16:12.240 So, you know, but what did I need to be right for? 00:16:12.399 --> 00:16:14.320 Like, what was so important in that for me? 00:16:14.399 --> 00:16:16.639 It's really just like the self-reflection. 00:16:16.720 --> 00:16:19.840 And then when you post your politics, you have people who are like, Oh, I hate you now. 00:16:20.000 --> 00:16:21.200 We can't be friends. 00:16:21.440 --> 00:16:22.559 It's so ridiculous. 00:16:22.720 --> 00:16:24.080 Like, were you even friends? 00:16:24.320 --> 00:16:27.919 Or people at the gym, you know, we're judging, oh, that workout attire. 00:16:28.000 --> 00:16:29.039 I can't believe she's wearing that. 00:16:29.120 --> 00:16:30.399 Why does she have a crop top on? 00:16:30.480 --> 00:16:31.279 Why are you following? 00:16:31.440 --> 00:16:32.399 Why are you posting? 00:16:32.480 --> 00:16:33.200 Like, same. 00:16:33.360 --> 00:16:37.759 I'm I did it, I posted it, I felt good about myself. 00:16:37.919 --> 00:16:40.159 I liked, you know, putting it out there. 00:16:40.240 --> 00:16:44.240 I I'm not trying to judge other people again, self-reflection. 00:16:45.200 --> 00:16:51.759 But I just, I don't know, living living your lives and trying to have your brain process all this. 00:16:51.919 --> 00:16:55.519 I I really think that we were never designed for this. 00:16:55.759 --> 00:17:00.399 I really think our brains are not meant to process all of this information. 00:17:01.200 --> 00:17:03.919 I think we're supposed to be in real communities. 00:17:04.079 --> 00:17:05.440 We're supposed to help people. 00:17:05.599 --> 00:17:07.759 And here's another part that I thought about. 00:17:07.920 --> 00:17:19.119 Some people's accounts on here, like mine, um, right now, this podcast, are meant to help people bring awareness, um, help inspire change. 00:17:19.440 --> 00:17:21.039 But what if some of that help? 00:17:21.279 --> 00:17:29.599 Like, what if some of the accounts, instead of being like, let me show you how to cook this meal in under 10 minutes so you can be a better parent? 00:17:29.839 --> 00:17:36.720 Like, would you ever go up to the mom who you see struggling at swim practice with her kids and be like, hey, do you need some help? 00:17:36.960 --> 00:17:37.599 Can I help you? 00:17:37.680 --> 00:17:39.200 Like, where can I be a good friend to you? 00:17:39.359 --> 00:17:40.559 Where can I be a good neighbor? 00:17:40.720 --> 00:17:42.079 Like, I think we've lost that. 00:17:42.240 --> 00:17:45.599 I don't think anyone's actually helping anyone in person anymore. 00:17:45.920 --> 00:17:55.200 If you're willing to post this for strangers, why would you not be willing to help someone in real life within your little community? 00:17:55.359 --> 00:18:02.720 I think that there would be so much value in that rather than just putting yourself on the internet and say, this is to help someone. 00:18:03.039 --> 00:18:11.680 And in all of this, you know, knowing that we were never meant to see any of this, we were never meant to see what people are doing 24-7. 00:18:12.000 --> 00:18:13.440 That's not how our brains work. 00:18:13.599 --> 00:18:16.160 And we wonder why we're all exhausted. 00:18:16.480 --> 00:18:21.839 We wonder why we're anxious and we constantly compare ourselves to everyone. 00:18:22.000 --> 00:18:38.160 I mean, the last time that you said, oh, I want this outfit, or I want this thing in my kitchen, or I need this gadget, was it because you actually thought in your head all on your own that you needed it, or because you saw someone on social media or something on social media that said that you needed it? 00:18:38.400 --> 00:18:45.519 I mean, we are being programmed without even knowing this, and we're allowing it and we're giving access. 00:18:45.680 --> 00:18:47.839 I'm not talking about an ad that pops up. 00:18:48.079 --> 00:18:50.079 It's everything's an ad. 00:18:50.319 --> 00:18:56.799 Everything everyone's doing is essentially an ad because they saw it somewhere and they're trying to keep up and they wanted to do this. 00:18:57.039 --> 00:19:00.559 I think we're just not meant to consume this much information. 00:19:00.880 --> 00:19:10.799 And, you know, we should try living in a much simpler life than in this digital world because we are designed by God to be more simple than this. 00:19:11.279 --> 00:19:16.960 Um, you know, I think knowing all this, the real war isn't left versus right. 00:19:17.119 --> 00:19:20.720 It isn't rich versus poor, it isn't old versus young. 00:19:21.039 --> 00:19:24.559 It's, you know, the your data. 00:19:24.880 --> 00:19:25.839 They're collecting your data. 00:19:25.920 --> 00:19:26.880 I know we hear it all the time. 00:19:26.960 --> 00:19:34.400 They're collecting your data, and yes, they are, but it's not just your data that's valuable, it's your attention. 00:19:34.640 --> 00:19:36.799 This is a war for your attention. 00:19:36.960 --> 00:19:43.680 I mean, the biggest companies in the world are spending billions of dollars trying to keep you scrolling just one more minute. 00:19:43.839 --> 00:19:45.039 And they do, they succeed. 00:19:45.119 --> 00:19:46.240 I catch myself all the time. 00:19:46.319 --> 00:19:48.480 I'm like, oh my gosh, stop, don't scroll. 00:19:48.640 --> 00:19:53.119 Like another great thing I heard it was like, don't bring your phone in the bathroom and don't say, ew, that's gross. 00:19:53.200 --> 00:19:55.119 I know everyone brings their phone in the bathroom. 00:19:55.279 --> 00:19:57.440 Can you go to the bathroom without bringing your phone in? 00:19:57.599 --> 00:19:57.839 Really? 00:19:57.920 --> 00:19:58.400 Can you? 00:19:58.559 --> 00:19:59.440 It's a question. 00:19:59.680 --> 00:20:02.799 Like, they just want your attention. 00:20:03.039 --> 00:20:07.200 Every app that you download on your phone wants to send you notifications. 00:20:07.599 --> 00:20:10.400 You know, headlines have become dramatic and repetitive. 00:20:10.480 --> 00:20:16.319 You'll hear the same thing over and over and over, the same headline on every news station. 00:20:16.640 --> 00:20:21.440 The outrage is spreading like wildfire, like we talked about in last week's episode. 00:20:21.680 --> 00:20:24.000 Everyone's outraged over the silliest things. 00:20:24.160 --> 00:20:25.519 No one cares about good news. 00:20:25.680 --> 00:20:27.200 Everyone wants to be outraged. 00:20:27.279 --> 00:20:29.759 And these videos, like they keep autoplaying. 00:20:29.839 --> 00:20:30.240 Why? 00:20:30.480 --> 00:20:31.759 For your attention. 00:20:32.000 --> 00:20:35.279 Every little second of your attention has so much value. 00:20:35.440 --> 00:20:40.720 It is the most valuable thing you have, not your money, your attention, your time. 00:20:40.960 --> 00:20:42.640 And you're just giving it away. 00:20:42.799 --> 00:20:45.039 Free will, free will, just giving it away. 00:20:45.200 --> 00:20:48.000 Here's my attention, my human attention. 00:20:48.160 --> 00:20:56.160 You know, every company wants more of it, every politician wants more of it, every media outlet wants more of it, every influencer wants your attention. 00:20:56.400 --> 00:20:58.960 Advertisers, they're not really out to sell products. 00:20:59.039 --> 00:21:04.319 They want your attention first, and then your attention allows them to get those products sold. 00:21:04.559 --> 00:21:08.160 And wherever your attention goes, your behavior follows. 00:21:08.400 --> 00:21:11.440 And your behavior, money follows. 00:21:11.680 --> 00:21:13.759 Your attention is so valuable. 00:21:14.000 --> 00:21:15.440 They can't live without it. 00:21:15.680 --> 00:21:21.279 And, you know, I makes me question like what kind of person is the hardest person to manipulate? 00:21:21.440 --> 00:21:23.759 Because I feel like we're all just so easy to manipulate. 00:21:23.920 --> 00:21:44.400 I would say the hardest person to manipulate is someone who can sit quietly with their thoughts, someone who isn't desperate for approval, someone who isn't distracted, someone who reads something and then doesn't react, and who actually goes and does their own research on something, someone who's not emotionally hijacked by every headline. 00:21:44.559 --> 00:21:47.519 I know I have been, I've, you know, we've all been there. 00:21:47.759 --> 00:21:54.160 Um, someone who's not validated by strangers, like, why are we seeking validation from strangers on the internet? 00:21:54.480 --> 00:21:55.759 It's mind boggling. 00:21:55.920 --> 00:21:59.440 You know, those are the kind of people who are hard to manipulate. 00:21:59.519 --> 00:22:07.759 And And on the flip side, the easiest person to influence, someone who's distracted, someone who's reactive, someone who's scrolling all day long. 00:22:08.079 --> 00:22:11.599 I, everywhere I go, I see people just scrolling on their phone. 00:22:11.680 --> 00:22:18.400 You know, I was standing in line getting ice cream with my son the other day, and this grandma in front of us was just scrolling videos on her phone. 00:22:18.640 --> 00:22:25.359 The kid was running off somewhere playing, so it's not like she was neglecting the child, but like, can we sit anywhere without scrolling on our phone? 00:22:25.519 --> 00:22:26.960 They're getting our attention. 00:22:27.039 --> 00:22:28.960 You know, it makes you feel behind. 00:22:29.200 --> 00:22:30.960 It makes you feel, oh, I need approval. 00:22:31.039 --> 00:22:31.920 I'm behind. 00:22:32.079 --> 00:22:33.839 I can't live in this silence. 00:22:34.000 --> 00:22:37.440 You know, I need the constant simulation or I need the validation. 00:22:37.599 --> 00:22:43.200 Like anytime there's a problem, you're posting in a Reddit feed, like, is this, am I the problem? 00:22:43.440 --> 00:22:45.759 You're in mom's groups on Facebook. 00:22:45.839 --> 00:22:53.279 I always say Facebook groups are amazing and they are, they can be a great tool for information, but I feel like we're just giving our attention away, needing validation. 00:22:53.440 --> 00:22:54.640 Am I doing the right thing? 00:22:54.799 --> 00:22:59.359 Like, we are not capable of figuring things out on our own. 00:22:59.519 --> 00:23:05.279 And I think this is my conspiracy head, you know, like the way I think sometimes, usually not wrong. 00:23:05.359 --> 00:23:06.559 I'd love to be wrong. 00:23:06.720 --> 00:23:08.400 But I really think this was planned. 00:23:08.480 --> 00:23:13.039 And it feels to me now like stepping out of this like it was intentional. 00:23:13.200 --> 00:23:15.440 And the worst part is we fell for the trap. 00:23:15.680 --> 00:23:20.640 We all fell for the trap, you know, convenience it makes things easier. 00:23:20.799 --> 00:23:22.319 Here's everything online for you. 00:23:22.480 --> 00:23:26.880 You know, open your AI and ask it every question, it'll be faster. 00:23:27.119 --> 00:23:29.920 But we really need to start paying attention. 00:23:30.240 --> 00:23:35.519 And, you know, we're we we need to start protecting our minds. 00:23:36.400 --> 00:23:41.279 You think about like every night you go to bed and you lock your door, right? 00:23:41.440 --> 00:23:42.880 I would hope that you lock your door. 00:23:43.039 --> 00:23:46.160 We don't just leave it open for anyone. 00:23:46.240 --> 00:23:55.279 Um, you know, you put a password on every account that you have, you protect your kids, you don't just hire a random stranger to babysit your kids, I would hope. 00:23:55.440 --> 00:24:02.319 You know, you protect your identity, um, you

structure.rationale: The episode has a loose but followable throughline — from social media oversharing to the attention economy to manipulation resistance — though it meanders conversationally without tight segmentation or clear signposting.

relevance.rationale: This is a lifestyle/self-help episode about social media habits, attention economy, and digital wellness — not a news or politics episode, despite brief incidental mentions of COVID opinions and political posting.

clipability.rationale: Several genuinely self-contained, quotable moments exist — particularly the 'the real war isn't left vs. right, it's a war for your attention' passage and the 'hardest person to manipulate' list — that could stand alone as preview clips.

card.tagline: The real war isn't political — it's a battle for your attention
card.description: Melissa argues that social media platforms, advertisers, and media outlets have engineered a quiet war to capture your attention, and most of us are losing it without realizing.
card.reason: Because this episode challenges how you spend your most valuable resource

scored.feedTitle: All Caught Up: A Newsworthy podcast
scored.description:
Each week the All Caught Up team examine a newsworthy topic, talk to those in the know and consider what it means for young Australians and beyond. 


Hosts: Anoushka Israni @anoushkaisrani  and Ben Tresno-Turner @bentresnoturner 

Sound Editor and Engineer: Michelle Zeng  @michelle.zeng.creative

Executive producer: Alexandra Spring  @newsworthy_aus

latestEpisode.title: AI-assisted dating is here. Will it change Gen Z’s love lives?

AI. 00:06:15.360 --> 00:06:17.120 Yeah, other sides of the coin, in my opinion. 00:06:17.360 --> 00:06:18.639 But maybe that's not the case. 00:06:18.800 --> 00:06:23.040 I came across an app made by a clinical psychologist that utilizes AI. 00:06:23.439 --> 00:06:24.800 Hi, I'm Rachel Harker. 00:06:24.959 --> 00:06:28.319 I'm a clinical psychologist and founder of Tribal Group. 00:06:28.399 --> 00:06:30.160 So we have two apps. 00:06:30.319 --> 00:06:36.480 One is Tribal, our dating platform, and the other one is Connect by Tribal, which is our friendship and community platform. 00:06:36.800 --> 00:06:44.240 To give you a little bit more background context, Tribal, the app, it uses psychology journals to create an algorithm to then match people up. 00:06:44.639 --> 00:06:51.600 So I think AI is such a good tool to assist, but it definitely shouldn't be replacing humans. 00:06:52.000 --> 00:06:52.160 Okay. 00:06:52.399 --> 00:06:54.480 So almost like an assistant, if you will. 00:06:54.639 --> 00:07:01.279 So we have put in the psychology, we've put in the evidence, and we use AI to help us with that and refine it. 00:07:01.439 --> 00:07:04.160 So I mentioned before the compatibility quiz. 00:07:04.240 --> 00:07:07.279 So that has scientific journals backing it up. 00:07:07.360 --> 00:07:22.240 We have the scoring system that I developed, and we've input that into the algorithm, but then we use AI to refine that over time and almost provide kind of more informative recommendations based on the psychology that was input. 00:07:22.319 --> 00:07:23.759 So they work hand in hand. 00:07:24.319 --> 00:07:24.560 Right. 00:07:24.639 --> 00:07:31.040 And I downloaded the app just for scientific purposes here, and they gave me a compatibility score ranked on a bunch of different questions. 00:07:31.120 --> 00:07:37.759 So a 44 question quiz, some were on family value, some were how career-centric you are or how creative you are. 00:07:38.000 --> 00:07:42.399 But interestingly, in my opinion, you can't see who you match with for 72 hours. 00:07:42.480 --> 00:07:45.680 You can only see their sort of AI compatibility score. 00:07:45.920 --> 00:07:47.199 That's very interesting. 00:07:47.360 --> 00:07:55.519 I also think with the compatibility score, one thing that might be an issue, the algorithm doesn't really account for the whole opposite attract idea, does it? 00:07:55.600 --> 00:07:58.240 It just sort of pigeonholes you into one concept. 00:07:58.560 --> 00:08:00.800 That's something that we are refining over time. 00:08:01.279 --> 00:08:04.959 The more data that you put into an AI model, the more it will learn. 00:08:05.199 --> 00:08:08.480 So we have had almost 12,000 downloads since we launched. 00:08:08.560 --> 00:08:14.000 So we now have 12,000 data points of people's quizzes, and you can see how they then match. 00:08:14.079 --> 00:08:20.959 And that's where AI actually is an amazing tool because we might say, well, these are the answers to the quiz, and this is what we think is a good recommendation. 00:08:21.120 --> 00:08:33.279 But then as AI learns from those 12,000 people, they'll be like, actually, you know, it's okay to, it might be really good to have the same family values, but a compatibil compatible couple might have quite different career goals. 00:08:33.360 --> 00:08:40.159 One might be okay to maybe take, you know, a step back and support their partner going forward, depending on, you know, family dynamic. 00:08:40.399 --> 00:08:43.679 So you don't always have to have the exactly same values. 00:08:43.840 --> 00:08:45.279 They just need to be aligned. 00:08:45.600 --> 00:08:50.080 Okay, so they're trying to see AI as more of an assistant, not like the star of the show. 00:08:50.159 --> 00:08:51.759 It's not taking any of the credit. 00:08:51.919 --> 00:08:56.320 And we do also agree that there are some positive aspects of using AI in dating. 00:08:56.480 --> 00:08:58.480 For example, how about like religion matching? 00:08:58.639 --> 00:09:04.480 If you're maybe Christian and you only want to date other Christians, this is a good way to sort of filter out those people who just don't suit your type. 00:09:04.720 --> 00:09:08.399 Or even for age range, you've got AI age verification for safety. 00:09:08.559 --> 00:09:10.399 Date within your age range. 00:09:10.559 --> 00:09:11.840 No one too young, no one too old. 00:09:12.000 --> 00:09:12.240 I agree. 00:09:12.320 --> 00:09:16.799 And how about like another form of AI safety is like face scanning, so you can verify your profile. 00:09:16.879 --> 00:09:20.000 So maybe that's going to reduce the amount of catfishes available online. 00:09:20.320 --> 00:09:20.639 Definitely. 00:09:20.720 --> 00:09:23.200 And then you've also got stating your goals, you know? 00:09:23.360 --> 00:09:26.320 You know when somebody wants a long-term or just a short-term hookup. 00:09:26.720 --> 00:09:30.320 So maybe it's not that bad after all, integrating dating with AI. 00:09:33.759 --> 00:09:38.480 Look, I'm all for AI making things a bit more efficient and a little bit easier for you. 00:09:38.720 --> 00:09:42.879 But when AI and dating get mixed up, I'm really not sure how I feel about it. 00:09:43.120 --> 00:09:44.399 But that's just my opinion. 00:09:44.559 --> 00:09:51.679 So we asked around the UNSW campus to see how other students felt about AI's integration into dating apps and if it worked out for them. 00:09:52.080 --> 00:09:53.200 My name is Eva. 00:09:53.360 --> 00:09:55.360 I'm a student at UNSW. 00:09:55.759 --> 00:10:00.240 There are a couple people that I matched with based on the AI feature. 00:10:00.559 --> 00:10:07.279 And I'm gonna be so honest, I did not see any sort of compatibility between me and them. 00:10:07.440 --> 00:10:18.240 Like, for example, um on a very shallow level, I'm very into sports, but for some reason I was getting matched with people that are more musical and so on. 00:10:18.559 --> 00:10:25.840 And in fact, I thought I would give it a good crack and go on a date based on this AI compatibility level, and I did. 00:10:26.399 --> 00:10:37.200 And what I've learned is that there's just such a huge difference, and what I kind of gathered is that AI doesn't account for all the niche parts of your personality. 00:10:37.679 --> 00:11:07.919 For example, I I'm a bit more of a serious person, and I like someone that's a bit more like you know, bubbly and easygoing, but you can't really catch that on the apps, and AI definitely can't catch that because it does not understand the niche parts of personality, and yeah, so that actually ultimately made me stop going on hinge altogether because I did some research, and AI is really an underlying tone of hinge these days. 00:11:08.159 --> 00:11:08.879 Definitely, yeah. 00:11:09.120 --> 00:11:14.000 Um, and so yeah, now I don't

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear throughline from scam risks to app features to an expert interview to student vox pops, with identifiable segments and a loose but functional narrative arc.

relevance.rationale: This episode is a lifestyle/technology feature about AI in dating apps, with no substantive connection to news events, legislation, policy, or political developments.

clipability.rationale: The student vox pop from Eva and the clinical psychologist's explanation of AI-assisted matching both work as reasonably self-contained moments with minimal setup required.

card.tagline: Swiping on AI: Is your dating app matchmaker actually a machine?
card.description: Two UNSW students investigate how AI is reshaping Gen Z dating — from romance scams to psychology-backed compatibility algorithms — with a clinical psychologist and real user experiences.
card.reason: Because this episode explores how AI is quietly changing how young people find love

scored.description: Plugged In Australia is your essential podcast for the latest electric vehicle news tailored to Aussie drivers. We break down fresh updates on sales trends, policy changes like road-user charges and tax exemptions, and infrastructure developments—from charging networks in Sydney to regional rollouts. Get quick insights on new models hitting the market, like affordable BYD imports and Tesla’s latest, plus analysis on how global shifts affect Oz. Whether you’re tracking EV adoption rates or debunking myths, tune in weekly for concise, no-fluff coverage to keep you informed on the road to a greener future. Subscribe now and plug into the conversation

latestEpisode.title: Episode 68; Quick Charge — Cheaper Tavascan, Electric X5 and Volvo’s EX90 Price Cut
