transcript.scoringWindow: WEBVTT 1 00:00:05.719 --> 00:00:08.240 As the day closes, we look at the critical push 2 00:00:08.240 --> 00:00:12.000 for substance overform and the ongoing impeachment proceedings. We examine 3 00:00:12.000 --> 00:00:14.720 whether procedural technicalities are stalling a trial meant to serve 4 00:00:14.759 --> 00:00:18.440 the public's demand for accountability. Good evening, Philippines. I am Brian, 5 00:00:18.480 --> 00:00:20.600 and as the sun sets on a busy legislative day, 6 00:00:20.839 --> 00:00:23.160 let us break down what moved the needle since this morning. 7 00:00:23.559 --> 00:00:25.640 It is time to reflect on the stories that shaped 8 00:00:25.640 --> 00:00:29.719 our national conversation today. The most significant development of the 9 00:00:29.800 --> 00:00:32.600 day involves the growing outcry regarding the direction of the 10 00:00:32.600 --> 00:00:35.960 impeachment trial currently underway in the Senate. As the proceedings 11 00:00:35.960 --> 00:00:38.600 have progressed throughout the week, a sharp divide has emerged 12 00:00:38.640 --> 00:00:41.039 between those advocating for strict adherents to the rules of 13 00:00:41.079 --> 00:00:43.240 court and those demanding that the core purpose of the 14 00:00:43.240 --> 00:00:46.359 impeachment process be upheld. Critics are now calling for a 15 00:00:46.399 --> 00:00:50.359 major shift in focus from technical rules to actual political substance. 16 00:00:51.399 --> 00:00:54.399 The prevailing argument is that an impeachment trial is not 17 00:00:54.560 --> 00:00:58.000 merely a legal exercise played out by officials. It is 18 00:00:58.079 --> 00:01:01.039 fundamentally a demand for account of ability from the people. 19 00:01:01.520 --> 00:01:04.879 There is a growing concern among observers that rigid procedures 20 00:01:04.920 --> 00:01:08.480 and microscopic procedural disputes may be used as a shield 21 00:01:08.519 --> 00:01:13.400 to stall the inquiry into grave allegations of misconduct. Proponents 22 00:01:13.439 --> 00:01:15.920 of this shift argue that if the Senate allows form 23 00:01:16.000 --> 00:01:20.560 to swallow substance, they risk undermining the very democratic mechanism 24 00:01:20.599 --> 00:01:24.599 designed to hold high ranking officials accountable. The focus must 25 00:01:24.640 --> 00:01:27.799 remain on whether the evidence presented meets the threshold of 26 00:01:27.799 --> 00:01:31.280 the charges, rather than becoming distracted by how those pieces 27 00:01:31.319 --> 00:01:35.040 of evidence are presented in a court room setting. Legal 28 00:01:35.079 --> 00:01:37.640 experts noted today that while the rules of court provide 29 00:01:37.640 --> 00:01:40.079 the structure for any trial, the ultimate goal of an 30 00:01:40.079 --> 00:01:43.920 impeachment is to serve the public interest. By prioritizing technicalities 31 00:01:43.920 --> 00:01:46.280 over the underlying allegations, there is a risk that the 32 00:01:46.319 --> 00:01:49.799 process becomes stalled and eventually loses its momentum. The Senate 33 00:01:49.840 --> 00:01:53.000 faces the daunting task of balancing these two needs, ensuring 34 00:01:53.040 --> 00:01:55.599 a fair, orderly trial while ensuring that the inquiry does 35 00:01:55.640 --> 00:01:59.760 not become a performative exercise in procedural perfection. This ten 36 00:01:59.799 --> 00:02:02.519 Shih highlights the importance of the role of the senators 37 00:02:02.560 --> 00:02:05.120 as judges, who must balance their legal duties with their 38 00:02:05.239 --> 00:02:08.639 roles as representatives of the Filipino people. As we move 39 00:02:08.680 --> 00:02:11.800 forward into the coming days, the scrutiny on these proceedings 40 00:02:11.800 --> 00:02:15.360 will only intensify. The public expects a trial that seeks 41 00:02:15.439 --> 00:02:18.039 the truth of the allegations rather than one that seeks 42 00:02:18.080 --> 00:02:22.199 to avoid the questions through a maze of rules. Ultimately, 43 00:02:22.319 --> 00:02:24.680 the success of this process will be measured by whether 44 00:02:24.719 --> 00:02:28.159 it fulfills its role as democracy's way of demanding and accounting. 45 00:02:28.719 --> 00:02:31.840 In other news, today, the Supreme Court has officially issued 46 00:02:31.840 --> 00:02:35.479 a ruling regarding the No Contact apprehension policy. The court 47 00:02:35.479 --> 00:02:39.520 has junked various petitions against the program, effectively green lighting 48 00:02:39.599 --> 00:02:43.120 the use of advanced technology for remote traffic enforcement across 49 00:02:43.120 --> 00:02:47.400 the country. This decision provides local authorities with the legal 50 00:02:47.439 --> 00:02:51.120 backing to utilize cameras and sensors to penalize violators without 51 00:02:51.120 --> 00:02:55.000 direct physical intervention. In the agricultural sector, the Department of 52 00:02:55.039 --> 00:02:58.719 Agriculture is actively seeking an additional one billion pesos in funding. 53 00:02:59.240 --> 00:03:02.159 These funds are intended to sustain fuel subsidies for local 54 00:03:02.199 --> 00:03:05.520 farmers and fisherfolk whose current allocations have been depleted due 55 00:03:05.560 --> 00:03:09.560 to high costs. This move aims to stabilize the livelihoods 56 00:03:09.560 --> 00:03:12.719 of those at the forefront of our food security. Additionally, 57 00:03:12.919 --> 00:03:15.319 fact checkers have worked today to debunk a viral video 58 00:03:15.439 --> 00:03:18.680 circulating on social media. The footage, which appeared to show 59 00:03:18.680 --> 00:03:22.400 a clash between protesters and police at Lewisang Bonifacio, was 60 00:03:22.439 --> 00:03:25.560 proven to be an AI generated fabrication. There were no 61 00:03:25.680 --> 00:03:30.599 reported confrontations during the Iglesia and nin Ni Cristo rally. Finally, 62 00:03:30.639 --> 00:03:33.759 we are tracking developments regarding your household costs as certain 63 00:03:33.800 --> 00:03:36.800 tax exemptions for cooking fuels are scheduled to expire soon. 64 00:03:37.319 --> 00:03:40.319 The Supreme Court's decision to uphold the no contact apprehension 65 00:03:40.360 --> 00:03:44.120 policy is a significant development for urban management and public safety. 66 00:03:44.520 --> 00:03:47.520 By clearing the legal path for technology based enforcement, the 67 00:03:47.560 --> 00:03:50.840 court allows for a more consistent and less confrontational method 68 00:03:50.879 --> 00:03:54.879 of managing traffic. This move is expected to modernize our 69 00:03:54.919 --> 00:03:57.719 infrastructure by reducing the need for manual checkpoints in high 70 00:03:57.759 --> 00:04:01.919 traffic areas. Furthermore, at streamline the process of identifying violators, 71 00:04:02.120 --> 00:04:05.639 potentially leading to higher compliance rates for many urban centers 72 00:04:05.680 --> 00:04:09.439 facing growing congestion. This technological shift represents a crucial step 73 00:04:09.479 --> 00:04:13.000 towards safer and more orderly public spaces. However, not all 74 00:04:13.000 --> 00:04:17.199 news today is favorable for the average consumer's pocketbook. As 75 00:04:17.199 --> 00:04:19.959 the period of tax breaks on kerosene and liquid petroleum 76 00:04:19.959 --> 00:04:22.720 gas comes to an end, many households may face higher 77 00:04:22.720 --> 00:04:25.759 costs for their essential cooking and heating needs. The expiration 78 00:04:25.839 --> 00:04:28.480 of these excise tax suspensions means that families who rely 79 00:04:28.560 --> 00:04:30.439 on these fuels will see a direct impact on their 80 00:04:30.480 --> 00:04:35.399 daily expenses. Specifically, calculations suggest that previous exemptions save consumers 81 00:04:35.439 --> 00:04:38.560 over five pesos per liter for kerosene and nearly thirty 82 00:04:38.639 --> 00:04:42.800 seven pesos for a tank of gas. As these savings vanish, 83 00:04:42.959 --> 00:04:46.040 the immediate impact on household budgets will be felt by 84 00:04:46.079 --> 00:04:49.319 many across various regions. We must look at the bigger 85 00:04:49.360 --> 00:04:53.040 picture regarding how we consume information in this digital age. 86 00:04:53.319 --> 00:04:57.920 Today's events highlight an increasingly complex battle against misinformation that 87 00:04:58.040 --> 00:05:02.879 impacts both our legal systems our perception of reality. From 88 00:05:02.879 --> 00:05:06.959 the emergence of AI generated videos showing non existent conflicts 89 00:05:06.959 --> 00:05:10.240 to the viral myths concerning the legality of currency, the 90 00:05:10.279 --> 00:05:14.240 public is being bombarded by fake news on a daily basis. 91 00:05:14.879 --> 00:05:18.439 The fact check regarding the Lewisang Bonifacio rally shows how 92 00:05:18.519 --> 00:05:22.480 easily sophisticated technology can be used to manufacture narratives that 93 00:05:22.519 --> 00:05:27.920 do not reflect reality. Similarly, the clarification provided by authorities 94 00:05:27.959 --> 00:05:31.600 regarding photocopied PESO bills serves as a reminder that certain 95 00:05:31.639 --> 00:05:35.800 hacks circulating online are actually illegal acts. These incidents underscore 96 00:05:35.800 --> 00:05:38.639 a desperate need for critical thinking and verification before any 97 00:05:38.720 --> 00:05:42.480 content is shared on social media platforms. As technology makes 98 00:05:42.480 --> 00:05:45.519 it easier to create convincing fakes, we must become more 99 00:05:45.519 --> 00:05:50.279 discerning consumers of information. We see the intersection of law, technology, 100 00:05:50.279 --> 00:05:53.040 and public perception in every story today. When a video 101 00:05:53.040 --> 00:05:55.720 can be manufactured or rule can be twisted by influencers, 102 00:05:55.959 --> 00:05:58.879 the truth becomes harder to isolate. These incidents underscore the 103 00:05:58.959 --> 00:06:02.439 necessity for educational initiatives that promote media literacy among our citizens. 104 00:06:02.839 --> 00:06:05.560 As we navigate these challenges, how can we as citizens 105 00:06:05.560 --> 00:06:08.000 better shield ourselves from digital deception? In an era where 106 00:06:08.000 --> 00:06:11.920 the line between truth and fabrication is increasingly blurred, the 107 00:06:12.000 --> 00:06:15.040 only thing that is constant is change. As we move 108 00:06:15.120 --> 00:06:18.319 through this complex political landscape, let us remember to stay 109 00:06:18.360 --> 00:06:22.040 focused on facts and remain resilient. Your awareness is your 110 00:06:22.079 --> 00:06:25.680 strongest tool against uncertainty that is our wrap for tonight. 111 00:06:25.879 --> 00:06:28.040 I am Brian. Thank you for tuning in to your 112 00:06:28.040 --> 00:06:32.240 evening briefing from Montila. Have a good night. Thank you 113 00:06:32.279 --> 00:06:35.879 for listening to Philippine Politics now. Please note that the 114 00:06:35.920 --> 00:06:39.720 commentary and opinions shared on this show belong exclusively to 115 00:06:39.759 --> 00:06:43.120 the individuals speaking and do not represent the standpoint of 116 00:06:43.160 --> 00:06:46.519 the entire network, its parent company, or its sponsors.

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear evening-briefing format with a defined lead story (impeachment), secondary news items, and a thematic close on misinformation, though transitions between segments are somewhat abrupt and the misinformation thread feels loosely stitched together.

relevance.rationale: The episode is squarely focused on timely Philippine political developments — an active Senate impeachment trial, a same-day Supreme Court ruling, and a live agricultural funding dispute — making it highly relevant to News/Politics listeners tracking Philippine affairs.

clipability.rationale: The impeachment framing — 'if the Senate allows form to swallow substance, they risk undermining the very democratic mechanism' — is a genuinely standalone, quotable moment, but most of the episode's value is cumulative rather than concentrated in discrete hooks.

card.tagline: Is the Philippine Senate letting procedure kill accountability in real time?
card.description: Brian's July 9 evening briefing breaks down the impeachment trial's substance-vs-procedure crisis, a Supreme Court traffic ruling, and an AI-fabricated protest video debunked.
card.reason: Because the Philippine Senate impeachment trial reached a critical procedural flashpoint today

I'm sure there are 0:15:48.760 --> 0:15:52.680 some good print journalists out there, but I'll be completely 0:15:52.720 --> 0:15:55.520 honest with you, that's just not how I consume my news. 0:15:56.080 --> 0:15:59.520 I read some, I flipped through things on social media, 0:15:59.560 --> 0:16:02.520 but I've always been an audio listener. I've always been 0:16:02.560 --> 0:16:04.840 more of a visual and an audio learner, as I'm 0:16:04.840 --> 0:16:06.800 sure you are. That's why you get a lot of 0:16:06.800 --> 0:16:10.760 your news from the radio, and so I think that 0:16:11.960 --> 0:16:14.160 being able to put a picture to the story in 0:16:14.200 --> 0:16:18.320 an easily digestible way is what our local television news 0:16:18.360 --> 0:16:21.200 people do a great job. Anyan, in radio, there's there's 0:16:21.240 --> 0:16:24.040 a couple of good radio reporters out there, Nick Gerda 0:16:24.160 --> 0:16:28.160 from KPCC. But I can count the amount of great 0:16:28.640 --> 0:16:31.480 radio journalists out there, you know, I don't. I'm not 0:16:31.600 --> 0:16:33.920 technically a journalist. I do a news program here, but 0:16:33.920 -->

structure.rationale: The episode moves through distinct segments (diaper contract corruption, fire rebuild failures, Newsom's broken promise) with a loose but identifiable throughline of California government accountability, though transitions are conversational rather than deliberate.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers specific California political accountability issues — a suspicious nonprofit diaper contract tied to Jennifer Newsom, the 18-month LA fire rebuild failure, and Gavin Newsom's unspent $2B fire relief pledge — making it substantively on-topic for news/politics listeners, though the Fryman Canyon hiking fee angle from the title is absent in this excerpt.

clipability.rationale: Several standalone-worthy moments exist — particularly the diaper contract math breakdown and the stat that 70% of Newsom's fire relief money was never spent — but most require at least minimal setup to land with full impact.

card.tagline: Newsom's $2B fire promise: 70% unspent, victims still waiting 18 months later
card.description: A California radio host digs into a suspicious nonprofit diaper contract tied to Jennifer Newsom and exposes how LA fire victims are still homeless while promised relief money vanished back into the state budget.
card.reason: Because this episode exposes how California's fire victims are still being failed

scored.description:
Welcome to the Mix 92.3 R&B Playback Podcast! 🎙️

Detroit's favorite R&B station just got even better—on demand.

Listen in for the best moments from the Steve Harvey Morning Show and Keith Sweat’s Sweat Hotel, plus exclusive celebrity interviews hosted by Mix 92.3’s own Cheron and Bushman bringing you the inside on music, culture, and real talk.

From Keith’s wild confessions, to Shirley's juicy Strawberry Letter, and Nephew Tommy's legendary prank calls, we’ve got your mornings and nights covered. 🎧

Missed it live? Want to play it back? Hit follow and take the Mix 92.3 experience with you, wherever you go.

latestEpisode.title: Shirley's Strawberry Letter: Can we make this thing work or not ?

structure.rationale: The segment follows a clear read-then-react format with a mid-break recap, giving it a recognizable two-part structure, though pacing is loose and conversational throughout.

relevance.rationale: This is a relationship advice/entertainment radio segment with zero connection to news or political events.

clipability.rationale: The milk-and-infidelity riff and the 'fake a stroke' bit are genuinely funny standalone moments, but most of the humor depends on following the letter's accumulated chaos.

structure.rationale: The episode shifts abruptly from a reasonably structured geopolitical analysis of Turkey to an unrelated conspiracy theory segment about graphene oxide and brain-scanning chips, with no clear throughline connecting the two.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers substantive geopolitical topics — Turkey-NATO dynamics, Erdogan's F-35 negotiations, Russia-Turkey tensions, and Middle East policy — but is undercut by a significant segment promoting COVID vaccine nanotech conspiracy theories, diluting its news/politics credibility.

clipability.rationale: The Erdogan F-35 framing ('when they deliver the thirty-fives, everyone will realize America keeps its word — that sounds like a threat') offers a usable standalone moment, but most segments require significant setup or veer into fringe territory that limits standalone utility.

card.tagline: Is Erdogan playing NATO, Israel, and Russia all at once?
card.description: A deep dive into Turkey's geopolitical balancing act at the NATO summit, Erdogan's F-35 leverage over Trump, and long-term succession planning by Western powers.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down Turkey's pivotal role in the current NATO summit standoff

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear broadcast news format with a headline tease, a brief news update segment, and three distinct story blocks with a clean close, though pacing between segments is slightly abrupt.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers three timely, specific political and legal stories — Haitian TPS deportation deadlines, a Supreme Court ruling, and a federal judge blocking a DOJ subpoena targeting Fulton County election workers — all with direct policy and civil rights implications.

clipability.rationale: The Haitian TPS work permit expiration segment and the federal judge blocking the DOJ subpoena both contain self-contained, quotable moments with enough context to stand alone as previews.

scored.description:
Take some zany and serious journeys with The Pipeman aka Dean K. Piper, CST on The Adventures of Pipeman also known as Pipeman Radio syndicated globally “Where Who Knows And Anything Goes”.

Listen to & Watch a show dedicated to motivation, business, empowerment, inspiration, music, comedy, celebrities, shock jock radio, various topics, and entertainment. The Adventures of Pipeman is hosted by Dean K. Piper, CST aka “The Pipeman” who has been said to be hybrid of Tony Robbins, Batman, and Howard Stern. The Adventures of Pipeman has received many awards, media features, and has been ranked for multiple categories as one of the Top 6 Live Radio Shows & Podcasts in the world.

Pipeman Radio also consists of multiple podcasts showing the many sides of Pipeman. These include The Adventures of Pipeman, Pipeman in the Pit, and Positively Pipeman and more. You can find all of the Pipeman Podcasts anywhere you listen to podcasts. With thousands of episodes that focus on Intertainment which combines information and entertainment there is something for everyone including over 5000 interviews with celebrities, music artists/bands, authors, speakers, coaches, entrepreneurs, and all kinds of professionals.

Then there is The Pipeman Radio Tour where Pipeman travels the country and world doing press coverage for Major Business Events, Conferences, Conventions, Music Festivals, Concerts, Award Shows, and Red Carpets. One of the top publicists in music has named Pipeman the “King of All Festivals.” So join the Pipeman as he brings “The Pipeman Radio Tour” to life right before your ears and eyes.

The Adventures of Pipeman Podcasts are heard on The Adventures of Pipeman Site, Pipeman Radio, Talk 4 Media, Talk 4 Podcasting, iHeartRadio, Pandora, Amazon Music, Audible, Spotify, Apple Podcast, Google Podcasts and over 100 other podcast outlets where you listen to Podcasts. The following are the different podcasts to check out and subscribe to:

• The Adventures of Pipeman
• Pipeman Radio
• Pipeman in the Pit
• Positively Pipeman

Follow @pipemanradio on all social media outlets
Visit Pipeman Radio on the Web at linktr.ee/pipemanradio , theadventuresofpipeman.com, pipemanradio.com, talk4media.com, w4cy.com, talk4tv.com, talk4podcasting.com

Download The Pipeman Radio APP

Phone/Text Contact – 561-506-4031

Email Contact – dean@talk4media.com  

The Adventures of Pipeman is broadcast live Wednesdays at 1PM ET and Music & Positive Interviews daily at 8AM ET on W4CY Radio (www.w4cy.com) and replays on K4HD Radio (www.k4hd.com  – Hollywood Talk Radio part of Talk 4 Radio (www.talk4radio.com) on the Talk 4 Media Network (www.talk4media.com). The Adventures of Pipeman TV Show is viewed on Talk 4 TV (www.talk4tv.com).

The Adventures of Pipeman Podcast is also available on www.theadventuresofpipeman.com and www.pipemanradio.com,  Talk 4 Media (www.talk4media.com), Talk 4 Podcasting (www.talk4podcasting.com), iHeartRadio, Amazon Music, Pandora, Spotify, Audible, and over 100 other podcast outlets.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-adventures-of-pipeman--941822/support.

transcript.scoringWindow: Bitcoin is not freedom, the delusion of digital escape by hammun solemony. Within digital libertarian circles, there is a persistent, almost religious belief that decentralized cryptocurrencies will organically starve the state of its power by enabling parallel, untaxable counter economies. This techno optimistic prophecy assumes that because the state cannot break the underlying mathematics of cryptography, it is effectively disarmed. Yet this worldview conflates economic friction with true sovereignty. Treating code as an exit strategy ignores the enduring reality that human beings reside in physical space governed by Westphalian models of territorial jurisdiction. In practice, the decentralized revolution has not liberated the masses. It has been systematically assimilated. For the overwhelming majority of its user base, Bitcoin has been corralled into a highly regulated, rigorously surveiled speculative asset. It sits not in peer to peer networks of dissident commerce, but within heavily compliant custodial architectures circumscribed by compulsory biometric identity verification and cleanly integrated into Wall Street exchange traded products. The ultimate victory of the fiat system is that the cryptocurrency community now measures the success of their revolutionary sound money entirely in the paper currency of the state they set out to destroy. The cipherpunk's architectural triumph has been tragically inverted. An immutable, transparent ledger has become a panoptic tool for state surveillance. The moment a wallet address is linked to a physical identity at a regulated gateway, the blockchain ceases to be a sanctuary of privacy and becomes an indelible public blueprint of an indival's entire economic life. This capture is not a failure of the protocol's engineering, but a reminder that sovereign authority is ultimately physical, not mathematical. The theoretical blind spots of digital agorism. This heavy reliance on technological escapism inherits the historical blind spots of both classical anarcho capitalism and late ninety s says techno utopianism. The strategy of building parallel counter economies to passively erode coercive authorities is a modern iteration of agorism, first formulated by Samuel Edward Conkin the third decades later, James Dale Davidson and Lord William Rhyes magg codified a similarly flawed prophecy in their nineteen ninety seven manifesto, The Sovereign Individual. They argued that the advent of microprocessing and strong encryption would inevitable render the nation state obsolete, empowering a cognitive elite to transcend borders through digital capital flight. Their central mechanism, sovereignty by exit, held that encrypted capital, being jurisdictionally stateless and mathematically unconfiscatable, nullifies the state's primary coercive lever. A private key memorized crosses no border, answers to no seizure order, yields to no tax authority. This is technically correct and strategically irrelevant. Davidson and Rhese Mogg confused capital mobility with bodily autonomy. The state has no need to crack a private key, it cracks the person holding it. Coercion flows through flesh, imprisonment, family targeting, physical immobility, or simply through severing the infrastructure that makes broadcasting a transaction possible. An encrypted key grants its holder perfect cryptographic sovereignty and zero political agency. The sovereign individual remained perfectly encrypted and perfectly powerless. Weber diagnosed this category error decades before Davidson and Rhyese magg formalized it. In his nineteen nineteen lecture Politics as a Vocation. He established that the state exists precisely because it successfully maintains a geographic monopoly on the legitimate application of physical force, a monopoly that is not economic but kinetic. The structural friction Bitcoin introduces operates entirely in the economic register. It cannot reach the coercive register, where sovereignty actually resides. A monopoly on violence is not negotiated away in the marketplace of ideas, nor subverted simply because capital is reallocated into an unhackable database. As Murray Rothbard famously articulated in his critique of Conkin, parallel black markets fundamentally ignore the brutal, kinetic reality of state power. Black marketeers might well benefit themselves in the micro sense, but they have no relevance to the macro struggle for liberty and against the state empirical state craft, weaponization, and infrastructural capture. The prevailing dogma assumes decentralized ledgers are inherently corrosive to authoritarian governance, but modern empirical state craft has thoroughly falsified this assumption. Far from collapsing under the weight of peer to peer cryptography, autocracies are fundamentally morally agnostic toward technology, seamlessly integrating it to fortify their own longevity. The recent crises of fractured states vividly demonstrate that adversarial regimes have no need to wage an expensive computational war against the SAHA two hundred and fifty six outs algorithm or mount a prohibitively costly fifty one percent attack when they can exert raw dominance over the energy grids, data centers, fiber optic cables, and localized populations. The Islamic Republic of Iran, for instance, was not destabilized by digital currencies. Instead, the state apparatus systematically commandeered its domestic energy grid to industrialize bitcoin mining, capturing an estimated four point five percent of the global hash rate to transmute trapped natural gas reserves into liquid capital. By co opting the Nakamoto consensus, the regime bypassed the Swift system and evaded international blockades, processing upwards of eight billion dollars through global exchanges like Binance. Meanwhile, the state criminalized the civilian population's access to those exact same networks. Following Iran's brutal January twenty twenty six massacre and the subsequent February War blackout, The authoritarian apparatus did not bother attempting to decipher cryptographic traffic. They simply executed a devastating severing of the nation's digital lifeline by enforcing an eighty eight day blackout nearly two thousand, one hundred continuous hours, dissidents were violently marooned. Whether capital was held on custodial exchanges or secured in self custodied hardware wallets, their assets remained cryptographically secure, but practically useless, incapable of purchasing food or organizing clandestine transport, proving that absolute control over network nodes renders a decentralized ledger politically irrelevant. The protracted collapse of Venezuela offers an equally searing indictment. While millions of desperate citizens adopted crypto assets like tether USDT to survive of hyperinflation pushing towards six hundred percent, this mass adoption did nothing to erode the regime's monopoly on violence. By late twenty twenty five, the Maduro administration authorized state sanctioned exchanges to sell crude oil to China in exchange for stable coins, effectively becoming the first nation to manage a massive share of its public finances via digital ledgers. What began as an organic tool for citizen survival was transmuted into an economic shield for the very regime oppressing them the persistence of territorial jurisdiction. The deepest indictment of digital agorism is not that the state won. It is that the state was never seriously threatened. Bitcoin's most triumphant institutional champions are now Black Rock, Fidelity and sovereign wealth funds, the precise architecture of concentrated financial power. The protocol was engineered to circumvent. The cipherpunks did not lose to the state, they recruited it. Meanwhile, the escape patch proved most illusory where authoritarianism was most absolute. Bitcoin did not redistribute power. It redistributed speculative upside to those already positioned to capture it, while offering the dispossessed and illusion of sovereignty sophisticated enough to be mistaken for the real thing. Cryptographic friction is no substitute for the physical courage required to dismantle authoritarianism until its advocates reckon honestly with the infrastructure. Their revolution requires. Infrastructure that states own, patrol, and conseverate will Decentralized networks will continue to serve as financial instruments for the powerful and political placebos for the desperate. For more content like this, visit mises dot org.

structure.rationale: Moves deliberately from thesis to historical precedent to empirical case studies to conclusion, with identifiable sections, though transitions between theoretical and empirical registers are occasionally abrupt.

relevance.rationale: Anchored by specific 2025-2026 geopolitical events — Iran's January 2026 massacre and internet blackout, Venezuela's late-2025 state crypto adoption — making it timely, though the core argument is more theoretical than breaking-news driven.

clipability.rationale: Multiple standalone, quotable lines — 'The cipherpunks did not lose to the state, they recruited it,' 'An encrypted key grants its holder perfect cryptographic sovereignty and zero political agency' — that land hard without requiring surrounding context.

card.tagline: The cipherpunks didn't lose to the state — they recruited it
card.description: A Misesian takedown of Bitcoin maximalism argues that Iran, Venezuela, and BlackRock have already proven crypto cannot escape territorial state power.
card.reason: Because this episode challenges a core assumption driving today's crypto-politics debate

scored.description: Kenny is a professional comedian, talk radio host, and proud member of the New Right. He's also a millennial (but don't hold it against him).

latestEpisode.title: Death penalty for child predators?

structure.rationale: The episode has identifiable segments (McConnell health/Kentucky politics, then a hard pivot to the death penalty topic), but the transitions are loose and the McConnell section meanders through jokes and speculation before the host signals the real topic with 'we're going to talk about killing pedophiles.'

relevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with a live legislative and legal debate — six states passing child rape capital punishment laws, the Kennedy v. Louisiana precedent, and Texas's pending action — making it clearly on-topic for News/Politics, though the McConnell health speculation and Whoopi Goldberg tangents dilute focus.

clipability.rationale: The death penalty segment contains several self-contained, quotable moments — the six-state rundown, the Kennedy v. Louisiana framing, and the 'is he more likely to kill his victim' question — though the McConnell satirical phone call bit requires too much setup to clip cleanly.

card.tagline: Six states now execute child rapists — should Texas be next?
card.description: Kenny Webster breaks down the wave of state laws making child rape a capital offense and the 2008 Supreme Court precedent standing in the way.
card.reason: Because this episode covers an active state-level legislative debate with national implications

Positive, progressive news for democracy daily! Democrats, Leftists, liberals, and democratic socialists welcome. A respite from troubling headlines in difficult times. Sure, read the bad news, but then listen to us! Send good news tips to beowulf@twosquaredmediaproductions.com or 202-656-6271

scored.description:
Jeffrey Epstein was a multi millionaire who had political and business ties to some of the most rich and powerful people in the world. From businessmen to politicians at the highest levels, Epstein broke bread with them all.

Yet for years the Legacy media and the rest of high society looked the other way and ignored his behavior as multiple women came forward with allegations of abuse.

Even after he was convicted and subsequently received a sweetheart deal those same so called elites welcomed him back with open arms.

Now after his death and the arrest of Maxwell, the real story is starting to come together and the curtain has begun to be drawn back and what it has revealed is truly disturbing.

From Princes to Ex Presidents, the cast of scoundrels in this play spans continents and political affiliations leaving us with a transcontinental criminal conspiracy possibly unlike any we have ever seen before.

In this podcast we will explore all of the levels of Jeffrey Epstein and his criminal enterprise.

From his most trusted assistants to obscure associates, we will leave no stone unturned as we swim through the muck searching for clarity and answers to some of the most pressing questions of the case.

From interviews with people directly involved in the case to daily updates, the Epstein Chronicles will have it all.

Just like our other project, The Jeffrey Epstein Show, you can expect no punches pulled and consistent content. We have covered the Epstein case daily(everyday since October 1st 2019) and will continue to do so until there are convictions. With a library of well over 1k shows, you can expect a ton of content coming your way including on scene reporting from the Maxwell trial and from places like Zorro Ranch.

Thank you for tuning in and I look forward to having you all along for the ride.




(Created and Hosted by Bobby Capucci)

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-epstein-chronicles--5003294/support.

00:00:26.879 First, I want to remove any and all pressure that comes with the thought of adding a website. 00:00:27.120 --> 00:00:32.960 This might feel like just one more thing to pack onto your already full podcasting plate. 00:00:33.119 --> 00:00:34.399 But I have some good news. 00:00:34.560 --> 00:00:41.200 A little over half of popular podcast hosting platforms actually provide a website with their hosting plan. 00:00:41.359 --> 00:00:45.439 So implementing a website may be much easier than you initially thought. 00:00:45.600 --> 00:00:53.119 I'd like to share three reasons how a podcasting website levels up your show, and then we can determine if it's truly a need. 00:00:53.280 --> 00:00:56.719 I want you to think of your website as your podcast home on the internet. 00:00:57.039 --> 00:01:16.079 Instead of sending listeners to multiple locations where they can hear your episodes like Spotify and Apple Podcasts or maybe even social media platforms, you can send them one link to your website, and that actually has everything available in one convenient spot and then let your listeners choose what listening platform works best for them. 00:01:16.319 --> 00:01:21.760 Since your website is the home base for your podcast, it's important to know what to include in that home base. 00:01:22.079 --> 00:01:26.640 So I want to break down some of the components of a really great podcast website. 00:01:26.799 --> 00:01:42.640 Typically, a podcasting website will include all of your episodes, your episode details like show notes, chapters, artwork, and more, your episode transcripts, if supported, and links to follow or subscribe through your favorite listening directories. 00:01:42.799 --> 00:01:46.879 Your website becomes the central hub where your audience can explore your content. 00:01:47.040 --> 00:01:56.079 And if you choose to build your own website or work with a host that gives you customization controls, then the best part is you get control over that experience. 00:01:56.239 --> 00:02:03.280 When you have control over those customization options, you want to focus on making your podcasting website really specific to you. 00:02:03.439 --> 00:02:07.760 Show off your personality and make sure that it fits your overall vibe of your podcast. 00:02:07.920 --> 00:02:13.680 Of course, you'll want to include the ability to not only display your episodes, but also play them on your website. 00:02:13.840 --> 00:02:20.639 Buzzprout websites include our built-in player, which can actually be customized to match the look and feel of your podcast. 00:02:20.800 --> 00:02:24.159 You'll want to provide a way of connecting with you as the podcaster. 00:02:24.319 --> 00:02:34.400 A website can give listeners an easy way to engage with you either by using email, comments, or message board, or even a direct message communication option like fan mail. 00:02:34.560 --> 00:02:40.240 Your podcasting website can highlight your hosts, your guest, and all of the contributors assigned to your podcast. 00:02:40.319 --> 00:02:45.039 But you'll even have the option of assigning each guest to the episodes that they're on. 00:02:45.199 --> 00:02:49.360 And then you'll see that list of episodes when you visit their page on your website. 00:02:49.520 --> 00:02:56.960 Your website should also include a follow page, of course, where listeners can subscribe easily to your podcast using their favorite listening directory. 00:02:57.120 --> 00:03:05.360 And last but certainly not least, we recommend including your social media links so that your listeners can easily join your community and the discussion around your podcast. 00:03:05.520 --> 00:03:10.800 A fun feature that we offer here at Buzzsproute for your podcasting website is actually PodRole. 00:03:10.960 --> 00:03:16.560 So PodRoll allows you to link to some of your favorite podcasts right on your podcasting website. 00:03:16.719 --> 00:03:24.719 And since podcasting is so easily spread through word of mouth, this is kind of like a virtual word of mouth recommendation for your favorite podcast. 00:03:24.960 --> 00:03:39.120 Since the goal here is to make this website personal to your podcast and avoid anything generic, you'll also want to make sure you are customizing things like the color scheme, the background image, the fonts you are using, and even the URL you are using for your website. 00:03:39.280 --> 00:03:46.080 If you have purchased your own domain name, we recommend go ahead and change the URL for your site to match that domain. 00:03:46.240 --> 00:03:50.080 This is building identity and branding around your podcast. 00:03:50.319 --> 00:03:58.400 I feel like this question about having a podcasting website naturally leads to the follow-up question: is the podcast host provided website enough? 00:03:58.560 --> 00:04:00.400 Or should I be building my own? 00:04:00.639 --> 00:04:08.879 Shoot us some fan mail if you'd like for me to dive a little deeper into this question and explore some of the pros and cons for building your own custom website. 00:04:09.039 --> 00:04:16.399 Whether you're using a website provided by your podcast host or creating something custom, a website helps to build your brand. 00:04:16.560 --> 00:04:21.199 A website isn't just about listening, it's about growing something bigger than your podcast. 00:04:21.360 --> 00:04:28.079 When you have a dedicated space on the internet, it gives you the opportunity to build brand identity, it becomes searchable. 00:04:28.240 --> 00:04:34.000 And as you grow, this will help with discoverability and even lead to some sponsorship opportunities. 00:04:34.240 --> 00:04:41.920 You can promote products or services that you offer through your website, and you can even turn listeners into subscribers and super fans. 00:04:42.079 --> 00:04:47.199 Your podcast website is essentially working as a marketing engine for everything else you're building. 00:04:47.439 --> 00:04:56.480 Even if you're not monetizing yet, having the foundation of a website early on makes a huge difference later down the road, depending on your goals and how they evolve. 00:04:56.639 --> 00:05:04.720 If you have questions about your podcasting website, you can reach out to us on the podcaster success team by emailing support at buzzsprout.com. 00:05:04.879 --> 00:05:12.319 If there's a question you'd like to answer on a future podcasting QA episode, send it to me by clicking the send us a text link in the show notes. 00:05:12.480 --> 00:05:16.959 Remember, your question could help hundreds of other podcasters just like you. 00:05:17.040 --> 00:05:18.319 So don't keep it to yourself. 00:05:18.480 --> 00:05:19.279 Just send it in. 00:05:19.439 --> 00:05:22.079 Be sure to join us every Monday to kickstart your week. 00:05:22.240 --> 00:05:23.519 Thank you so much for listening. 00:05:23.600 --> 00:05:26.000 And as always, keep podcasting.

structure.rationale: Clear intro question, enumerated reasons, and a defined close with a call to action give the episode a solid, navigable throughline.

relevance.rationale: This is a podcasting tips and platform marketing episode with zero connection to news, politics, or current events.

clipability.rationale: A few self-contained tips could stand alone briefly, but most segments rely on the surrounding instructional context to land.

card.tagline: Do you actually need a podcast website? Here's the honest answer.
card.description: Buzzsprout's Cara Passetti breaks down three reasons a podcast website builds brand identity, discoverability, and long-term monetization potential.

transcript.scoringWindow: WEBVTT 00:00:00.160 --> 00:00:07.839 I have nothing but compassion for Dustin Poirier after his uh meltdown and arrest at that Atlanta, Georgia airport. 00:00:08.000 --> 00:00:21.920 Dustin Poirier revealed on the Stephen Bartlett Diary of a CEO podcast that the arrest uh his meltdown happened because even though his father's day started out great, he got to spend time with his kids. 00:00:22.239 --> 00:00:26.000 Dustin revealed that he started thinking about his own father. 00:00:26.160 --> 00:00:34.000 And his father had a history of alcoholism and uh physical abuse with Dustin's mom. 00:00:34.159 --> 00:00:38.000 So I could see why that would start putting him in a dark place. 00:00:38.240 --> 00:00:48.399 And I guess he said, you know, on his travels en route, he had that layover at the uh Georgia airport, and there was somewhere where alcohol was available. 00:00:48.560 --> 00:00:57.039 So Dustin admitted he made a poor choice and started drinking to try to squash, I guess, his down feelings. 00:00:57.280 --> 00:01:00.799 So when we put it in that context, I think we could all empathize. 00:01:00.960 --> 00:01:15.280 I mean, it's Father's Day, it starts out great for him because he gets to spend time with his own kids, but once he starts reflecting on where his father's at and his own relationship with his father, I can see why that would put him in a downward spiral. 00:01:15.519 --> 00:01:23.519 So I hope folks on the internet who jumped on the bandwagon and started either making fun of Dustin or condemning him for that arrest. 00:01:23.680 --> 00:01:41.439 I hope they can grape together a little compassion in their heart and just hope that Dustin's able to get the help and support that he needs to work through how he'll handle not just his relationship with his father, but also his feelings and how he deals with that. 00:01:41.680 --> 00:01:43.920 So, best of wishes to you, Dustin. 00:01:44.079 --> 00:01:47.359 This is not easy, and I certainly wish you the best of this. 00:01:47.599 --> 00:01:54.319 Team De Los Santos will file a protest regarding the KO loss to Jose Valenzuela. 00:01:54.560 --> 00:02:02.480 I'm really glad to hear that because I thought Edwin de Edwin de los Santos was completely wronged in that fight. 00:02:03.280 --> 00:02:13.039 Yes, Valenzuela dropped him, but as de los Santos was semi-reclined on the canvas, Jose took another swipe at him, blatant foul. 00:02:13.919 --> 00:02:15.759 He threw another left uppercut. 00:02:16.960 --> 00:02:35.199 Now, boxing journalist Mike Coppinger had the audacity to say after the fight that the the punch didn't hit De Los Santos squarely and that it grazed his shoulder, according to either the officials or the commissioner. 00:02:35.439 --> 00:02:37.120 That is 100% false. 00:02:37.360 --> 00:02:40.639 That punch did connect on De Los Santos' chin. 00:02:40.879 --> 00:02:41.599 But you know what? 00:02:41.759 --> 00:02:54.960 Even if it didn't, it's an automatic DQ if you throw a punch at somebody who's down on the canvas because he aimed that uppercut at about two and a half feet off the canvas, which is where de los Santos' head was. 00:02:55.120 --> 00:03:01.360 There's absolutely no way Jose Valenzuela can claim he did not know that Edwin de los Santos was down. 00:03:01.680 --> 00:03:07.680 It's not like Valenzuela threw a punch at where you would expect de los Santos' head to be if he were standing. 00:03:07.759 --> 00:03:16.960 I mean, in basketball it'd be called a flagrant foul, but by the rules of boxing, Valenzuela should have been DQ'd and by default, Elon Edwin de la Rosa should have won that fight. 00:03:17.120 --> 00:03:21.039 So I'm interested to hear what the officials and the commission has to say about that. 00:03:21.280 --> 00:03:22.879 What do you guys think the outcome should be? 00:03:23.039 --> 00:03:28.879 Should Valenzuela get DQ'd and we give De Los Santos the W, or should we just call the whole thing a no contest? 00:03:29.039 --> 00:03:31.520 Or do we let Valenzuela keep the KO victory? 00:03:31.599 --> 00:03:33.120 I look forward to reading your comments. 00:03:33.280 --> 00:03:33.840 Thanks. 00:03:34.080 --> 00:03:40.879 That security guard at the airport who called for backup, I think he's incompetent as a security guard. 00:03:41.039 --> 00:03:46.479 That's the security guard whose body cam footage has been aired and memed all over social media. 00:03:46.639 --> 00:03:49.599 So if you're the security guard, you're not just a gate agent. 00:03:49.680 --> 00:03:52.639 You're security guard if you got that body cam on, right? 00:03:52.879 --> 00:03:55.840 And I don't know if he's armed or unarmed. 00:03:56.080 --> 00:03:58.080 However, let's think about this a bit. 00:03:58.319 --> 00:04:03.840 Any security guard at the airport most likely is going to be dealing with someone who is unarmed. 00:04:04.000 --> 00:04:12.240 Because how can anybody get their weapon past TSA unless there's some type of federal agent or military and they're allowed to have their weapon on board with them. 00:04:12.400 --> 00:04:16.720 But if that's the case, we should assume that they're not gonna be irresponsible with their weapon. 00:04:16.879 --> 00:04:23.040 So safe to bet that even if this person is an MMA fighter and they're drunk, they're not gonna have a weapon on them. 00:04:23.199 --> 00:04:28.879 Now, the first thing this security guard should have picked up on was that Dustin was already upset and angry. 00:04:29.040 --> 00:04:31.600 So what's your first the first thing you should do? 00:04:31.759 --> 00:04:34.399 You should try to de-escalate the situation. 00:04:34.639 --> 00:04:36.560 But what did this security guard do? 00:04:36.800 --> 00:04:39.199 Well, he's a wimp, so he escalated it. 00:04:39.279 --> 00:04:40.800 He said, I don't want to fight you, man. 00:04:40.959 --> 00:04:42.079 I'm gonna call for back. 00:04:42.240 --> 00:04:43.040 It's like, come on. 00:04:43.120 --> 00:04:45.360 You know, he's this is a professional MMA fighter. 00:04:45.439 --> 00:04:47.040 He doesn't really want to fight you. 00:04:47.199 --> 00:04:50.240 He fight, he used to fight for tons of money. 00:04:50.399 --> 00:04:55.680 I've been blessed to meet several super effective, humane, compassionate security guards. 00:04:55.839 --> 00:05:05.680 And one of them I got to observe de-escalate situations with other folks, and another security guard de-escalated a situation that I was involved in where I was very upset. 00:05:05.839 --> 00:05:16.879 But I would bet that if you asked any veteran security guard, they would probably tell you that at least 95% of the incidents they deal with can be handled through de-escalation. 00:05:17.040 --> 00:05:17.839 How do you do that? 00:05:18.000 --> 00:05:23.600 You reach out to the person, you find some point of connection, you divert them from whatever they're upset about. 00:05:23.839 --> 00:05:33.920 You may or may not be able to reason with them, so you might just have to be kind and try to communicate in a way that they don't feel attacked, and maybe they'll even open up. 00:05:34.079 --> 00:05:53.279 I dare say that if one of my very good friends who is a security guard had been there instead of that airport security guard, I bet he could have gotten to Dustin Poirier's heart and been able to reach him about the real story, which is Dustin was upset about his father, who's a chronic alcoholic and is now homeless. 00:05:53.519 --> 00:06:00.000 What Dustin needed in that moment was an open heart and an open ear, not somebody to try to flex on him. 00:06:00.160 --> 00:06:03.439 Because when a fighter feels like someone's flexing on them, what are they gonna do? 00:06:03.519 --> 00:06:05.199 They're just gonna up the ante even higher. 00:06:05.360 --> 00:06:09.680 Which is what Dustin did verbally, so it led to his arrest. 00:06:09.920 --> 00:06:12.879 Bottom line, diffuse, don't escalate. 00:06:13.120 --> 00:06:16.480 Let me know what you guys think of that incident in the comments. 00:06:16.639 --> 00:06:16.959 Thanks.

00:13:37.919 --> 00:13:38.320 Yeah. 00:13:38.559 --> 00:13:39.679 And you say what? 00:13:40.240 --> 00:13:41.679 You need to earn games. 00:13:41.919 --> 00:13:43.679 Just like you need to earn playing time. 00:13:43.840 --> 00:13:44.399 Yeah, yeah. 00:13:44.639 --> 00:14:01.200 Now, you know, and you've experienced this um a few years ago, I was probably on the phone for four hours every weekend, and a lot of it has to do with assigning, putting umpires where they are capable of being, right? 00:14:01.519 --> 00:14:03.600 So that's what I strive to do when I assign. 00:14:03.759 --> 00:14:22.080 Like I assign uh 43 college teams, umpires for 43 college teams, and I try to, you know, uh put maybe a less strong person with a really strong person, just to kind of balance that out and help train that other individual. 00:14:22.320 --> 00:14:28.960 Um, at the high school level, we really haven't been doing that much, but we will be from now on because we've had a change. 00:14:29.120 --> 00:14:35.279 But um, do you have a different scheduling philosophy when it comes to the matchups on the games? 00:14:35.519 --> 00:14:35.840 Yes. 00:14:36.080 --> 00:14:37.279 Well, I do. 00:14:38.080 --> 00:14:40.879 But in years past, we haven't had that at that level. 00:14:41.039 --> 00:14:42.080 We will have that now. 00:14:42.399 --> 00:14:46.320 Because you are well aware of who the who the top matchups are each. 00:14:46.879 --> 00:14:47.440 Absolutely. 00:14:47.679 --> 00:14:54.559 And you know, in in in in the LL, it I it it hurts me to say this, but on any given night, think about it, Chris. 00:14:54.639 --> 00:14:58.960 There's probably what, four, five big matchups on any given night. 00:14:59.039 --> 00:14:59.440 That's it. 00:14:59.600 --> 00:15:08.159 Every other game is yeah, so what I've been striving to do, and what is gonna this year and the next year, it's gonna get a lot better. 00:15:08.799 --> 00:15:15.360 We we have a we we do have a lot of good umpires, but we need to get those good umpires paired on those good games. 00:15:15.440 --> 00:15:15.679 Yeah. 00:15:15.840 --> 00:15:23.600 And that after that one debacle we had where it was like three games in a night that were just awful, we've made those changes. 00:15:23.759 --> 00:15:27.279 So you will see more and more of better umpires on big games. 00:15:28.080 --> 00:15:33.759 I mean, you know, you got Solanko E Town, but you can't have you gotta have two good umpires on that. 00:15:33.840 --> 00:15:35.759 So that's where we're headed. 00:15:36.480 --> 00:15:44.559 Um, you got anything while I scroll through my list of questions? 00:15:45.279 --> 00:15:47.519 Um I mean, I always have great. 00:15:48.000 --> 00:15:48.480 I got one. 00:15:48.559 --> 00:15:52.720 So what rule in the book would you and I got this? 00:15:52.879 --> 00:15:54.639 I got two questions now that I thought about it. 00:15:55.039 --> 00:15:57.519 What rule would you like would you change? 00:15:58.480 --> 00:16:01.759 Is there if any, would you change any of the rules? 00:16:02.480 --> 00:16:09.360 Well, the only thing that I would do, given that I do three different rules codes, I would put the rules codes all the same. 00:16:09.679 --> 00:16:14.720 So we have different pitching rules for USA versus high school versus college. 00:16:15.440 --> 00:16:20.320 So as a kid grows up, we're teaching them one way, and then all of a sudden they need to change. 00:16:20.480 --> 00:16:24.480 And that's why we see the illegal pitch pitching situations that we have. 00:16:24.639 --> 00:16:26.559 Because you know in high school you're allowed to step back. 00:16:26.720 --> 00:16:28.799 We sure can't do that in college, right? 00:16:29.120 --> 00:16:34.240 So I I just and there's other rules too that I wish they would all just be this be the same. 00:16:34.480 --> 00:16:45.039 Yeah, because I've seen that that's actually a really good point because I've seen that, especially with the pitching, like with different positions and different, and someone will call something, and I'm like, where I I've never heard that before. 00:16:45.200 --> 00:16:46.639 Like, what is that rule? 00:16:46.879 --> 00:16:59.360 And the problem is, you know, with something like that, and if a pitcher's not trained, you can see they're they're like kind of rattled, especially at the young ages, like and you could see them getting all off, like with their mechanics, uh, because then they're trying not to get in trouble. 00:16:59.600 --> 00:17:01.360 And you can't change something like that in a game. 00:17:01.600 --> 00:17:05.200 So you basically you want to like basically a universal rule book across the board. 00:17:05.440 --> 00:17:05.599 Yes. 00:17:05.759 --> 00:17:12.960 I I would like it if all of our rules, because it's difficult to train umpires because we all do multiple levels because there just aren't enough of us. 00:17:13.119 --> 00:17:13.440 Right. 00:17:14.000 --> 00:17:19.039 And so one one day I could do a USA softball game, the next day do a high school game, and the next day do a college game. 00:17:19.119 --> 00:17:20.880 I got three different sets of rules codes. 00:17:21.039 --> 00:17:21.440 Yeah. 00:17:21.680 --> 00:17:23.519 So I mean, I'm not that bright. 00:17:23.839 --> 00:17:24.400 You're gonna confuse them. 00:17:25.039 --> 00:17:25.599 I'm not that bright. 00:17:26.079 --> 00:17:31.759 So uh I that's what I would I mean, there are some rules that I usually say. 00:17:31.839 --> 00:17:36.960 You know what, I don't make the rules, I just enforce them, but none are coming to me right now. 00:17:37.359 --> 00:17:41.359 So that uh that leads me to a specific rule. 00:17:42.240 --> 00:17:46.319 What is the book definition of an infield fly? 00:17:46.559 --> 00:17:47.920 Ah, yes, that's a good one. 00:17:48.160 --> 00:17:48.880 An infield fly. 00:17:49.440 --> 00:17:54.799 You probably don't remember, but when I was back in Rec, I had an issue with you on an infield fly call. 00:17:55.119 --> 00:17:55.920 Well, you've been holding on the call. 00:17:56.079 --> 00:17:56.799 I do not remember. 00:17:59.599 --> 00:18:00.880 You've never had an issue with this. 00:18:01.519 --> 00:18:02.799 It was not like an argument or nothing. 00:18:02.880 --> 00:18:07.839 I just didn't agree with you, did you call Did you set this recording up tonight just to bring that to bring this question up? 00:18:08.160 --> 00:18:10.240 I think it just popped in my head when we were talking about rules. 00:18:10.400 --> 00:18:16.400 Well, but see, the thing about Chris is I know he looked at the rules book, so I can't just BS him because he looked. 00:18:16.559 --> 00:18:16.640 Yeah. 00:18:16.799 --> 00:18:18.799 And he'll say, I can't find a rule for that. 00:18:19.039 --> 00:18:20.880 Why are you looking at the rule book? 00:18:21.119 --> 00:18:23.039 Um, the infield fly rule. 00:18:23.200 --> 00:18:25.920 Okay, that this that's a great question. 00:18:26.559 --> 00:18:31.359 It's in play when there's either runners on first and second or basis loaded less than two outs, right? 00:18:31.519 --> 00:18:31.839 Yep. 00:18:32.079 --> 00:18:43.759 We call infield fly when there is a pop in the infield, pop fly in the infield within the confines of the infield that can be caught with relative ease. 00:18:44.079 --> 00:18:45.839 And what's it normal? 00:18:46.000 --> 00:18:49.119 Well, there we go with that subjectivity again. 00:18:49.519 --> 00:18:58.160 Um I base that on the level that I'm at, right? 00:18:58.240 --> 00:18:58.400 Yeah. 00:18:58.559 --> 00:19:02.240 So a college kid's gonna catch a lot more than a 10-year-old is. 00:19:02.640 --> 00:19:07.039 So if I feel like that 10-year-old is not gonna get to that ball, I'm not calling it, right? 00:19:07.200 --> 00:19:10.240 The other thing that comes into play is the weather, the wind, whatever. 00:19:10.480 --> 00:19:18.240 Just I always always have to keep in mind that the infield fly is a rule that protects the offense. 00:19:18.480 --> 00:19:18.720 Right. 00:19:18.880 --> 00:19:19.119 Right? 00:19:19.279 --> 00:19:21.119 It is there to protect the offense. 00:19:21.440 --> 00:19:28.559 So I'm not gonna call it if it's a ball that I think might get down, because I'm not because then I'm giving the defense an out. 00:19:28.799 --> 00:19:29.039 Yeah. 00:19:29.200 --> 00:19:33.599 So, but it's the whole thing is with normal effort. 00:19:33.759 --> 00:19:34.000 Right. 00:19:34.160 --> 00:19:36.799 The play needs to be made with normal effort. 00:19:37.119 --> 00:19:39.599 Not like a diving awesome catch. 00:19:39.839 --> 00:19:40.079 Correct. 00:19:40.160 --> 00:19:42.240 So and it has to be in the field of play. 00:19:42.480 --> 00:19:47.039 And it's because I I I've seen it where as soon as that ball goes up in the air, into a fly. 00:19:47.279 --> 00:19:47.440 Absolutely. 00:19:47.680 --> 00:19:51.839 It's like without even without even glancing down to see what actions the infield's taking. 00:19:52.160 --> 00:19:52.480 Correct. 00:19:52.640 --> 00:19:56.799 We are taught to not make that call till the apex of the pop-up. 00:19:57.119 --> 00:19:57.359 Okay. 00:19:57.519 --> 00:19:58.319 So you shouldn't be. 00:19:58.799 --> 00:20:00.079 And you're not following the ball the whole way. 00:20:00.319 --> 00:20:00.799 You gotta look at it. 00:20:00.960 --> 00:20:02.319 You should not be following the ball. 00:20:02.480 --> 00:20:06.079 But we should umpires, and this is what we teach, you shouldn't ever follow the ball. 00:20:06.240 --> 00:20:09.279 The kid will take you to where the ball's going, right? 00:20:09.519 --> 00:20:15.920 Just like when I'm behind a plate and there's a pop-up that goes and I have no freaking idea where it is, I just watch the catcher's shoulder. 00:20:16.000 --> 00:20:17.440 She'll take me to it, right? 00:20:17.599 --> 00:20:24.480 So any pop-up, I watch the kid, I'm not watching the ball because there's a whole lot that can happen down here when I'm watching that ball. 00:20:25.119 --> 00:20:30.559 And that the the obstruction, the interference, those are the things we need to be aware of, not watch the ball. 00:20:30.720 --> 00:20:36.240 So when the ball goes up, I know it's in the infield, I just watch players and then I make my determination. 00:20:36.400 --> 00:20:38.880 But I don't say it as soon as the ball's hit, you know. 00:20:39.200 --> 00:20:42.240 Yeah, my my mine with you, mine with you was what was it? 00:20:42.400 --> 00:20:58.799 It was we were on our JV field, and it was a pop-up to the third towards the third baseman, and the it was the wind was blowing decent, and it was carrying her further and further out towards the outfield, and you called infield fly, and I'm like, that was not normal effort. 00:20:58.960 --> 00:21:02.640 Like she had to so that was my only disagreement with you. 00:21:02.880 --> 00:21:03.440 You were right then. 00:21:03.599 --> 00:21:04.000 You were right. 00:21:04.160 --> 00:21:06.559 That was my only in all these years, my only disagreement. 00:21:06.799 --> 00:21:09.119 If it was not normal effort, you're you were right. 00:21:09.279 --> 00:21:10.319 I I blew it. 00:21:10.559 --> 00:21:15.359 But yeah, that's my least favorite rule is the drop third strike. 00:21:15.599 --> 00:21:20.640 The the drop third strike just gets very frustrating to me, probably as a pitcher's dad. 00:21:20.799 --> 00:21:40.799 Um, probably it's just it's but like it's just a frustrating rule because and

scored.description: Welcome to 'ASX BRIEFS,' the definitive podcast for enthusiasts, investors, and professionals keen on staying ahead of the curve in the Australian Stock Exchange (ASX). Hosted by Andrew Musgrave, 'ASX BRIEFS' delves deep into the heart of Australia's financial markets, bringing you insightful conversations with the minds shaping the future of investing down under. Each episode, join Andrew as he interviews a diverse lineup of fund managers, executives, and industry insiders, offering you a unique blend of expert analysis, strategic insights, and the latest trends affecting the ASX. Whether you're a seasoned investor or just starting out, 'ASX BRIEFS' is your go-to source for comprehensive updates and thought-provoking discussions designed to inform, inspire, and empower your investment journey. Tune in to 'ASX BRIEFS' and take the pulse of Australia's financial markets right at your fingertips.

Can you walk us through the logic of the deal? What does Calix give up? What does it retain? And why is this outcome strategically the right one for the company at this point in time? transcript.scoringWindow: Andrew Musgrave Welcome again to ASX Brief. And today we're joined by Phil Hodgson, the Managing Director and CEO of Calix Limited, an Australian industrial technology company developing platform solutions for industrial decarbonisation across cement, steel, alumina, critical minerals, as well as solutions in water treatment and carbon dioxide removal. Phil, great to have you with me today and welcome to the ASX Briefs Podcast. Phil Hodgson Thanks very much, Andrew. Andrew Musgrave Phil, for listeners who are new to Calix, can you explain the core platform technology, what it does, and why it matters for heavy industry? Phil Hodgson Certainly, Andrew. So, the core platform technology is a technology developed here in Australia. It's essentially around a new type of kiln or furnace, a new way to heat stuff up, as we say. A lot of today's minerals, cement, steel, etc., are made in furnaces and Calix has a new way to make these products. Essentially, what we do is we separate how you heat from what you heat. We do that with a rather large steel tube. The biggest tubes we've made are now as big as we'll ever make them, 1.8 meters in diameter and over 30 meters high. So, we're already a full scale on the core technology. We heat that tube to over a thousand degrees centigrade. So, this tube's glowing red hot. And whatever we heat goes down the middle of the tube. It's going to be a small particle size, much like sort of fine powder. So, iron ore fines, cement, sort of meal, all of absolutely the right size for our technology. That basically gets dropped down the tube. And as the dust or small particles floats down the tube, the red-hot walls of the tube radiate into the particles and heats them up and we can heat the tube any way you like. We can heat it with renewable energy, we can heat it with traditional fossil fuels on the outside of the tube and so the technology represents a way to heat stuff up that can use all sorts of different energy sources. But the other interesting thing is inside the tube, a lot of people may not realize that, say limestone, which is used to make cement, is nearly half by weight CO2. And so, when the cement and lime industries heat up that limestone, the CO2 comes out of the rock, and that's over half the emissions of the cement and lime industry. And so, when you're heating limestone in our tube, that CO2 doesn't escape. It doesn't head off into the atmosphere or get mixed with furnace gases. It comes out the top of the tube as a pretty pure stream. And so, the first applications of the technology we developed were to separate out CO2 when making lime and cement. But of course, since then we've gone on to look at all sorts of different applications, including our latest applications in making green iron, in which case we've got hydrogen inside the tube, which converts the iron ore to iron. So, you can see it's quite a versatile core technology platform that basically, as I say, is replacing the traditional kiln or furnace in all sorts of different industries. Andrew Musgrave And industrial decarbonisation is one of the hardest problems in climate. Sectors like cement and steel account for a significant share of global emissions and have few easy levers to pull. So why is Calix's approach particularly well suited to solving what others can't? Phil Hodgson Yeah, if you sort of think back to the tube, I described heated externally, it's actually a fairly simple, or we like to term it elegant solution. There are no moving parts, there's no complex pieces of equipment. It's basically a tube that's being heated externally. Of course, there's a lot of know-how that goes into developing the full-scale versions of this. But that's our first key advantage. The core concept is simple and elegant. The other advantage is, of course, decarbonisation, depending upon where you are in the world, is developing at different pieces. In some parts of the world, it's moving ahead, in other parts it's not. So, we need a technology that's going to be cost effective today, regardless of if there's a carbon price. And so, if you recall, I mentioned the energy flexibility of the tube, the ability to heat with electrons or heat with traditional fossil fuels or burning waste is actually one of the things that could make the technology cost effective today. So, you sort of get the decarbonization as a free ride or a cherry on top to economic solutions today with the tube. So, I think that's what separates us from other technologies attempting to achieve this. Andrew Musgrave Now, in April this year, the company announced the restructure of the lithium midstream project joint venture with PLS, another ASX listed company. Can you walk us through the logic of the deal? What does Calix give up? What does it retain? And why is this outcome strategically the right one for the company at this point in time? Phil Hodgson Yeah, this is an interesting one. We started our journey with PLS over three years ago, and that was to take a core, our core technology and use it in the lithium industry. PLS dig up spodumene, and we're using our core technology to take advantage of cheap energy, as in renewable energy at a mine site, to extract and concentrate lithium at PLS's site in the Pilbara. and so originally, we went into this deal as a joint venture partner with equity, us 45%, PLS 55%. but as the industry structurally changed and margins tightened on lithium, and that's the difference between the spodumene price and the lithium price, it became harder for us to participate in this simply because of the capital required. And so, we changed, we agreed to PLS to change the structure of the deal where we back out effectively our equity. We recovered all of the equity that we're put in, and PLS moved forward with carriage of the project, licensed from us. And so, the solution, which is the ability to produce lithium at a mine site with renewable energy, is jointly owned by us and PLS. And moving forward, we'll be sharing the margins on that when applied to third parties. But as part of the deal, PLS get a free license. So, so we got a capital back, PLS get a free license. So, the logic is that there's no downside for us. We're not exposed from a capital perspective. There's only upside with respect to license income from third parties, and we get paid engineering fees. It's really part of a capital light strategy that we're very focused on and doubling down on to some extent now as well. So that's the logic behind the deal. Andrew Musgrave Now turning to Leilac Technology. Leilac Limited is Calix's cement and lime application subsidiary. So, for our listeners who may not be familiar, can you explain what Leilac does and specifically why the ability to separate processed CO2 in cement is such a difficult and valuable problem to solve? Phil Hodgson Yeah. So, I described previously, I guess, our tube, the core part of our technology that we that we heat from the outside. And when we're processing cement, which is mainly limestone, that gets dropped down the middle of the tube and as that heats up, the CO2 comes out of the rock. And so that CO2 in a normal cement kiln gets mixed with furnace gases and ejected into the atmosphere. So separating CO2 from a whole lot of other gas is really difficult. You need big chemical plants to solve that problem and with our technology though, because the CO2 is trapped in the tube, it exits the top of the tube as a pretty pure stream. And so, the core technology has the ability to directly separate that CO2 that's coming out of the rock. And so that's what makes it a little different. We're not trying to separate a gas from a gas, we just don't allow the gases to mix in the first place. And of course, the ability to heat the tube, say with renewable electrons, you can see how we can start to get to very low to zero emissions cement using our technology. Andrew Musgrave And Leilac's hybrid heating system has the potential to lower fuel costs for cement producers independent of any carbon price. How important is that dual value proposition reducing costs now while enabling carbon capture later in convincing cement majors to commit to the technology? Phil Hodgson Well, I'd say it's critical. If you've in terms of the early part of the podcast, we talked a little bit about decarbonisation and the different pace at which different parts of the world are chasing decarbonisation. Some areas certainly stalled. So, if you have a technology that has a value proposition today without a price on carbon, then that can allow some of these cement majors to move forward on looking at the technology and committing to the technology. And they get the carbon and decarbonisation down the track as the cherry on top. So, the hybrid heating system, this is the ability of our tube to be heated in any way you like. We can have renewable electrons on the outside of the tube. We can have gas burning normal gases like fossil fuels, or we can burn waste. And so, the ability to switch between electrons, which may be expensive in some parts of the day, but cheap in other parts of the day, switching between those electrons and a standard fuel or even a waste fuel, which in some cases is very cheap to negative cost, that is what enables the technology to be economic today. And so, the hybrid heating system is a very important part of the technology that allows it to be economic today. Andrew Musgrave And you've landed a joint development agreement with the Adani Group's Ambuja Cements, one of the world's top cement producers, and it's a significant commercial milestone. So, how did this partnership come about and why has Leilac been selected as a strategic partner? Phil Hodgson Yeah, so we've been in discussion with many cement producers over several years, Adani among them. Adani, of course, became a major cement producer overnight with the purchase of Holcim's assets in India. They instantly became number two cement group in India and so in discussion with Adani and numerous other cement players, they've become

card.tagline: The Australian company turning a glowing steel tube into a climate solution
card.description: Calix CEO Phil Hodgson explains how a simple externally-heated tube could decarbonise cement, steel, and lithium production without waiting for a carbon price.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down a hard-to-decarbonise sector with unusual clarity
