when I seen it. 00:07:31.680 --> 00:07:40.240 And so to have them come to be a part of that and give those kids that experience that may not be having the opportunity to go to a business game. 00:07:40.240 --> 00:07:44.480 So we're just trying to bring that Detroit life to Battle Creek, our town. 00:07:44.720 --> 00:07:58.879 And these uh third to ninth graders are gonna get some professional level tips and coaching in this, and that's going to, I presume you know better than I inspire whatever uh basketball dreams they have. 00:07:59.199 --> 00:07:59.839 Exactly. 00:07:59.839 --> 00:08:12.000 Take it to the next level, and to say that I had this opportunity to work with the Pistons Academy, that is not only name-dropping, but it's a confidence builder. 00:08:12.240 --> 00:08:12.800 Absolutely. 00:08:12.800 --> 00:08:25.360 So if you've uh, for example, you hit a Lions clinic before and uh that was similarly situated, you got to see right uh how the kids respond to that. 00:08:25.360 --> 00:08:26.319 Talk about that. 00:08:26.319 --> 00:08:30.160 What did you see on that day and you expect to see on this one? 00:08:30.480 --> 00:08:42.639 I'm expecting to see a lot of smiles and a lot of hugs that the kids usually give, even not just to me, but to themselves and to their teammates, the encouragement after the Lions Academy. 00:08:42.639 --> 00:08:48.799 One of the cool things was parents will come up to me and say, they really put something through to my kids. 00:08:48.799 --> 00:08:53.600 They used to fight all the time and now they're engaging in teamwork and building and helping one another. 00:08:53.600 --> 00:09:01.039 And so that confidence alone, one of the things that our foundation wants to do is create opportunities on and off the court. 00:09:01.039 --> 00:09:07.120 So you have to be a class uh teammate, sportsman-like kind of not just on the field, but off the field as well. 00:09:07.120 --> 00:09:13.519 And so that is what I'm hoping to engage in this opportunities that most kids, bigger cities have. 00:09:13.519 --> 00:09:19.679 So if we can offer that to our kids in Battle Creek, we're really building in their future and surrounding areas. 00:09:20.720 --> 00:09:31.360 All right, Pistons Academy Clinic, August 22nd at full blast, nine in the morning until 1 p.m., looking for boys and girls in grades three to nine. 00:09:31.360 --> 00:09:34.639 What do they have to do to get registered, Brittany? 00:09:34.960 --> 00:09:40.320 They can go on to the Pistons Academy.com and register that way. 00:09:40.320 --> 00:09:40.879 Okay. 00:09:41.519 --> 00:09:51.919 And we'll put a link in the show notes too at battlecreekpodcast.com in case you can't uh write that down or get to it, so you can just remember that and then find this discussion and click through. 00:09:51.919 --> 00:09:53.200 So this is August. 00:09:53.200 --> 00:09:56.080 Do they have to be you probably recommend they get to it, right? 00:09:56.080 --> 00:09:57.519 If if they want to be a part of this. 00:09:57.840 --> 00:09:59.039 Get registered right away. 00:09:59.039 --> 00:10:00.960 It is filling up very quickly. 00:10:00.960 --> 00:10:04.240 I'm just hoping soon I check and it'll say sold out. 00:10:04.240 --> 00:10:14.159 So if they are looking for sponsorships, I encourage them to text me at 269-358-8383. 00:10:14.559 --> 00:10:14.960 All right.

structure.rationale: The interview follows a clear conversational throughline moving from the flag football event to the Pistons Academy clinic, with identifiable topic transitions and a functional close.

relevance.rationale: This episode is a community nonprofit/youth sports feature with no connection to news or political events.

clipability.rationale: Most moments require surrounding context to land, though the passage about kids fighting less after the Lions camp and the 'bringing Detroit life to Battle Creek' line offer limited standalone potential.

card.tagline: A local mom is bringing Detroit Lions and Pistons coaching to Battle Creek kids for free
card.description: B Squared Foundation founder Brittany details two upcoming free and low-cost youth sports events open to kids across Calhoun County and beyond.
card.reason: Because this episode spotlights free youth opportunities in your local community

scored.description:
Welcome to the Wall Street Truthbombs channel where we cover financial news, break down the markets, and deliver hard-hitting analysis with no corporate spin. We break down complex Wall Street stories and economic developments in a way that’s clear, direct, and unfiltered — so our audience gets the truth, not the talking points.

Wall Street Truthbombs is led by its host and creator, Mark Malek, a fearless financial commentator known for cutting through media noise, and delivering bold insights on what’s really happening in the markets. With a fast-growing audience of viewers tired of watered-down finance news,  brings honesty, urgency, and edge to every episode.

latestEpisode.title: THE Middle Class VANISHED as CONSUMERS COLLAPSE...

clip.text: not a wealth shield because they lack liquid emergency assets. 00:05:53.040 --> 00:05:56.480 The math is cracking them wide open anyway. 00:05:56.560 --> 00:06:00.160 And the generational data makes this even sharper. 00:06:00.480 --> 00:06:09.040 Millennials drop to an average market pulse index of 58.1 and Q1 of 26, a 1.2% quarterly decline. 00:06:09.199 --> 00:06:19.920 They lead all generations in significant index decreases, with nearly 13% experiencing sharp downward movement in a single quarter. 00:06:20.160 --> 00:06:35.360 Because of this specific asset drain, millennials now officially represent the single largest generational portion of the striver population at 7.59%, driven primarily by, well, a lack of assets. 00:06:35.600 --> 00:06:35.920

transcript.scoringWindow: --> 00:01:17.040 Okay, here is what the Equifax data actually shows. 00:01:17.280 --> 00:01:27.680 The market pulse index, which synthesizes credit, debt, and income and asset data across millions of anonymous households, fell for the second straight quarter. 00:01:27.840 --> 00:01:37.120 It dropped from 61.6 to 60.9, with declines observed across every single generation simultaneously. 00:01:37.280 --> 00:01:41.280 That sounds like a small move, but the direction is what really matters here. 00:01:41.359 --> 00:01:43.760 And the direction, my friends, is down. 00:01:44.319 --> 00:01:49.920 And inside that headline number is a structural breakdown that should stop you cold. 00:01:50.159 --> 00:01:54.159 Equifax segments US consumers into three separate tiers. 00:01:54.319 --> 00:01:55.599 They call them thrivers. 00:01:55.680 --> 00:01:58.959 That's the top 10% with an index above 80%. 00:01:59.280 --> 00:02:05.439 And the pivoting middle, those are the guys with indexes between 50 and 79. 00:02:05.599 --> 00:02:08.000 And finally, there are the strivers. 00:02:08.159 --> 00:02:14.560 That's the bottom 20% with an index below 49, facing extreme financial pressure. 00:02:14.719 --> 00:02:16.479 This is not a credit score. 00:02:16.639 --> 00:02:23.759 This is a composite of credit, debt, income, capacity, and assets built from real household data. 00:02:23.919 --> 00:02:26.400 And here's what happened in Q1 of 26. 00:02:26.639 --> 00:02:34.879 The data proves we are extending a brutal K-shaped spectrum, and everyone is moving to the extremes. 00:02:35.120 --> 00:02:41.360 In Q126, the high resilience thriver segment shrank by exactly 5%. 00:02:42.080 --> 00:02:48.479 The population facing severe economic pressure, the strivers expanded by 2%. 00:02:48.960 --> 00:02:54.400 And the traditional pivoting middle class posted a net change of exactly 0%. 00:02:55.599 --> 00:02:59.199 Look, I want you to really sit with that for just a second. 00:02:59.360 --> 00:03:09.439 The top tier is shrinking, the bottom tier is growing, and the middle is not moving, which means people are not climbing up, they are falling down. 00:03:09.599 --> 00:03:13.199 And they've been doing it for six straight quarters. 00:03:13.520 --> 00:03:17.360 I know you know what I'm talking about, no matter which tier you fit in. 00:03:17.520 --> 00:03:22.719 Before we get deeper into this, if you like this type of content, please click like and don't forget to subscribe. 00:03:22.800 --> 00:03:25.039 It's really important to be in the know about this stuff. 00:03:25.120 --> 00:03:27.280 And this is exactly how you do it. 00:03:27.520 --> 00:03:31.919 Now, here is where this gets into shadow data territory. 00:03:32.080 --> 00:03:39.199 You know I love the shadow data because Equifax did something most macro analysts are completely ignoring. 00:03:39.360 --> 00:03:46.319 They looked at six quarters of data running from the third quarter of 24 through the first quarter of 2026. 00:03:46.479 --> 00:03:50.159 And they tracked exactly where the middle class is going. 00:03:50.479 --> 00:03:54.879 And what they found is devastating in its precision. 00:03:59.439 --> 00:04:08.319 That is the percentage of households leaving the middle class and moving into the striver tier that share one defining characteristic. 00:04:08.560 --> 00:04:17.759 They hold under$100,000 in investable assets, not income, not debt level, not credit score, assets. 00:04:18.000 --> 00:04:20.399 The firewall is assets. 00:04:20.720 --> 00:04:22.160 Think about what that means. 00:04:22.319 --> 00:04:44.720 It means that in an economy where credit card rates are running north of 20%, which is, I'm pretty sure, a record, where rents have not come down, where groceries costs what they cost right now, the single biggest predictor of whether you fall out of the middle class is whether you have a financial cushion to absorb the shocks that life keeps throwing at you. 00:04:44.879 --> 00:04:54.399 And if that cushion is under$100,000, according to six quarters of Equifax data, the odds are stacked heavily against you. 00:04:54.639 --> 00:05:06.079 Conversely, more than two-thirds of households successfully climbing into the Thriver tier came from the segment with over$1 million in assets. 00:05:06.240 --> 00:05:07.920 This is not a story about income. 00:05:08.079 --> 00:05:15.040 This is a story about wealth compounding wealth and the absence of wealth compounding vulnerability. 00:05:15.199 --> 00:05:23.759 And if you think this is just a story about financially irresponsible people ruining their credit, you're dead wrong. 00:05:24.000 --> 00:05:27.360 Here's the ultimate truth bomb hiding in this report. 00:05:27.680 --> 00:05:39.519 Over 13% of financially struck strivers and 41% of the pivoting middle maintain elite credit scores above 780. 00:05:40.319 --> 00:05:45.519 You can read that again if you want, but they are the ones that are deeply responsible. 00:05:45.680 --> 00:05:52.879 They pay their bills on time, but a credit score is not a wealth shield because they lack liquid emergency assets. 00:05:53.040 --> 00:05:56.480 The math is cracking them wide open anyway. 00:05:56.560 --> 00:06:00.160 And the generational data makes this even sharper. 00:06:00.480 --> 00:06:09.040 Millennials drop to an average market pulse index of 58.1 and Q1 of 26, a 1.2% quarterly decline. 00:06:09.199 --> 00:06:19.920 They lead all generations in significant index decreases, with nearly 13% experiencing sharp downward movement in a single quarter. 00:06:20.160 --> 00:06:35.360 Because of this specific asset drain, millennials now officially represent the single largest generational portion of the striver population at 7.59%, driven primarily by, well, a lack of assets. 00:06:35.600 --> 00:06:35.920 Why? 00:06:36.160 --> 00:06:50.720 Because they are in their peak debt years: mortgages, student loans, childcare, and they're navigating all of it without the generational wealth safety net that cushioned prior cohorts at the same life stage. 00:06:50.879 --> 00:06:54.160 Prior generations could lean on family assets as a buffer. 00:06:54.399 --> 00:06:56.720 Millennials, in large part, cannot. 00:06:56.879 --> 00:07:00.560 They are on the tightrope and the net is pretty much gone. 00:07:00.800 --> 00:07:05.519 Now, here's the question I want you to ask your financial advisor. 00:07:05.680 --> 00:07:12.800 How much of the revenue growth in your equity portfolio is dependent on the pivoting middle actually having money to spend? 00:07:12.959 --> 00:07:21.439 Because if the answer is a lot, and for most broad market funds it is, you need to understand what this data is exactly telling you. 00:07:21.680 --> 00:07:23.759 So, what does this mean for your money? 00:07:23.920 --> 00:07:28.160 Well, here's the implication that the stock market is not quite pricing in yet. 00:07:28.319 --> 00:07:33.040 Consumption, and you know this, is approximately 70% of US GDP. 00:07:33.199 --> 00:07:34.319 I tell you this all the time. 00:07:34.560 --> 00:07:37.040 Markets are priced for consumer resilience. 00:07:37.199 --> 00:07:54.879 But that, but what the Equifax data tells us right now is that the consumer base is rapidly polarizing, with the spenders at the top representing a shrinking slice of the population, and the broad middle losing ground quarter after quarter. 00:07:55.120 --> 00:08:01.439 Broad market equity valuations are built on an assumption of widespread consumer demand. 00:08:01.680 --> 00:08:04.879 That assumption is getting hollower by the quarter. 00:08:05.040 --> 00:08:16.240 The companies that serve the middle, retail, restaurants, autos, consumer discretionary, are exposed to a customer base that is quietly running out of cushion at the moment. 00:08:16.399 --> 00:08:22.560 And when the cushion's gone, the spending actually stops, not gradually, abruptly. 00:08:22.720 --> 00:08:33.840 The stock market loves averages, average spending, average income, average consumer health, but the Equifax data shows that averages are lying to you right now. 00:08:34.000 --> 00:08:40.080 The average hides a very significant bifurcation that is actually accelerating. 00:08:40.240 --> 00:08:47.519 And when the bottom tier expands fast enough and the middle finally cracks, well, those averages break all at once. 00:08:47.679 --> 00:08:51.600 That is not a slow leak, that is a structural fracture. 00:08:51.759 --> 00:08:57.919 And history tells us that markets do not price that in until they're already sitting inside it. 00:08:58.159 --> 00:09:00.399 So, what should you be watching at the moment? 00:09:00.559 --> 00:09:03.759 Well, keep your eye on consumer discretionary earnings. 00:09:03.919 --> 00:09:08.720 Watch credit card delinquency rates, particularly in the Millennial and Gen X cohorts. 00:09:08.879 --> 00:09:17.679 Remember, next week we start to get earnings and the banks come out first, and they will very much talk to you about credit cards and credit card delinquency rates. 00:09:17.840 --> 00:09:30.799 And watch what happens when the pivoting middle, you know, that 70% that is currently holding flat starts to move because based on six quarters of Equifax trend data, the direction they're moving in is not up. 00:09:31.120 --> 00:09:38.639 So your truth bomb for today is this Equifax just proved that the US consumer is not one story, it is three. 00:09:38.720 --> 00:09:49.919 And the story the stock market is betting on belongs to the 10% that is shrinking, not the 70% that is stalling or the 20% that is falling. 00:09:50.159 --> 00:09:54.960 Join me every day for Wall Street Truth Bombs, where I drop them all right here before the market figures them out.

structure.rationale: Clear throughline from data presentation to generational breakdown to investment implications, with a deliberate 'truth bomb' payoff at the close, though a mid-episode subscribe prompt briefly interrupts the flow.

relevance.rationale: This is financial markets and consumer economics analysis — substantive and data-driven, but fundamentally a personal finance/investing show, not a News/Politics episode; the K-shaped economy framing has no direct policy, legislative, or political hook.

clipability.rationale: Several self-contained, punchy moments — particularly the assets-as-firewall argument and the credit score paradox ('you can have a 780 and still be cracking') — work well as standalone previews without requiring much setup.

card.tagline: Equifax data reveals the middle class isn't stalling — it's quietly collapsing
card.description: Six quarters of Equifax household data show the pivoting middle going nowhere while the bottom tier swells — and the stock market hasn't priced it in yet.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down data the broad market is currently ignoring

transcript.scoringWindow: Its Storytime with Mitch Jesserich. Today, two short German folk tales written down by the Grimm Brothers. The Grimm fairy tales are arguably the most well known folk stories in the Western world. Disney has popularized many of them with such films as Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs, and Cinderella. We were told many of the stories as kids like Little Red Riding Hood, Hansel and Gretel, and Rumpel Stillskin. The Grimm Brothers, Jakab and Wilhelm, set out in the early eighteen hundreds to collect and write down oral stories in the German language. The writing down of oral stories is what a fairy tale is. The brothers, who were academics, saw how industrialization was changing the world, and they feared the loss of these stories. So they talked to many, many people, mostly women, and ended up collecting hundreds of tells. Today the stories are seen as innocent tells meant for children. Disney played a big role in that, but so did the brothers themselves, who revised the stories many times over the years to make them more socially acceptable. But with the first publication of these tells in eighteen twelve, the original stories they were told were much darker and stranger than the ones popularized later on. Today, I read for you two short tells. The first, a ghost story, connected to the high rate of infant mortality of the day. Its called the Stolen Pennies. The second one is shockingly gruesome and shows these werent necessarily for kids. These are oral stories that was the media of their day and meant for everyone. The stolen pennies. One day a father was sitting at the table with his wife and children, and a good friend who was visiting him, and they were having their noonday meal. As they were sitting there, the clock struck twelve, and the visitor saw the door open, and a pale child dressed in snow white clothes entered. He didnt look around or say anything, but went silently into the next room. Shortly thereafter he returned and went away just as quietly as he had entered. On the second and third day the child came again. Finally, the visitor asked the father who the beautiful child was that entered the room every day at noon. The father answered that he knew nothing about him. He hadnt seen anything. The next day, as the clock struck noon, the child entered again, and the visitor pointed the child out to the father. But he didnt see the boy. Neither did the mother nor the children. The visitor stood up, went to the door, opened it a little, and looked inside. There he saw the pale child sitting on the floor, digging and rummaging in the cracks of the boards. However, as soon as the child noticed the visitor, he disappeared. Now the visitor told the family what he had seen and gave an exact description of the boy. The mother was then able to recognize the child and said Alas, its my own dear child who died four weeks ago. Then they ripped up the boards on the floor and found two pennies that the boy had received from his mother at one time to give to a poor man, but the child had thought, You can buy yourself a biscuit for that. Therefore, he had kept the pennies and had hidden them in the cracks of the floor. This is why he hadnt been able to rest in his grave, and had come back every day at noon to look for the pennies. So the parents gave the money to a poor man, and after that the little child was never seen again. The second tell is part two of a story called How Some Children Played at Slaughtering. There once was a father who slaughtered a pig, and his children saw that. In the afternoon, when they began playing, one child said to the other, You be the little pig, and Ill be the butcher. He then took a shiny knife and slit his little brothers throat. Their mother was upstairs in a room bathing another child, and when she heard the cries of her son, she immediately ran downstairs. Upon seeing what had happened, she took the knife out of her sons throat and was so enraged that she stabbed the heart of the other boy, who had been playing the butcher. Then she quickly ran back to the room to tend to her child in the bathtub, but while she had been gone, he had drowned in the tub. Now the woman became so frightened and desperate that she wouldnt allow the neighbors to comfort her, and finally hung herself. When her husband came back from the fields and saw everything, he became so despondent that he died soon thereafter. Two Tales from the Grim Brothers. And that does it for story time. Until next time, be well.

structure.rationale: The episode has a clear intro contextualizing the Grimm Brothers, followed by two sequentially presented stories with a brief close, though pacing is entirely linear with minimal signposting between sections.

relevance.rationale: This episode is a reading of 19th-century German folk tales with no connection to current news or political events.

clipability.rationale: The gruesome second tale has shock value as a standalone moment, but both stories require the framing introduction to land properly and neither excerpt would function as a compelling news/politics preview.

card.tagline: Two dark Grimm tales read aloud — before Disney sanitized them
card.description: Mitch Jeserich reads the original, unrevised 1812 Grimm Brothers stories: a ghost haunting his own pennies and a shockingly violent children's game gone wrong.
card.reason: Because you enjoy narrative storytelling rooted in historical cultural context

past week, leaving corn usage just below the pace needed to meet USCE's current forecast. 00:01:16.640 --> 00:01:20.239 It's only one week, so we're not overly concerned, but it is something to keep an eye on. 00:01:20.400 --> 00:01:27.760 In our opinion, today's move was mostly technical after rallying nearly 40 cents from last week's low, both September and December futures ran into chart resistance. 00:01:27.920 --> 00:01:36.799 December futures stalled almost exactly at the 50% retracement of the recent break near 466, with additional resistance waiting between 468 and 473. 00:01:37.040 --> 00:01:38.400 A setback wasn't surprising. 00:01:38.560 --> 00:01:44.000 What was a little more disappointing was how hard the market sold off into the close, leaving a daily key reversal on the chart. 00:01:44.159 --> 00:01:47.920 That doesn't automatically mean the rally is over, but it does put the bulls on notice. 00:01:48.079 --> 00:01:55.519 Adding to that, open interest declined during this week's rally, suggesting much of the buying came from traders covering short positions rather than fresh money entering the market. 00:01:55.680 --> 00:01:57.760 Corn is trading its own fundamentals at the moment. 00:01:57.840 --> 00:01:59.120 Weather and demand are key. 00:01:59.280 --> 00:02:02.480 September corn futures lost eight and three quarter cents to close at 435. 00:02:02.640 --> 00:02:06.319 December futures gave back eight cents to finish at 456 and a quarter. 00:02:06.560 --> 00:02:13.199 Soybean futures stretched to fresh highs for the move before backing away into the close as traders took profits after a sharp two-day rally. 00:02:13.360 --> 00:02:18.560 The Chinese flash sale was supportive, but not surprising, which much of the bullish news already priced into the market. 00:02:19.360 --> 00:02:22.240 Despite today's turn lower, the technical picture remains constructive. 00:02:22.400 --> 00:02:32.240 Yesterday's open interest showed soybean open interest increasing by nearly 19,500 contracts, suggesting fresh money continues to enter the market rather than simply covering existing positions. 00:02:32.400 --> 00:02:33.280 That's a constructive sign. 00:02:33.439 --> 00:02:37.840 Soybeans have a clear demand story through the soy oil market, which is providing solid support. 00:02:38.000 --> 00:02:41.360 August soybeans closed virtually unchanged at$1,193.25. 00:02:41.520 --> 00:02:45.439 November futures settled at$1,192.5 cents. 00:02:45.680 --> 00:02:51.039 Wheat futures continued to work higher early in the session, testing key technical resistance before backing off into the close. 00:02:51.199 --> 00:02:53.599 September Chicago reached its 50-day moving average. 00:02:53.759 --> 00:02:58.080 Kansas City tested the mid-June highs, and Minneapolis challenged its 200-day moving average. 00:02:58.479 --> 00:03:02.879 None of those levels were able to hold today, but all three exchanges remain within striking distance. 00:03:03.039 --> 00:03:06.159 Friday's USDA was a report could provide the next catalyst. 00:03:06.319 --> 00:03:15.520 After last week's surprise reduction in wheat acreage, the USDA will likely trim production estimates that would give wheat another fundamental boost, but the market still needs export demand to follow through. 00:03:15.680 --> 00:03:18.080 A softer US dollar could certainly help improve U.S. 00:03:18.240 --> 00:03:18.960 competitiveness. 00:03:19.360 --> 00:03:23.840 For now, wheat still feels like a market trying to decide whether this rally is more than just short covering. 00:03:24.000 --> 00:03:26.719 The funds have been heavily short, so it bounced isn't surprising. 00:03:26.879 --> 00:03:33.199 Now the market needs stronger demand, another production concern, or a supportive USTA report to convince buyers to push through resistance. 00:03:33.360 --> 00:03:37.120 September Chicago futures last 10.3 quarter cents at 6.07 and 3 quarters. 00:03:37.280 --> 00:03:41.599 September Kansas City futures finished at 6.45 and a quarter down seven and a half cents. 00:03:41.840 --> 00:03:46.159 September Minneapolis futures settled at 6.30 and three quarter down two and a quarter cents. 00:03:46.240 --> 00:03:50.639 The cattle complex continues to show signs of weakness, prompting many to ask what has changed. 00:03:50.719 --> 00:03:51.919 Part of the answer is seasonal. 00:03:52.080 --> 00:03:57.599 Summer beef demand typically slows after the Independence Day holiday, with the next major grilling holiday still months away. 00:03:57.680 --> 00:04:01.759 As temperature rises, beef demand normally eases, but seasonality is only part of the story. 00:04:01.919 --> 00:04:05.439 Heavier carcass weights and rising beef imports are also weighing on the market. 00:04:05.599 --> 00:04:11.280 Last week, cattle averaged 1455 pounds compared with 1410 pounds a year ago. 00:04:11.439 --> 00:04:18.000 Those extra 45 pounds per head are adding a significant amount of beef production, partially offsetting the decline in slaughter numbers. 00:04:18.160 --> 00:04:26.720 Processors are also trimming larger cuts more aggressively, selling the excess trim separately while marketing higher value trimmed primals combined with a 10% increase in beef imports. 00:04:26.879 --> 00:04:29.360 These factors are helping boost available beef supplies. 00:04:29.439 --> 00:04:32.319 The result has been a notable shift in boxed beef market. 00:04:32.399 --> 00:04:37.920 On July 6th, Choice Box Beef was$390.98 and Select was$377.53. 00:04:38.000 --> 00:04:43.759 One year later, Choice has slipped to a$386.48, while Select had fallen to$365.87. 00:04:43.920 --> 00:04:49.439 The Choice Select spread has also widened dramatically, increasing from$9.99 a year ago to$2061 today. 00:04:49.519 --> 00:04:56.000 That suggests demand for premium beef has remained relatively resilient while lower quality beef has faced greater price pressure. 00:04:56.160 --> 00:05:03.839 The CME box beef index tells the same story, declining from$392.16 on July 6, 2025 to$386.35 this year. 00:05:04.000 --> 00:05:08.319 Despite historically tight cattle supplies and lower slatter levels, box beef values have softened. 00:05:08.480 --> 00:05:14.399 If this trend continues, it could eventually pressure cash cattle prices and further squeeze already thin packer margins. 00:05:14.560 --> 00:05:17.920 This will be an important trend to monitor as the market moves through the remainder of the summer. 00:05:18.079 --> 00:05:24.959 August cattle closed down 80 cents, closing at 237.62 and a half, and December was off 35 cents, closing at 233.52.5. 00:05:25.279 --> 00:05:28.480 August feeders were up$1.40, closing at$3.62.05. 00:05:28.959 --> 00:05:32.480 And October was up$85, closing at$3.54.62.5. 00:05:32.720 --> 00:05:38.879 The Hog Bulls responded in an impressive fashion today, with October futures gapping higher at the open and never looking back. 00:05:39.040 --> 00:05:41.439 By the close, the contract had posted a gain of more than$3. 00:05:41.920 --> 00:05:46.879 The next challenge for the Bulls is to prove this was the beginning of a sustained move higher rather than just a one-day event. 00:05:47.040 --> 00:05:49.120 There was no major news catalyst behind this rally. 00:05:49.199 --> 00:05:53.279 Instead, the market found support from stronger cash hog prices and firm report product values. 00:05:53.360 --> 00:05:57.439 That combination was enough to attract buyers with most contracts posting new 30-day highs. 00:05:57.519 --> 00:06:02.160 For now, the June low is looking more and more like a seasonal low if the market follows its typical trading pattern. 00:06:02.240 --> 00:06:04.399 This breakout could develop into a move of roughly$10. 00:06:04.959 --> 00:06:12.079 That would place October Lean Hogs near the$90 level, which we discussed in yesterday's comments as a realistic upside target under improving market conditions. 00:06:12.240 --> 00:06:20.160 August hogs were up$2.72.5 cents, closing at$99.65, and October was up$3.87.5, closing at$85.52.5. 00:06:20.480 --> 00:06:21.839 This concludes today's commentary. 00:06:21.920 --> 00:06:24.800 We hope everyone has a great evening, and we'll talk to you tomorrow.

structure.rationale: Well-organized commodity-by-commodity rundown with a consistent format of context, technical analysis, and closing prices for each market, though transitions between segments are formulaic rather than deliberate.

relevance.rationale: This is agricultural commodities market analysis covering corn, soybeans, wheat, cattle, and hogs — financial/markets content with no substantive political or news policy dimension.

clipability.rationale: Price-specific and technically dense language makes most moments hard to use standalone; the cattle carcass weight comparison and the hog breakout target near $90 are the most self-contained moments but still require commodity context.

card.tagline: Corn ignores crude oil while cattle face a post-holiday demand hangover
card.description: Sam breaks down Wednesday's technical pullbacks in grains, a widening Choice-Select beef spread, and a surprise $3 surge in October lean hog futures.

scored.description: The Quantum AI Digest is your daily gateway to the fast-evolving world of artificial intelligence, delivering fresh episodes packed with the latest AI industry news. Each day, we curate the most significant advancements from the past 24 hours, offering a vibrant blend of breakthroughs, policies, and trends shaping the AI landscape. From pioneering research to transformative applications, our podcast distills complex updates into clear, engaging summaries for tech enthusiasts, developers, and curious minds.

latestEpisode.title: The Quantum AI Digest: 8 July 2026 Daily Dispatch

transcript.scoringWindow: WEBVTT 00:00:00.000 --> 00:00:08.560 In the fast-paced world of artificial intelligence, a wave of significant developments is reshaping the industry, regulation, and business. 00:00:08.880 --> 00:00:25.280 Kicking off with industry news, Meta has made a major leap by launching Muse Image, a free AI image generator, now available across Meta AI, Instagram stories, WhatsApp, and soon to be integrated into Facebook and Messenger. 00:00:25.519 --> 00:00:43.359 This new model, developed by Superintelligence Labs, lets users create and edit images within apps, interact socially through features like photo uploads and friend mentions, and perform prompt-based edits such as removing distractions or generating QR codes. 00:00:43.600 --> 00:00:50.640 Muse Image is already ranked as Arena's number two text-to-image model, just behind GPT Image 2. 00:00:50.880 --> 00:00:59.679 Alongside, Muse Video, a new AI-powered video generator, is in live preview and ranks third on the Arena leaderboard. 00:00:59.759 --> 00:01:12.319 These launches mark Meta's shift from using third-party creative AI to building its own proprietary multimodal generative tools, reinforcing the company's position in both consumer and business segments. 00:01:12.560 --> 00:01:26.640 Meanwhile, Microsoft is accelerating its move toward in-house AI by phasing out models from OpenAI and Anthropic in products like Excel, Outlook, and Copilot, replacing them with proprietary MAI models. 00:01:26.799 --> 00:01:40.159 Led by Mustafa Suleiman, this strategy aims to reduce costs and dependency on outside providers, aligning with a broader industry movement toward good enough models over the most powerful, resource-intensive solutions. 00:01:40.400 --> 00:01:50.079 The transition accompanies Microsoft's restructuring and workforce cuts, with over 4,800 employees affected, especially in the Xbox division. 00:01:50.239 --> 00:02:03.200 On the research front, Anthropic revealed a major breakthrough with JSpace, an internal workspace within its CLOD model that enables complex multi-step reasoning, similar to a notepad for internal thought. 00:02:03.359 --> 00:02:09.759 This emergent capability wasn't explicitly programmed and plays a critical role in handling challenging queries. 00:02:10.000 --> 00:02:24.479 Anthropic is also broadening access to its CLOD co-work platform for persistent cross-device AI collaboration and has globally relaunched its Fable V model with enhanced content moderation following regulatory discussions. 00:02:24.719 --> 00:02:42.159 Turning to the dynamic Chinese AI sector, DeepSeek has begun developing its own AI silicon, marking an important path toward hardware self-sufficiency amid ongoing export controls and signaling a broader trend of custom chip design among Chinese and American labs alike. 00:02:42.400 --> 00:02:54.719 Tencent is making waves with the open sourcing of its high-three language model, while Naichuan has announced Wangcat 2, a coding model with a trillion parameters, both trained entirely on domestic chips. 00:02:54.960 --> 00:03:02.000 These moves reflect China's rapidly growing capability to build competitive AI systems independent of US hardware. 00:03:02.159 --> 00:03:09.919 OpenAI remains in the spotlight, preparing to release new models, GPT 5.6 Sol, its flagship. 00:03:11.039 --> 00:03:14.400 The AI industry also saw notable corporate maneuvers. 00:03:14.639 --> 00:03:29.759 Figma, Aqua hired AI app builder BUD, enhancing its offering in agent-driven design, while TerraWolf signed a$19 billion data center lease with Anthropic, underscoring escalating investment in AI infrastructure. 00:03:30.080 --> 00:03:45.360 Apple and Broadcom extended their silicon partnership to target advanced servers, and Samsung posted a record$58 billion quarterly profit, even outpacing NVIDIA and Apple, though chip sector sell-offs hit its stock. 00:03:45.680 --> 00:03:56.240 Nvidia, meanwhile, has postponed its Kyber AI server launch to 2028 due to manufacturing hurdles, revealing ongoing constraints in AI hardware supply. 00:03:56.479 --> 00:03:59.120 Job market dynamics remain mixed. 00:03:59.360 --> 00:04:14.800 While top executives increasingly frame AI as a tool for productivity and not job elimination, layoffs tied to automation continue, with Microsoft and Xbox set for more than 3,000 reductions by fiscal year 2027. 00:04:15.280 --> 00:04:21.839 Other highlights include Softbank entering the AI GPU market to ease infrastructure bottlenecks. 00:04:22.319 --> 00:04:32.160 Elon Musk's XAI, now SpaceXAI, is planning AI compute satellite launches after a$1.77 trillion IPO. 00:04:32.399 --> 00:04:45.600 ByteDance and Alibaba have restricted AI companion features for miners to comply with Beijing's new guidelines, and new unicorns like Even Reality's technology are emerging in the AI-driven smart glasses space. 00:04:45.759 --> 00:04:55.920 On the open source front, QT, General Intuition, and Epic Games have unveiled Mira, a neural network generated, fully playable rocket lead game. 00:04:56.160 --> 00:05:11.360 Shifting to regulatory and policy news, China is considering new rules to curb overseas access to its AI models, and has enacted regulations preventing chatbots for miners from encouraging extreme emotions or fostering dependency. 00:05:11.600 --> 00:05:16.240 These steps are in response to both domestic concerns and mirrored by U.S. 00:05:16.399 --> 00:05:17.199 restrictions. 00:05:17.439 --> 00:05:29.360 In the United States, Illinois is leading with the first state level Artificial Intelligence Safety Measures Act, mandating rigorous risk frameworks and annual audits for major AI developers. 00:05:29.600 --> 00:05:35.279 Fines can range from one to three million dollars per offense, with broad industry support. 00:05:35.519 --> 00:05:50.720 Meanwhile, Brazil has passed a pioneering digital statute to protect minors, requiring robust legal compliance, privacy by default, and transparency from digital platforms or risk penalties of up to ten percent of local revenue. 00:05:50.959 --> 00:06:07.759 In the European Union, all new cars must now have driver monitoring cameras, raising privacy issues, and Google's new privacy policy will use user uploaded images and voice data for AI training unless users opt out. 00:06:08.000 --> 00:06:11.199 Cooperation between labs and governments is intensifying. 00:06:11.439 --> 00:06:23.600 Anthropics relaunch of Fable 5 came after direct talks with US authorities while the company collaborates with Amazon, Microsoft, and Google to standardize measures against AI jailbreaks. 00:06:24.480 --> 00:06:37.279 OpenAI's Sam Altman is informally proposing a 5% US government stake in leading AI labs, including his own, in response to increasing questions about AI's value distribution. 00:06:37.439 --> 00:06:50.160 On the startup and product landscape, Norm AI, a legal AI platform charging only for successful outcomes, has reached unicorn status following a$120 million series C raise. 00:06:50.560 --> 00:07:02.160 Other major investments include$2 billion for Thrive Holdings,$100 million for video intelligence startup 12 labs, and$150 million for Even Realities Technology. 00:07:02.319 --> 00:07:03.360 Launches abound. 00:07:03.600 --> 00:07:20.399 Venna's Muse Suite, Anthropics Upgraded AI platforms, Mini Max M3's multimodal assistant, and practical business tools like ChatGPT for PowerPoint, Scandit's barcode skills for agents, and a host of new developer and compliance products. 00:07:20.560 --> 00:07:31.680 The ecosystem now includes open source workflow builders, code auditing tools, AI-enabled presentation designers, and diverse offerings for security, productivity, and analytics. 00:07:32.160 --> 00:07:48.959 Emerging strategic trends show a shift away from reliance on partner labs, with giants like Microsoft and Meta prioritizing homegrown models for better control and cost effectiveness, while open source solutions are causing downward pressure on pricing models across the industry. 00:07:49.199 --> 00:07:59.279 The rapid fragmentation of the AI landscape is making it harder for developers and companies to plan for the future as each player pursues its own independent strategy. 00:07:59.519 --> 00:08:11.439 Additionally, the labor market is grappling with both productivity boosts and significant burnout as new roles emerge for so-called agentic engineers who leverage AI workflows at scale. 00:08:11.759 --> 00:08:21.120 Persistent AI agents, both software and hardware, are now running around the clock, redefining business automation and operational continuity. 00:08:21.279 --> 00:08:29.120 Venture investment remains strong, but with increased emphasis on building lasting infrastructure and sustainable business models. 00:08:29.279 --> 00:08:42.080 In research and academia, key advances include Anthropics J space discovery, aligning AI cognition more closely with neuroscience, and enabling more robust problem solving capabilities. 00:08:42.320 --> 00:08:57.840 New benchmarking tools such as Dashbench and PACE are dramatically reducing the cost of evaluating models and agent workflows, while advances in hardest engineering, memory, and anti-Doom training are addressing technical limitations like repetitive output. 00:08:58.080 --> 00:09:06.960 Infrastructure milestones include distributed training breakthroughs and the world's first commercial nuclear-powered satellite for orbiting AI compute. 00:09:07.279 --> 00:09:10.159 Courtesy of SpaceX and City Labs. 00:09:10.399 --> 00:09:18.879 Across regions, China is asserting itself through regulatory controls, open source innovations, and domestic chip investment. 00:09:19.120 --> 00:09:28.159 The United States stands out for regulatory leadership and major infrastructure plans, with SpaceX AI aiming for AI satellites. 00:09:28.399 --> 00:09:32.159 Brazil's new child safety statute is setting global standards. 00:09:32.399 --> 00:09:38.320 Meanwhile, Europe mandates facial monitoring in all cars, prompting privacy debates. 00:09:38.480 --> 00:09:40.080 A few cautionary notes. 00:09:48.720 --> 00:09:59.679 And according to the AI at work 2026 report, nine out of ten organizations deploy AI on unaudited data, but only 2% actually trust the decisions these systems make. 00:09:59.840 --> 00:10:04.720 The primary obstacles remain organizational and procedural, not technical. 00:10:04.960 --> 00:10:10.720 Businesses are advised to rigorously audit data and measure returns before rolling out AI at scale. 00:10:10.960 --> 00:10:24.639 Finally, on the cultural front, the speed of AI advancement

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear sectioned format moving through industry, regulation, startups, and research, but the rapid-fire bulletin style creates uneven pacing with little connective tissue between items.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers AI industry and tech business news with some regulatory and policy angles, but its core subject matter is technology/industry rather than News/Politics — the policy segments are secondary to product launches and corporate moves.

clipability.rationale: Most segments are dense lists of product names and funding figures that require surrounding context to land, though the Sam Altman 5% government stake proposal and the '90% of orgs deploy AI on unaudited data' stat offer isolated usable moments.

card.tagline: Meta, Microsoft, and China are all ditching outside AI — here's why
card.description: A rapid daily dispatch covering Meta's Muse launch, Microsoft's OpenAI phase-out, DeepSeek's chip push, and Illinois's first state AI safety law.

structure.rationale: The conversation follows a loose but identifiable throughline analyzing characters and themes, though it meanders with casual asides and lacks tight segmentation.

relevance.rationale: This episode is entirely about analyzing the characters and story of the Broadway musical 'The Music Man,' with no connection to current news or political events.

clipability.rationale: A few quotable moments exist — the 'attractive salesmen get away with shitty things' riff has standalone energy — but most exchanges require context about the show and prior conversation to land.

card.tagline: Is Harold Hill actually a bad person, or just a charming con man?
card.description: The BSB Podcast digs into the moral ambiguity of 'The Music Man,' debating whether Harold Hill, Marian, and the whole cast are villains, heroes, or just gloriously gray.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down the morality and romance of a classic Broadway show

structure.rationale: The excerpt meanders through state fair food chat, event promotion, and a caller segment with loose transitions and no clear signposting — conversational radio format with minimal structural discipline.

relevance.rationale: This episode is a community radio talk show focused on state fair food, local events, and Indiana Black Expo — lifestyle and community content with only a passing, incidental mention of Senator Warnock as a guest speaker, not substantive political coverage.

clipability.rationale: The conversation is highly context-dependent, built on in-group banter and local references that would be largely opaque to a cold listener without setup.

card.tagline: State fair food, Indiana Black Expo, and Senator Warnock coming to Indy
card.description: Tina Cosby and guests preview the Indiana Black Expo Summer Celebration, including a keynote from Senator Raphael Warnock, while swapping state fair food tips.
card.reason: Because this episode covers Indianapolis community events happening this week

scored.description:
Tired of the mainstream media? You’re not alone.
Hosted by Dr. Steve Turley—best-selling author, political analyst, and the "Patriot Professor"—the Turley Talks Podcast delivers bold, faith-filled commentary on the biggest stories shaping politics and culture. From the rise of nationalism and populism to breaking news and election updates, this daily podcast is your front-row seat to the conservative revolution reshaping America and the world.
You'll love Dr. Steve’s hopeful, unapologetically pro-America first perspective. Learn how to stand strong for faith, family, and freedom—and discover how patriots like you are building a new conservative age.
Subscribe now and join the movement.

structure.rationale: The episode moves through a loose list of grievances and encouragements without clear segment breaks or signposting, relying on the host's stream-of-consciousness progression rather than deliberate structural architecture.

relevance.rationale: The episode engages directly with specific current political developments — SCOTUS rulings on birthright citizenship, mail-in ballots, and transgender athletes in sports — giving it substantive news hooks, though the framing is heavily ideological commentary rather than reportorial analysis.

clipability.rationale: Several punchy declarative moments exist but most require the surrounding ideological context to land as intended, and the most quotable lines carry inflammatory content that limits usability as neutral preview clips.

structure.rationale: The episode jumps between multiple unrelated stories (train conductor, Trump/NATO, book promotion) without clear segmentation or transitions, creating a loose, radio-stream-of-consciousness feel.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers current political flashpoints — a fired train conductor's patriotic remarks, Trump's NATO press conference on Iran, and a 'Black English' curriculum controversy — giving it clear news/politics relevance with specific timely hooks.

clipability.rationale: The train conductor firing segment has a few punchy standalone moments, but most exchanges require setup and the book promotion and call-in segments dilute clip density significantly.

card.tagline: Train conductor fired for saying America is the greatest country on Earth
card.description: Todd Starnes defends a Chattanooga railway worker fired after telling passengers America is the greatest nation, while previewing Trump's fiery NATO press conference on Iran.
card.reason: Because this episode covers a viral free-speech firing and Trump's NATO remarks

scored.description:
WagerTalk is a daily sports betting podcast directly from Vegas that prepares our audience to make the most educated bets possible. Listen to Vegas experts cover the National Football League (NFL), College Football, Major League Baseball (MLB), National Basketball Association (NBA), College Basketball, National Hockey League (NHL), Soccer, Mixed Martial Arts (MMA), Horse Racing and NASCAR from a Las Vegas betting perspective. Our panel of professional handicappers provides free sports picks, sports predictions, and betting odds covering all major sporting events to help you beat the spread and make money wagering sports.

Make sure to check out our handicappers at WagerTalk.com and follow the WagerTalk crew on Twitter @WagerTalk and Sportsmemo at Sportsmemo.com and @sportsmemo.

structure.rationale: The episode has a clear conversational throughline moving from the LaMelo trade to Celtics/Jalen Brown discussions, with identifiable topic transitions, though pacing is loose and dialogue occasionally rambles.

relevance.rationale: This is NBA trade analysis and basketball operations content — sports subject matter that scores in the 0-20 range regardless of analytical depth or specificity.

clipability.rationale: A few quotable moments exist — particularly the 'verbal pretzel' line about Brad Stevens and the Giannis aging argument — but most exchanges require prior context about the trade rumors to land.

card.tagline: Evan Turner breaks down why the Celtics may be done with Jalen Brown
card.description: SI's NBA crew and Evan Turner dissect the Celtics' aggressive push to trade Jalen Brown after his best season yet, and what four first-round picks says about Boston's direction.
card.reason: Because you follow NBA front office moves and roster shake-ups

scored.description:
Manchester City news, opinions and updates brought to you on The City Social by the Esteemed Kompany YouTube channel. A whole range of guests join Steven to talk all things Man City and football multiple times a week. Blues who have followed their team for years, journalists with expertise knowledge of the goings on around the club, entertaining content creators from varied backgrounds and many more.

From previews, to reactions, transfer news, opposition debates and academy updates, this is a podcast for all Man City fans. If you want to join me and thousands of other blues from across the world, hit that subscribe button to support a lifelong blue!

Follow Esteemed Kompany on various other platforms:

🔗 www.esteemedkompany.com

📹 Youtube.com/esteemedkompany

🎙️ x.com/stevenmcinerney

📸 instagram.com/esteemedkompany

🤳 tiktok.com/@steven_mcinerney

structure.rationale: The hosts work through squad positions methodically with a clear framework (position-by-position roster building), though the conversation meanders and loses thread at points with frequent self-corrections.

relevance.rationale: This is a football squad analysis podcast focused on Manchester City's transfer window and player roles — sports content with no substantive political or news dimension.

clipability.rationale: A few punchy player assessments (the 'good pair of jeans' Kovacic line, the Reinders 'absolute fucker' take) are quotable but most moments require listener familiarity with the squad context to land.

card.tagline: Who stays, who goes? Breaking down Maresca's ideal City squad position by position
card.description: Two City fans debate Gusto vs. Nunes, Rico Lewis's surprise case for staying, and which midfielders Maresca will cut before next season.
card.reason: Because you follow Manchester City's summer rebuild closely

structure.rationale: There's a discernible throughline — deconstructing Cooper's retconning — but the episode meanders through LASIK analogies and free-market digressions before returning to the central argument.

relevance.rationale: The episode is substantively focused on a specific North Carolina Senate race and Roy Cooper's political record, making it clearly on-topic for News/Politics, though it's regional rather than nationally timely.

clipability.rationale: The 'retconning' framing and the listener text about Cooper's eight-year record produce a few self-contained, quotable moments, but most segments require context about North Carolina politics to land.

card.tagline: Roy Cooper is rewriting his record — Pete Kaliner isn't letting him
card.description: Kaliner dissects Cooper's Senate campaign ads, arguing his folksy image masks a progressive record of vetoes and deference to national Democrat donors.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down a key 2026 Senate race and candidate credibility

scored.description: Catch two of British Columbia's most admired and respected sports journalists Monday to Friday on CHEK. Don Taylor and Rick Dhaliwal host Donnie and Dhali - The Team. Follow along with Donnie and Dhali as they interview guests, and have fun discussing the latest in local sports news. Tune in live from 10am to 12pm on CHEK, the CHEK+ app or download the Podcast to listen whenever!

card.tagline: Newfoundland callers debate oil tank rules, insurance headaches, and heat pumps

scored.description: Welcome to C-Suite Perspectives, a podcast series by The Conference Board. Hosted by our President & CEO, Steve Odland, this weekly conversation will take an objective, data-driven look at a range of business topics aimed at executives. Listeners will come away with what The Conference Board does best: insights for what's ahead™.

latestEpisode.title: How Effective Are Today's Boards? We Have the Data

that different. 208 00:08:39,922 --> 00:08:42,702 Steve Odland: Now, at DuPont, you've talked about Scope 1, 2, and 3 209 00:08:42,802 --> 00:08:45,152 emissions, and you're tracking that. 210 00:08:52,132 --> 00:08:55,342 impact each set of carbon emissions? 213 00:08:55,722 --> 00:08:56,002 Scott Collick: Sure. 214 00:08:56,102 --> 00:08:59,772 So Scope 1 would be your direct carbon dioxide or carbon dioxide equivalents. 215 00:08:59,772 --> 00:09:03,542 There's other gases that have a global warming potential. 216 00:09:03,542 --> 00:09:06,212 We convert all those into a carbon dioxide equivalent. 217 00:09:06,472 --> 00:09:07,892 So those are your direct emissions. 218 00:09:08,642 --> 00:09:11,902 Scope 2 is the electricity you use and the emissions from 219 00:09:11,902 --> 00:09:13,402 the electricity that you use. 220 00:09:13,782 --> 00:09:15,862 And then Scope 3 has two components. 221 00:09:15,862 --> 00:09:19,342 Your upstream, so all the raw materials, your purchased goods and 222 00:09:19,342 --> 00:09:21,702 services, your logistics upstream. 223 00:09:22,212 --> 00:09:25,872 And then you also have a downstream Scope 3, which is logistics. 224 00:09:26,162 --> 00:09:29,432 It's how the product is used in use, and it's also end of life, as well 225 00:09:29,432 --> 00:09:30,922 as a variety of other categories. 226 00:09:30,932 --> 00:09:34,792 But it's really, your direct emissions, electricity, and your upstream and 227 00:09:34,792 --> 00:09:36,982 downstream emissions make up all scopes. 228 00:09:37,442 --> 00:09:40,142 Steve Odland: Yeah, and so Scope 1 and 2 seem pretty straightforward. 229 00:09:40,142 --> 00:09:42,202 Scope 1 is in your control. 230 00:09:42,202 --> 00:09:46,548 It may not be easy to reduce, but it's in your control cause it's coming from your 231 00:09:46,548 --> 00:09:48,735 plants or your products or your offices. 232 00:09:49,255 --> 00:09:52,675 Scope 2 is a little harder, but also relatively easy, cause 233 00:09:52,675 --> 00:09:54,405 it's tracked to electricity. 234 00:09:54,475 --> 00:09:55,575 You know what it is. 235 00:09:56,225 --> 00:09:58,265 But Scope 3's hard, Scott, isn't it? 236 00:09:58,265 --> 00:10:01,895 I mean, cause you're dealing with up and down the supply chain, and it's 237 00:10:01,895 --> 00:10:05,635 through your vendors and suppliers, who you don't directly control. 238 00:10:05,635 --> 00:10:09,375 But you can influence it, and you guys have done a great job at that. 239 00:10:09,855 --> 00:10:12,085 Scott Collick: Yeah, so that's actually my biggest headache, so we're 240 00:10:12,085 --> 00:10:13,855 well on our way on our Scope 1 and 2. 241 00:10:13,855 --> 00:10:18,495 We're down 76% on our Scope 1 and 2 emissions since 2019, because 242 00:10:18,495 --> 00:10:20,435 that's been our constant focus. 243 00:10:20,435 --> 00:10:23,705 So much, most of my attention is now on Scope 3. 244 00:10:24,715 --> 00:10:27,255 And it's really our inbound raw materials, to be honest with you. 245 00:10:27,255 --> 00:10:29,865 So, right now, and most companies are doing that, you 246 00:10:29,865 --> 00:10:31,825 start with a spend-based model. 247 00:10:31,845 --> 00:10:34,905 I spend $1 million dollars times some factor, and then 248 00:10:34,905 --> 00:10:36,135 that's your carbon emissions. 249 00:10:36,165 --> 00:10:38,935 Well, the only way that you can lower the emissions is spend less. 250 00:10:39,285 --> 00:10:42,665 So it's really important for companies to switch from spend-based 251 00:10:42,665 --> 00:10:44,465 models to activity-based models. 252 00:10:44,475 --> 00:10:49,765 So I would tell you, we're almost at 60% activity-based model, and 253 00:10:49,765 --> 00:10:52,025 we're at almost 30% primary data. 254 00:10:52,025 --> 00:10:55,255 That's actually where our suppliers give us a product carbon footprint 255 00:10:55,275 --> 00:10:57,645 of the materials that we buy, and that's really important for 256 00:10:57,645 --> 00:10:59,045 the next phase of the journey. 257 00:10:59,285 --> 00:11:02,465 You have to transition from spend-based models to actual 258 00:11:02,585 --> 00:11:06,655 activity-based models to know the carbon of the inbound raw materials. 259 00:11:06,655 --> 00:11:08,905 But it's my biggest headache right now, Steve. 260 00:11:09,295 --> 00:11:13,085 Steve Odland: And this goes back to your point that there has to be carbon fluency. 261 00:11:13,508 --> 00:11:16,218 And that means that you've got to understand what it is, where 262 00:11:16,218 --> 00:11:17,468 it is, you've got to track it. 263 00:11:17,528 --> 00:11:20,448 It's not only in your direct operations, but it's all the way 264 00:11:20,448 --> 00:11:21,948 up Scope Two and Scope Three. 265 00:11:22,258 --> 00:11:23,348 So it's pretty broad here. 266 00:11:23,368 --> 00:11:29,168 Now, you know, you hear a lot about these ESG-style frameworks, or regulatory 267 00:11:29,178 --> 00:11:32,118 frameworks, and there are a lot of these competing ones around the world. 268 00:11:33,140 --> 00:11:36,603 How do you simplify this, Scott, so that it's truly applicable? 269 00:11:36,623 --> 00:11:40,443 Cause if it sounds like rocket science, people will freak out on it, and they 270 00:11:40,443 --> 00:11:42,543 don't really adopt it and make progress. 271 00:11:42,543 --> 00:11:45,553 But I think you've talked about simplifying the framework. 272 00:11:45,563 --> 00:11:46,543 What do you mean by that? 273 00:11:46,543 --> 00:11:48,523 And how do you advise companies to do that? 274 00:11:48,904 --> 00:11:49,274 Scott Collick: Yeah. 275 00:11:49,334 --> 00:11:52,024 So, one, I like to start with business, not the framework. 276 00:11:52,024 --> 00:11:55,963 So, I like to get, at my company, I like the carbon data at a company 277 00:11:55,963 --> 00:11:58,963 level, and then I break it down by business and a product level. 278 00:11:59,314 --> 00:12:01,173 It's got to be how your business runs. 279 00:12:01,173 --> 00:12:02,624 Don't fit to the framework. 280 00:12:02,824 --> 00:12:04,304 Look at it how your business runs. 281 00:12:04,593 --> 00:12:07,924 But I will tell you, the key step that I found is, and this is where most 282 00:12:07,934 --> 00:12:11,334 companies fall short, is I think it's really important to normalize the data, 283 00:12:11,374 --> 00:12:13,673 and turn it into an intensity metric. 284 00:12:14,253 --> 00:12:16,233 Greenhouse gas per million dollars of revenue. 285 00:12:16,253 --> 00:12:18,374 So I'll go back to my iPhone 17 example. 286 00:12:18,863 --> 00:12:23,044 So I was meeting with one of our LCA practitioners, talking about this 287 00:12:23,054 --> 00:12:26,884 iPhone 17, and I said, "Hey, this has 55 kilograms of carbon dioxide." 288 00:12:26,884 --> 00:12:30,490 And she goes, "Wow." And I said, "You know, that actually is 300 pounds 289 00:12:30,490 --> 00:12:34,510 per pound of unit," cause iPhone only weighs about 0.2 kilograms. 290 00:12:34,800 --> 00:12:38,010 She goes, "Wow, that's incredibly high." By the way, that would be 291 00:12:38,010 --> 00:12:41,790 higher than any product that we make at DuPont, 300 kilograms of carbon 292 00:12:41,790 --> 00:12:43,240 dioxide per kilogram of product. 293 00:12:43,640 --> 00:12:47,480 But I said, "You're looking at it wrong. This is incredibly value-added. 294 00:12:47,820 --> 00:12:51,660 If you look at it on an economic intensity, it's only about 70 metric 295 00:12:51,670 --> 00:12:53,190 tons per million dollars of revenue." 296 00:12:54,060 --> 00:12:56,460 And it's really important that you have to look at both lenses. 297 00:12:56,540 --> 00:13:00,500 Engineers look at it, things on a kilogram per kilogram, but financial 298 00:13:00,500 --> 00:13:04,990 people look at things from a metric tons or kilograms per million dollars 299 00:13:04,990 --> 00:13:06,460 of revenue, and I think it's important. 300 00:13:06,500 --> 00:13:09,360 And that's one of the things, is you got to link it much closer 301 00:13:09,360 --> 00:13:10,480 to how you run the business. 302 00:13:10,500 --> 00:13:12,240 We run business in dollars. 303 00:13:13,390 --> 00:13:13,620 Steve Odland: Yeah. 304 00:13:13,650 --> 00:13:16,340 Scott Collick: And then I like to look at every one of our businesses 305 00:13:16,370 --> 00:13:17,990 on all these intensity metrics. 306 00:13:18,040 --> 00:13:19,020 And it normalizes. 307 00:13:19,030 --> 00:13:22,330 Not all our businesses and not all companies are the same size revenue. 308 00:13:22,330 --> 00:13:25,946 So I think that's really important to add onto the frameworks, to start looking 309 00:13:25,946 --> 00:13:28,546 at things on an economic-intensity basis. 310 00:13:29,026 --> 00:13:31,756 Steve Odland: Yeah, and it does allow comparability too, across 311 00:13:31,756 --> 00:13:36,066 organizations, both kilogram for kilogram, but also kilograms per dollar. 312 00:13:36,096 --> 00:13:37,476 It's really an important point. 313 00:13:37,516 --> 00:13:37,726 Scott Collick: Yeah. 314 00:13:38,406 --> 00:13:42,356 Steve Odland: We're talking about carbon, carbon fluency, and sustainability 315 00:13:42,406 --> 00:13:44,736 metrics at DuPont and broadly. 316 00:13:44,976 --> 00:13:46,986 We're going to take a short break and be right back. 317 00:13:47,746 --> 00:13:49,636 Welcome back to C-Suite Perspectives. 318 00:13:49,676 --> 00:13:52,756 I'm your host, Steve Odland, from the Conference Board, and I'm joined 319 00:13:52,756 --> mistakes that companies 326 00:14:15,066 --> 00:14:17,490 make when they're trying to get into this, and they're trying to 327 00:14:17,490 --> 00:14:19,060 talk about their carbon footprint? 328 the really second one is treating all carbon reductions as equally 331 00:14:27,160 --> 00:14:29,960 valuable without cost or business impact. 332 00:14:29,970 --> 00:14:32,120 So, like, we talk about structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear interview format with an identifiable break, logical progression from definitions to frameworks to common mistakes, though pacing is conversational rather than tightly signposted.

relevance.rationale: Corporate sustainability strategy is broadly relevant to ongoing ESG and climate policy debates, but this episode lacks specific hooks to current news events or political developments, functioning more as evergreen business content.

card.reason: Because this episode covers a major shift in how entertainment is consumed

scored.description: Stay informed faster with Australia Daily News Summary by NewsCard® — your daily news briefing in just 5 minutes. Perfect for your morning commute or coffee break. Each episode delivers the biggest stories from Australia and around the world, explained simply and clearly with insights that go beyond the headlines. Get your quick news update and understand what's happening — and why it matters.

latestEpisode.title: Australian financial year changes hit households, Geelong refinery fire disrupts fuel, Chinese missile test sparks defence debate

clip.text: and household budgets. Authorities are watching for knock-on effects while the energy market looks for stability. — — [curious] In Canberra, defence is back in sharp focus after warnings about Australia’s air and missile defence capability ... following a reported Chinese nuclear missile test. The debate now is not just about hardware, but readiness, range, and how quickly Australia can strengthen its protection in a more dangerous region. That has direct implications for national security, alliances, and defence spending. — — [serious] In federal politics, the pressure is building over economic management and migration settings ... with the government still facing scrutiny on cost of living, housing, and border policy.

transcript.scoringWindow: [warm] This is NewsCard Daily for Thursday July 9, 2026 ... the biggest stories from Australia and around the world in just minutes. [serious] We begin in Canberra where the new financial year changes are now hitting household budgets ... and they matter for millions of Australians. The minimum wage rises, super is paid more quickly, paid parental leave expands, and tax relief starts flowing through pay packets. For workers, that is some immediate help. For businesses, it means new payroll rules and higher costs at a time when many are still under pressure from inflation and weak demand. — — [serious] In Victoria, the fallout from the Geelong oil refinery fire is still a major concern ... with fuel supply disruptions rippling across the state and beyond. Any hit to refining capacity lands fast at the bowser, and that can quickly feed into freight costs and household budgets. Authorities are watching for knock-on effects while the energy market looks for stability. — — [curious] In Canberra, defence is back in sharp focus after warnings about Australia’s air and missile defence capability ... following a reported Chinese nuclear missile test. The debate now is not just about hardware, but readiness, range, and how quickly Australia can strengthen its protection in a more dangerous region. That has direct implications for national security, alliances, and defence spending. — — [serious] In federal politics, the pressure is building over economic management and migration settings ... with the government still facing scrutiny on cost of living, housing, and border policy. At the same time, the creation of a new Community Strong Australia party adds another sign that independents and minor parties are shaping the political map heading toward the next battle in Canberra. — — [urgent] Now to our region ... where Australia’s relationship with Pacific neighbours remains a key strategic story. The recent Nakamal Agreement with Vanuatu strengthens security, policing, climate, and infrastructure ties ... and that matters because Canberra is competing hard for influence in the Pacific while island nations face rising climate and economic pressure. — — [serious] Overseas in the Middle East, the Gaza war remains the most volatile international story ... with reports of continued Israeli strikes and mounting civilian tolls. The conflict is still driving humanitarian concern, regional tension, and political pressure on allies, including Australia, as governments weigh calls for restraint and aid access. — — [urgent] Meanwhile in the United States, politics and security are colliding again ... with major attention on the next phase of the presidential race and on immigration detention after another death in custody drew scrutiny. That feeds a broader debate about border enforcement, accountability, and the tone of American public life as the country stays deeply divided. — — [warm] That's NewsCard Daily. For more top stories and quick summaries that keep you informed in minutes, download NewsCard ... available in the App Store.

structure.rationale: Each story is cleanly segmented with tonal cues and brief transitions, giving the episode a reliable bulletin format, though the rapid-fire structure sacrifices depth and the close is a promotional tag rather than a substantive wrap.

relevance.rationale: The episode is squarely focused on timely, specific news and political developments — Australian fiscal policy changes, a defence debate triggered by a Chinese missile test, Pacific geopolitics, Gaza, and US politics — all anchored to a specific date with clear policy stakes.

clipability.rationale: Several segments open with self-contained framing — particularly the missile defence and Pacific agreement passages — but most clips would feel thin at 30-45 seconds given the deliberately compressed, summary-only style.

card.tagline: New financial year bites, missile test rattles defence, Pacific deal shifts alliances
card.description: Australia's July 2026 policy changes hit pay packets and businesses while a Chinese missile test reignites urgent debate about national air defence readiness.
card.reason: Because this episode covers the week's biggest Australian policy and security stories

scored.description:
My name is Peter Pru aka Peter Prusinowski and I help busy professionals build income focused portfolios through options trading, dividends, and smart investing...without the stress or staring at charts all day. I break down complex strategies like my Ark Options Strategy (cash-secured puts, covered calls, dividends, interest, and appreciation sales) in a simple, no-BS way. 

My goal? To help you take control of your financial future with strategies that actually work. Whether you’re new to options or looking to refine your approach, you’re in the right place.

Sell Options. Collect Premiums. Repeat

*Not financial advice, just sharing my thoughts*

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear throughline from mistake identification to mechanical explanation to corrected mental model, with a mid-episode product plug that briefly interrupts the flow.

relevance.rationale: This episode is personal finance and investment product analysis — covered call ETFs, options premium mechanics, and portfolio construction — with no connection to news or political events.

clipability.rationale: The 'contractual income vs. market derived income' distinction and the $50k scenario walkthrough are genuinely self-contained moments that could stand alone as a preview without much setup.

card.tagline: The costly mental model mistake that makes JEPI and SPYI dangerous for retirees
card.description: Peter Pru breaks down why treating covered call ETFs as bond replacements cost him thousands and how to correctly position them in a portfolio.
card.reason: Because this episode exposes a common ETF misconception that affects income investors

clipability.rationale: The 50-day moving average explainer is the most self-contained moment, but most observations require prior context about the day's market action to land meaningfully.

scored.description:
Why are so many Minnesota high school graduates unable to read or do math at grade level? Does Tim Walz actually think he's going to be the next president of the United States? And why does he keep swearing so much? 
 
These are the kinds of hard-hitting questions we get into every week on the American Experiment Podcast, where we unpack the week's biggest stories, interview Minnesota's movers and shakers, and "stop the tape" on clips of our state's most ridiculous elected officials. 
 
New episodes drop every Tuesday afternoon and are available on every major podcasting platform.

structure.rationale: The episode moves through distinct story segments with a clear conversational throughline, but transitions are informal and pacing is uneven, with tangents (the dog park drama) disrupting the policy focus before a guest interview segment begins.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers specific, timely Minnesota policy issues — SNAP penalties, a federal lawsuit, school budget negotiations, and a state paid leave program going dark — but the scope is largely state-level and the SNAP headline is the sharpest national hook.

clipability.rationale: The school board member's Facebook post about 'pissing on white corpses' and the observation that Minnesota's paid leave program has gone radio silent since April are genuinely self-contained, reaction-worthy moments, though most segments require at least minimal setup.

structure.rationale: The episode moves through distinct topic blocks (Iran/war, Russia-Ukraine, bond markets, license plate surveillance) with verbal signposting between segments, but transitions are abrupt and pacing within sections is loose and repetitive.

relevance.rationale: The episode directly engages with timely geopolitical developments — Trump pulling out of Iran ceasefire, the Russia-Ukraine war trajectory, Fed policy under new chair Kevin Warsh, and domestic surveillance expansion — all current and substantive, though the analysis is opinion-heavy rather than rigorously reported.

clipability.rationale: A few punchy declarative moments exist — particularly the bond market 5% threshold warning and the Russia-Ukraine 'cut off Zelensky' argument — but most require surrounding context to land and the host's conversational style dilutes standalone impact.

card.reason: Because this episode connects the Iran ceasefire collapse to your wallet

A voice for the New Right, Hammer delivers blistering commentary and weekly interviews with today's top conservative thinkers.

structure.rationale: The episode has a clear throughline — expert interview with Rich Goldberg followed by host analysis — with identifiable segments and a running four-goals framework, though pacing is somewhat uneven in the solo commentary section.

relevance.rationale: The episode is sharply focused on a specific, ongoing U.S.-Iran military and diplomatic conflict in 2026, including the failed MOU, Strait of Hormuz operations, and midterm political implications — exactly the kind of timely, substantive foreign policy coverage News/Politics listeners seek.

clipability.rationale: The Godfather Part III analogy and the 'bleeding out of arteries' regime assessment are genuinely self-contained, quotable moments, though most of the expert exchange requires some setup to land fully.

card.tagline: Trump called Iran's leaders 'scum' — so what happens to the MOU now?
card.description: Josh Hammer and FDD's Rich Goldberg break down why the Iran deal collapsed, what's next for the Strait of Hormuz, and whether Trump can close this out before midterms.
card.reason: Because the U.S.-Iran conflict just hit a critical turning point this week

scored.feedTitle: Podmastery: podcasting insights and advice for indie creators
scored.description:
Are you a podcaster who's looking to improve and grow your podcast? You're in the right place. Together, we'll help you attain 'podmastery'. 
I'm Neal Veglio, a veteran podcaster who first started dabbling with RSS in the early 2000s.
Podcasting is such a complex medium, with so many factors that can impact your success. It's my goal to cover all these topics with you, and help you maximise the results you're getting from your efforts.
And while my shows now enjoy thousands of listeners every single week, it wasn't always easy. 
When I took a career break from radio for a few years in 2007, I had to learn how to build audience without the lift of an FM frequency. 
I learned a lot from that experience.
I now help other podcasters to achieve their goals.
Each episode, I'll be offering you some insights into what I've done and what I've helped my  podcasting agency clients to do with all our podcasts in the hope we can help YOU increase your podcast's chances of becoming more successful! 
And ensuring you can avoid the dreaded 'podfade'!
Take one step closer to Podmastery. Click follow in your favourite podcast app, and let's grow together.

latestEpisode.title: What 5,000 Podcast Listeners Just Told Us About Indie Show Growth
