But for anything in podcasting infrastructure, clean is pretty much the equivalent of a Michelin star. Someone follows B2B Podcasting Insights on Apple Podcast. The new episode lands. Wanna watch? There's a button. Tap it. Watch it. Driving? Just tap the button again. Listen to it from exactly the same place it was. Clean. There's no, "Head over to our YouTube channel for the full experience." There's no, "This episode contains visuals best appreciated on our website. Go to www.blah."

transcript.scoringWindow: Around two months ago, I made the argument both for and against Apple Podcasts HLS video, which meant that, of course, I then had to go and actually enable it to test it because there's a version of podcasting about podcasting that stays permanently theoretical, and I find that version slightly embarrassing. I mean, some of my peers have no problem with spouting so-called expertise around stuff that they've never actually tried or achieved, but I hold myself to a much better standard. So I did it. My other podcast, B2B Podcasting Insights, I enabled video in Captivate, and this is a real commercial show. I mean, I make that show as a lead generation tool And I wanted some actual results to present. So this is my report, and hopefully, my podcasting Padawan, you will benefit from it Welcome to Podmastery Podcasting Insights. It's the podcast for indie creators who want more from their shows. I'm Neal Veglio, the podmaster, and each episode, I'm doing my level best to help you make progress on your journey towards podmastery. Now, if you've been following the last few episodes, you know that I've been working through the case for and against this, not just for you, but also my own head, to figure out whether or not I want Podnos, my podcasting agency, to start leveraging this on behalf of clients. I need the theory of it, what it is, what it isn't. Well, this episode is gonna be different from the usual ones because this is an actual field report. I've done it. I've enabled video in Apple Podcasts for my show, B2B Podcasting Insights, and that show's a little bit like this one, but it's specifically targeting people wanting to get the most out of their B2B podcasts, their branded podcasts, a show they're doing for the benefit of their business. And that kind of podcast is in a crowded market. At last count, I noted around 50 shows that I could class as a direct competitor to my own. And I'd heard that shows with HLS video enabled were getting prioritized in the feeds within Apple Podcasts. More on that in a second. But the point is, the show is now a video podcast on Apple Podcasts via Captivate, and I've now got two weeks of data and an honest verdict on the whole thing to share with you. So let's start with the basics Let's talk about what HLS video actually is. HLS stands for HTTP Live Streaming. Three letters that sound less like a streaming protocol and more like something you might see mentioned in a scary letter from HMRC. Also, it isn't, despite what your inbox's AI summary might suggest, as a high-level strategy document that someone needs back by Friday, complete with your comments. It's actually an Apple-developed streaming standard. What makes it different from previous attempts at video in podcasting, and believe me, there have been lots of attempts over the years, as far back as the mid-2000s when the video-enabled iPod Nano was released. Anyway, what makes this tech different from the MP4 pass-through standard that Libsyn have bragged on providing for years is that this tech streams in chunks. Your video gets broken into small segments and delivered at whatever quality the listener's connection can handle right at that moment. So instead of a long delay while the podcast apps download enough of that MP4 file to begin playing it to you, this protocol means it can start playing almost immediately. And with a strong signal, you get high quality. If you hit patchy 4G through a motorway tunnel, the quality adjusts automatically with no buffering, no spinning wheel of screw you while you scream about your phone, your service provider, and the life choices that all led you to this exact tunnel just as John on your favorite podcast was starting to explain email open rate success. The previous method that I just alluded to, as in attaching a video file directly to an RSS enclosure, was technically possible. Some hosts offered it Like I say, Libsyn bragged about it. In fact, when I told Libsyn that I was leaving them to move to Captivate a few years ago, that was one of the things that they mentioned. "Oh, you won't be able to publish video anymore." Almost nobody was publishing video via Libsyn anyway because most podcast apps just stripped the video and played the audio version anyway, which is kind of like the digital equivalent of ordering a steak and being handed just the peppercorn sauce and an IU- and an IOU note where the steak should have been. HLS works because Apple Podcasts is genuinely built for it. In fact, they invented it. It's not a workaround that a bunch of Silicon Valley nerds came up with just so they could enjoy watching production companies nagging Amy Poehler into applying some makeup for recordings. And it's a clean experience. I know, that's not a word that gets pulses racing. Nobody is subscribing to a magazine called The Clean Podcast User Experience Quarterly. Three issues a year, free tote bag if you renew before March. But for anything in podcasting infrastructure, clean is pretty much the equivalent of a Michelin star. Someone follows B2B Podcasting Insights on Apple Podcast. The new episode lands. Wanna watch? There's a button. Tap it. Watch it. Driving? Just tap the button again. Listen to it from exactly the same place it was. Clean. There's no, "Head over to our YouTube channel for the full experience." There's no, "This episode contains visuals best appreciated on our website. Go to www.blah." No QR code that you're scanning one-handed while doing 60 on the A40. There's none of that. The video just sits there. Offline downloads work too, the video version specifically, and I'm mentioning that because things in podcasting that are supposed to work and usually don't has become kind of a meme in the podcasting industry, certainly over the last six or seven years whenever Apple Podcasts tends to update something Now, before you make any plans at all, your podcast host needs to support this. If they don't, there's no clever workaround, there's no plugin, there's no bloke on Fiverr who can sort it all out for you for $15. The encoding and delivery isn't happening at creator level, it's at host level, full stop. Now, Captivate supports it, and that's what I use for B2B Podcasting Insights. So do Transistor, Acast, Art19, Omni, Libsyn, and Buzzsprout. I think I've got most of them there, if not all of them. Oh, rss.com, I think they now do it as well. A few of the others appear to be treating it less as a technical roadmap item and more for a long-running philosophical debate about the nature of video itself in podcasting. Now, look, I get it. It can be a bit annoying when new stuff happens in podcasting that gives us all an excuse to go, "I've been around in podcasting since before it was called podcasting, and if it doesn't have an RSS feed, it's not a podcast, and that includes video." But look, it does have an RSS feed now. So I look forward to hearing their conclusions on why they're still not doing it, possibly in time for the next Gen Alpha deciding to reinvent what podcasting is. Maybe they'll invent astral projection podcasting. Who knows? Anyway, the day Apple properly announced this, the usual fleet of podcasting commentators on LinkedIn had already formed their strong opinions and published thoughtful 10-page carousels, everyone with a different font, in roughly the time it took the rest of us to finish reading Apple's press release. And a fair number of those carousels didn't even seem to realize that the feature was dependent on who you host with. So we've learned to take these people's opinions with a massive pinch of salt, and that's why I'm bringing you here some real insight from a real podcast person, me, who's in the trenches doing it So let's talk about the workflow. Yes, it's changed. Not massively. Don't buy into the drama of, oh, it's gonna take 10 times longer than it used to to make podcasts now. Not massive changes like that, but it has changed, and how much that matters to you depends entirely on what your show is. Now look, B2B Podcasting Insights, my show that I have now enabled video for in Captivate, it's a monologue narrative show. I record it in BoomCastr. I'm telling you that now upfront, past- partly because that's the right thing to do and partly because I've spent many years mocking people who bury that kind of detail in paragraph four of a blog post that nobody was ever reading. Glass houses, you see? I'm fully aware. Right. The actual workflow change then. Uploading the video file to Captivate instead of just the audio, which does Right, the actual workflow change then. Uploading the video file to Captivate instead of just the audio does add way more time to the upload procedure. If you've done a webinar in the past four years, you're basically there already. You just might need some tweaks. Two, the edit has to work for two audiences at once. scored.description: Russia Decoded explains how the Kremlin shapes its narrative of current domestic and international events through tightly curated state news broadcasts. Co-hosts Andy Kuchins, a senior fellow at CFTNI and adjunct professor at Johns Hopkins SAIS in Washington, and Chris Monday, a professor of economics at Dongseo University in Busan, South Korea, are veteran observers of Soviet and Russian media. Kuchins and Monday frame their discussions around the weekly Russian news round-up program Vesti Nedeli (“News of the Week”), broadcast on Russia’s Channel 2 on Sunday evenings. Viewed through the right lens, Vesti Nedeli offers a uniquely revealing perspective on how Vladimir Putin and Russian elites understand the world, and how they seek to influence it.

latestEpisode.title: July 6, 2026: Does Putin Have a Way Out?

structure.rationale: The show moves through distinct news stories in a roundtable format with loose but identifiable transitions, though pacing is conversational and occasionally meandering between topics.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers timely cybersecurity news including a DHS network breach claim, a SharePoint KEV addition, Pegasus spyware in the EU Parliament, and Apple's Hide My Email flaw — all current, specific, and substantive infosec stories with clear policy and government dimensions.

clipability.rationale: The Windows 11 device identifier tracking exchange ('Are you telling me that operating systems track and collect information about their users?') and the DHS press-release-over-security riff are genuinely self-contained and quotable, but most moments require surrounding context to land.

card.tagline: DHS got breached — and their response was a press release, not a patch
card.description: This week's infosec roundup covers a DHS network breach, a critical SharePoint CVE, and a Pegasus spyware twist inside the EU Parliament investigating Pegasus.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down real government security failures happening right now

scored.description:
Free speech, culture, censorship, Big Tech, and the conversations shaping the future are all colliding in real time, and most people are only seeing a small piece of the picture. The Jeff Dornik Show brings together voices from across politics, business, health, faith, and culture to have real conversations that cut through narratives and get to the truth.

Hosted by Jeff Dornik, founder and CEO of Pickax, this show is built on one premise: the most important ideas are often the ones that are ignored, suppressed, or misunderstood. Through in-depth interviews and direct discussions, Jeff explores the forces influencing our world, from political agendas and cultural shifts to personal health, entrepreneurship, and the battle over free expression in the digital age.

This is not a scripted or controlled environment. Guests are free to speak, ideas are challenged openly, and conversations go where they need to go. Whether it is politics, business, faith, health, or culture, the goal is simple: honest dialogue without filters.

If you are looking for real conversations, diverse perspectives, and a deeper understanding of what is actually happening beneath the surface, this show is for you.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-jeff-dornik-show--4788100/support.

structure.rationale: The conversation meanders loosely from AI workforce displacement to Tower of Babel analogies to the Magi to EMPs, with no clear segmentation or deliberate pacing.

relevance.rationale: The episode is primarily religious/conspiratorial commentary on AI and technology through a biblical eschatology lens, not substantive news or political coverage of current events.

clipability.rationale: A few quotable lines exist (e.g., 'best thing for national security is pen and paper') but most moments require the surrounding theological framework to make sense as standalone clips.

card.tagline: Is AI a Satanic plot to conquer the world like the Tower of Babel?
card.description: Jeff Dornik argues quantum AI and transhumanism mirror biblical patterns of world domination, framing tech elites as modern-day Magi serving Satan's agenda.
card.reason: Because this episode frames quantum AI as a spiritual and existential threat

Your go-to source for UFO and UAP news. No fluff. Just the latest information.

Five episodes a week covering Pentagon reports, congressional testimonies, whistleblower revelations, leaked footage, and the latest developments in government disclosure with host Cristina Gomez. 

Visit UFONews.co for articles, archives, and extra content.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/strange-and-unexplained--5235662/support.

structure.rationale: The episode moves with a clear throughline from disinformation claims to comparative US/France disclosure approaches to polling data, with identifiable segments and guest voices, though transitions occasionally meander into tangents.

relevance.rationale: The episode is substantively tied to current political developments — Trump's anticipated UFO speech, congressional hearings with David Grusch, and France's parliamentary UAP forum — making it genuinely timely news/politics-adjacent content, though the core subject matter is paranormal rather than strictly political.

clipability.rationale: Several self-contained, quotable moments land well — particularly the 'staged disclosure' framing from the political scientist and the closing argument about state control versus public right to know — usable without heavy setup.

card.tagline: Is UFO disclosure a political power grab disguised as transparency?
card.description: Analysts argue Trump's UFO speech and congressional hearings are 'staged disclosure' designed to frame aliens as a threat and expand state control — not reveal truth.
card.reason: Because this episode interrogates the political machinery behind this week's UFO headlines

scored.description:
NaturalQueen77 TV is a true crime podcast dedicated to the cases the mainstream forgot. While the biggest names in crime get documentaries and bestsellers, countless victims and unsolved mysteries slip through the cracks. This show pulls them back into the light.

Each episode dives deep into lesser-known murders, disappearances, and cold cases — examining the evidence, the suspects, and the systemic failures that left these cases gathering dust. And when a well-known case has more to the story than people think, we cover that too.

From small-town tragedies to overlooked urban cases to infamous crimes that still have unanswered questions, NaturalQueen77 TV tells the stories that deserve airtime.
New episodes drop weekly. Subscribe and follow on social media to join the conversation.

She's staying at a friend's place tonight. 4 00:00:24.839 --> 00:00:29.920 She'll be home tomorrow. Tomorrow never came. Nine years later, 5 00:00:30.079 --> 00:00:33.399 her mother is still waiting for the truth, and the 6 00:00:33.520 --> 00:00:36.679 man who may hold it has been sitting in a 7 00:00:36.759 --> 00:00:42.679 Florida prison cell for murdering someone else. This is the 8 00:00:42.799 --> 00:00:46.200 last known moment. I'm your host, and this is the 9 00:00:46.240 --> 00:00:51.200 story of Keyshae Jacobs. Before we go further, this episode 10 00:00:51.240 --> 00:00:55.799 discusses violent crime, dismemberment, and the disappearance of a young 11 00:00:55.880 --> 00:00:59.359 black woman. If that's not something you can sit with today, 12 00:00:59.520 --> 00:01:03.359 that's okay, Hey, come back to us next week. Everything 13 00:01:03.479 --> 00:01:07.239 in this episode is sourced from Richmond Police Department statements, 14 00:01:07.560 --> 00:01:14.159 court records, and reporting from WRIIC, WTVR twelve On Your Side, 15 00:01:14.560 --> 00:01:19.680 and the Charlie Project Missing Persons Database. No speculation is 16 00:01:19.799 --> 00:01:24.239 presented as fact. Keysha Unique Jacobs was twenty one years old. 17 00:01:24.680 --> 00:01:28.480 She stood five foot three. She lived in Richmond's East End, 18 00:01:28.560 --> 00:01:32.680 the Churchhill area, close to family, close to home. By 19 00:01:32.760 --> 00:01:35.959 every account, she was the kind of daughter who checked in, 20 00:01:36.519 --> 00:01:40.000 who texted her mother Tony to let her know where 21 00:01:40.040 --> 00:01:43.319 she was and when she'd be back. That's exactly what 22 00:01:43.480 --> 00:01:46.719 she did. On the twenty sixth of September twenty sixteen, 23 00:01:47.280 --> 00:01:50.439 Kyshay told her mother she was heading out, last seen 24 00:01:50.480 --> 00:01:54.120 in the area near Chimborazzo Park and that she'd be 25 00:01:54.239 --> 00:01:57.719 staying somewhere for the night. She said she'd be home 26 00:01:57.799 --> 00:02:00.560 the next day. She had been in common contact with 27 00:02:00.640 --> 00:02:05.200 a man named Otis Lee Tucker. Investigators would later determine 28 00:02:05.239 --> 00:02:08.919 she was headed toward his residence. She never came home, 29 00:02:09.400 --> 00:02:13.439 She never made contact again, and for years no one 30 00:02:13.520 --> 00:02:16.960 knew what happened to her. Tony Jacobs didn't wait for 31 00:02:17.039 --> 00:02:21.560 police to tell her something was wrong. She knew almost immediately, 32 00:02:22.360 --> 00:02:27.080 and from very early on, she pointed investigators toward one name, 33 00:02:27.479 --> 00:02:32.680 Otis Tucker. Tucker had a documented history of violence stretching 34 00:02:32.759 --> 00:02:37.159 across multiple states. He was not a stranger to law enforcement, 35 00:02:37.599 --> 00:02:41.240 and in the days after Keishay vanished, he left Virginia. 36 00:02:41.759 --> 00:02:45.919 When investigators searched the location tied to Tucker, they found 37 00:02:46.000 --> 00:02:52.479 Keyshay's simcard, They found blood stained items, physical evidence connecting 38 00:02:52.560 --> 00:02:57.840 her to that residence and to Tucker, But evidence connecting 39 00:02:57.879 --> 00:03:00.840 someone to a scene is not the same as evidence 40 00:03:00.879 --> 00:03:04.960 that proves what happened there. Richmond police pursued the case 41 00:03:05.000 --> 00:03:08.599 for years. Tucker was named a person of interest, but 42 00:03:08.759 --> 00:03:13.159 without a body, without a confession, without enough to charge him, 43 00:03:13.439 --> 00:03:18.840 the case stalled. While Keyshay's case sat unresolved, Otis, Tucker's 44 00:03:18.879 --> 00:03:23.199 violence didn't stop. In twenty twenty two, in Florida, Tucker 45 00:03:23.280 --> 00:03:27.439 murdered a woman named Ashley Fowler. He was convicted and 46 00:03:27.639 --> 00:03:32.560 sentenced to life in prison. It was that conviction that 47 00:03:32.800 --> 00:03:36.479 separate act of violence that would eventually crack open what 48 00:03:36.639 --> 00:03:40.840 happened to Keisha. In twenty twenty four, nearly eight years 49 00:03:40.919 --> 00:03:46.759 after Keyshe disappeared, Richmond investigators traveled to Jacksonville, Florida, to 50 00:03:46.840 --> 00:03:50.520 sit down with Tucker again, this time with help from 51 00:03:50.520 --> 00:03:55.919 an unexpected source, Tucker's own father, who traveled with police 52 00:03:56.000 --> 00:04:00.280 and encouraged his son to finally tell the truth. Tuckerer 53 00:04:00.360 --> 00:04:04.319 admitted he was with Keishay when she died. He claimed 54 00:04:04.400 --> 00:04:07.879 she died of an overdose in his home. He admitted 55 00:04:07.960 --> 00:04:11.199 to disposing of her body, He did not confess to 56 00:04:11.280 --> 00:04:15.479 killing her. To secure that admission, Richmond police reached an 57 00:04:15.520 --> 00:04:19.000 agreement with Tucker they would not use his statements about 58 00:04:19.079 --> 00:04:23.480 Keishay's death against him in future prosecution. It was a 59 00:04:23.560 --> 00:04:29.319 trade the truth in exchange for accountability he'd likely never faced. 60 00:04:29.920 --> 00:04:33.920 On the eleventh of July twenty twenty four, Richmond Police 61 00:04:34.000 --> 00:04:38.279 Chief Rick Edwards stood in front of cameras and confirmed 62 00:04:38.319 --> 00:04:42.759 what Tony Jacobs had feared for nearly eight years. Her 63 00:04:42.879 --> 00:04:48.399 daughter was dead. Quote. It's a very sad story, Edwards said, 64 00:04:48.759 --> 00:04:51.759 and I know all of us hoped she would be found, 65 00:04:51.959 --> 00:04:56.560 but unfortunately she was not. End quote. The method Tucker 66 00:04:56.680 --> 00:04:59.879 used to dispose of Keishay's remains has made it nearly 67 00:05:00.120 --> 00:05:04.920 impossible for investigators to recover them. As of this recording, 68 00:05:04.959 --> 00:05:08.680 her body has never been found. Kishey's case has been 69 00:05:08.720 --> 00:05:14.120 classified as exceptionally closed, a designation law enforcement uses when 70 00:05:14.160 --> 00:05:17.560 they believe they know what happened but don't have enough 71 00:05:17.560 --> 00:05:22.160 admissible evidence to bring charges. Otis Tucker has never been 72 00:05:22.279 --> 00:05:26.399 charged in connection with her death. A December twenty twenty 73 00:05:26.439 --> 00:05:31.519 five investigative report revisited the case, raising hard questions about 74 00:05:31.519 --> 00:05:35.279 the strength of Tucker's confession and about how the investigation 75 00:05:35.519 --> 00:05:39.720 was handled in the years before it, those questions remain open. 76 00:05:40.199 --> 00:05:44.920 Keisha Jacobs disappeared in twenty sixteen. It took eight years 77 00:05:44.920 --> 00:05:47.439 for her family to get an answer, and the answer 78 00:05:47.480 --> 00:05:52.519 they got still isn't justice. No charges, no trial, no 79 00:05:52.639 --> 00:05:56.040 accountability for the man who admitted to disposing of her body. 80 00:05:56.480 --> 00:06:00.279 Her story sits inside a pattern we talk about allen 81 00:06:00.319 --> 00:06:04.759 on this show. Cases involving missing Black women that move slower, 82 00:06:05.240 --> 00:06:09.639 get less coverage, and too often get closed without consequence. 83 00:06:10.079 --> 00:06:15.199 Tony Jacobs is still out there, still carrying her daughter's name, 84 00:06:15.839 --> 00:06:19.800 still asking the same question. So many mothers on this 85 00:06:19.959 --> 00:06:23.839 show have asked us to ask on their behalf, who 86 00:06:23.920 --> 00:06:26.959 is going to answer for this? If you have any 87 00:06:27.000 --> 00:06:32.160 information about kish Jacob's case, please contact the Richmond Police 88 00:06:32.160 --> 00:06:36.680 Department Cold Case Unit. This has been the last known moment. 89 00:06:37.439 --> 00:06:41.720 Thank you for listening and for remembering her name. Known moment.

relevance.rationale: This is a true crime/missing persons episode — well-produced and socially conscious, but not a News/Politics episode; the racial disparity angle is noted but does not transform the content into political or policy coverage.

clipability.rationale: The opening hook ('Tomorrow never came. Nine years later, her mother is still waiting') and the closing indictment of systemic neglect of missing Black women both function as strong standalone moments requiring minimal context.

card.tagline: Eight years, one admission, zero charges — Keeshae Jacobs still has no justice
card.description: A Richmond woman vanished in 2016; the man who admitted to disposing of her body sits in prison for killing someone else, uncharged in her death.
card.reason: Because this case exposes how missing Black women are failed by the justice system

Stay informed, stay creative, and stay ahead with daily AI news made simple.

structure.rationale: Loose conversational format that drifts between Rangers, Devils, Knicks, Giants, and World Cup without clear segment breaks or deliberate pacing.

relevance.rationale: Pure New York sports talk — NHL trades, NBA free agency, Giants speculation — with no substantive political or news content beyond a passing July 4th holiday mention.

clipability.rationale: Most moments require prior context about specific trades or team situations; the AC joke at the close is the only genuinely standalone exchange, but it's trivial.

card.tagline: Rangers trades, Knicks championship buzz, and LeBron rumors on July 4th
card.description: Terminello and Alex break down the Rangers' draft-pick trade, Trocheck to Utah, Nico Hischier's extension, and whether the Knicks' title can lift all of New York.
card.reason: Because you follow New York metro sports teams and offseason moves

Williamson Health Sells to Ascension (MauryCountySource) Williamson Health’s Board of Trustees has voted to sell the county-owned health system to Ascension, a move that would end nearly seven decades of county ownership if approved by the Williamson County Commission, the Nashville Business Journal reports. The board unanimously approved Ascension’s proposal during a special meeting Monday. The agreement includes a $700 million purchase price, along with commitments for additional investments that bring the total value of the deal to about $950 million. Under the proposal, Ascension would invest $235 million over the

transcript.scoringWindow: the library director last week. Three women, Kara Williams, Carla Walker and Rita Parks, were nominated for two open seats on the library board. Williams received the county representative seat with 11 of 20 votes and Walker was chosen for the seat representing the City of Columbia by acclamation. After some debate, the commission also voted to raise Library Director Meghan Cyr’s salary from $80,000 to $95,000, to compete with the offer she had received from the Spring Hill Library the previous week. Officials and commissioners argued that retaining Cyr was worth spending $15,000 and skipping Budget Committee review. County Finance Director Doug Lukonen praised her as a hard worker and his favorite of the five library directors he has worked with. Library Board Trustee Beverly Simmons highlighted Cyr’s oversight of the Columbia branch renovation, work on the new Mount Pleasant Library, long-overdue accuracy improvements to the card catalogs, expansion of the digital and print collections, cross-departmental and nonprofit collaborations and morale improvements that have made Columbia and Mount Pleasant branches partners instead of rivals. Though the Spring Hill offer was tempting, Simmons said that Cyr’s heart is with the county library system. “It’s fiscally responsible to retain Director Cyr,” Simmons told the commission. “The process of finding another [director]… will be expensive, with no guarantee of finding someone as qualified or capable as she.” “I think we should do everything in our power to keep her,” agreed County Mayor Sheila Butt. Commissioners Gabe Howard, Tommy Wolaver and Kevin Markham wanted to send the raise back to the budget committee for review and accommodation in the fiscal year 2026-2027 budget. They observed that a raise of almost 20 percent would necessitate either moving funds around or withdrawing money from the county’s reserves — decisions which fall to the budget committee, especially during the closing of budget season. Commissioner Scott Sumners, one of the architects of the annual budget, countered that holding the vote was the timely thing to do, even if the raise would probably create a general-fund deficit of the same amount. “She’s gonna be gone by [July]… I think time is of the essence on this,” he warned. The commission voted 6-14 against sending the raise back to budget and approved the raise 15-5. MRMG Recognized for Diabetes Care (MSM) Maury Regional Medical Center has been recognized among a select group of hospitals nationwide for its commitment to delivering high-quality care to people living with diabetes. The Leapfrog Group and the American Diabetes Association® (ADA) recently named Maury Regional Medical Center a “Recognized Leader in Caring for People Living with Diabetes,” making it one of just 37 hospitals across 14 states to receive the designation in 2026. “Diabetes is a disease well known to shorten people’s lives if not well managed. This recognition reflects the exceptional commitment of our physicians, nurses, pharmacists, dietitians, diabetes educators and care teams who work collaboratively every day to support patients living with diabetes,” said Martin Chaney, MD, chief executive officer for Maury Regional Health. The Recognized Leader in Caring for People Living with Diabetes program is in its third year, evaluating hospitals based on their care for patients with diabetes during admission, hospital stay, and discharge. The program recognizes hospitals aligned with the ADA’s Standards of Care in Diabetes and Leapfrog’s standards for excellence in hospital safety and quality. “People living with diabetes face added risks when hospitalized, and institutions like Maury Regional Medical Center are going the extra mile for the safety of adults living with diabetes,” said Leah Binder, president and CEO of The Leapfrog Group. “We congratulate Maury Regional Medical Center for being one of 37 hospitals across the U.S. to earn this distinction.” Hospitals are assessed both on the implementation of these processes and on how well they execute care delivered to patients. The designation highlights hospitals that demonstrate excellence in key areas of diabetes management and patient safety, including: • Blood glucose (blood sugar) testing and hypoglycemia (low blood glucose) protocols • Specialized preparation for inpatient surgery • Meal and insulin regimen planning • Robust discharge planning for high-risk patients with diabetes • Patient safety standards • Learn more about the Recognized Leader in Caring for People Living with Diabetes designation, including full details on the standards and instructions for hospitals applying for the recognition at www.leapfroggroup.org. School District Names Instruction Coordinator (Press Release) Maury County Public Schools is pleased to announce the addition of Dr. Michele Murray as the school district's new Instruction Coordinator, bringing nearly three decades of educational experience and a proven record of instructional leadership to Maury County. Dr. Murray began her career in education in 1997, serving in a variety of roles. Her experience includes teaching multiple subjects across elementary and middle school grade levels, serving as a K-8 school interventionist, K-5 elementary academic coach, and higher education student teaching supervisor and adjunct professor. Most recently, Dr. Murray completed her 10th year as a school administrator. Her leadership experience includes two years as a K-8 assistant principal, five years as an elementary principal with Sullivan County Schools and the past three years as an elementary principal with Williamson County Schools. "Maury County Public Schools continues to build on our academic momentum, attracting outstanding educational leaders that are essential to our success," said Lisa Ventura, superintendent of Maury County Public Schools. "Dr. Murray's extensive experience in curriculum, instruction and school leadership, combined with her passion for supporting educators and student achievement, makes her an outstanding addition to our school district. We look forward to the positive impact she will have on our schools." Dr. Murray is eager to begin collaborating with educators across the school district to support teaching and learning. "I am honored to join Maury County Public Schools and begin this new chapter alongside an incredible team," Dr. Murray said. "I'm grateful to join a district committed to excellence and look forward to partnering with dedicated educators to support the exceptional work already happening systemwide." Dr. Murray earned her bachelor's degree in elementary education, master's degree in early childhood education, and doctorate in educational leadership from East Tennessee State University. Williamson Health Sells to Ascension (MauryCountySource) Williamson Health’s Board of Trustees has voted to sell the county-owned health system to Ascension, a move that would end nearly seven decades of county ownership if approved by the Williamson County Commission, the Nashville Business Journal reports. The board unanimously approved Ascension’s proposal during a special meeting Monday. The agreement includes a $700 million purchase price, along with commitments for additional investments that bring the total value of the deal to about $950 million. Under the proposal, Ascension would invest $235 million over the next 10 years for facility improvements and electronic health records. The company also pledged another $140 million for strategic projects during the first five years, plus $20 million for routine capital improvements. The sale is not final. The Williamson County Commission must approve the agreement before it can move forward. Commissioners are scheduled to discuss the board’s decision during a special meeting Monday evening, with a vote on a letter of intent expected in September. Williamson Health began exploring its future last year after financial projections showed the system could face annual losses of more than $31 million by 2029. Leaders also determined the hospital would need about $150 million in additional capital improvements over the next five years. During the review process, Williamson Health contacted 28 healthcare organizations about a possible partnership or sale before narrowing the list to several finalists. Ascension was ultimately selected over the remaining bidders. Board members also considered ways to keep Williamson Health independent, including reducing costs, seeking more funding, negotiating higher insurance reimbursements, converting to a nonprofit, and requesting county tax support. According to the board, none of those options would provide the long-term funding needed to keep the health system financially stable. More than 70 employees, community members, and local leaders attended Monday’s meeting. No one spoke during the public comment period. (Mayoral Forum (Press Release) Join Maury Alliance on July 9th at 5:30pm at the Cherry Theater on the campus of Columbia State Community College to hear from the two Maury County Mayoral candidates: Sheila Butt and Gabe Howard. This moderated Q&A session with the candidates will give attendees an opportunity to learn more about each candidate and their vision for the future of Maury County. If you would like to submit a question for consideration, please send them to Alyssa at atate@mauryalliance.com. This event is open to the public and free to attend! Doors open at 5pm, and the program will begin promptly at 5:30pm. Spring Hill Budget Commended (MauryCountySource) On Friday, June 26, the City of Spring Hill received official word from the State of Tennessee Comptroller’s Office that the City’s 2027 Fiscal Year budget has been approved, receiving recognition from the State Comptroller as being a “Distinguished Submission.” The budget was submitted shortly after it was passed on second reading by the Spring Hill Board of Mayor and Aldermen on June 15, via Ordinance 26-10. “This recognition from the State Comptroller reflects the thoughtful planning, fiscal discipline, and teamwork that went into developing our FY27 budget. I want to especially thank Finance Director Rebecca Holden and Budget Manager Chelsea Perryman for their outstanding leadership and dedication,” said Mayor Matt Fitterer. “Our shared commitment to transparency, accountability, and financial stewardship helps ensure that the City of Spring Hill is well-positioned to meet the needs of residents today while planning responsibly for tomorrow while minimizing burden on taxpayers. This distinction reflects the high standard our team strives to uphold in serving our community.” “We commend the governing body and local officials for submitting a well presented, timely, and organized budget. Complete and detailed submissions allow our office to efficiently review the budget and provide an accurate assessment within a timely manner which supports financial health in local governments in the State of Tennessee,” stated the letter from the Comptroller’s Office. “We greatly appreciate the detail, time, and effort that you put into the budget submission and look forward to continuing our work with Spring Hill to make government work better.” Spring Hill EMS Gets Year Extension on Service Contract (MSM) Emergency medical services will continue operating citywide in Spring Hill for at least another year after the Board of Mayor and Aldermen approved a one-year extension of the city’s existing EMS interlocal agreement last week. The board approved the resolution extending the agreement between Spring Hill, Williamson County and Williamson Health while local leaders evaluate long-term options for emergency medical service delivery. Spring Hill was contracted for citywide EMS service in 2006. Under Tennessee law, emergency medical services are considered an essential county function requiring counties to provide the service directly or arrange for it to be provided. Since 2020, EMS services in Spring Hill have been provided through an agreement involving Williamson County and Williamson Health, allowing a single provider to serve residents on both sides of the Maury-Williamson county line. City staff said maintaining one provider throughout Spring Hill remains the most effective option, ensuring consistent service and avoiding a split EMS system based on county boundaries. Williamson County indicated EMS service would discontinue in the Maury County portion of Spring Hill at the end of the fiscal year unless Spring Hill provided a grant. Mayor Matt Fitterer said the city is not interested in bearing the costs that state law identifies as a county obligation. After discussions involving officials from Spring Hill, Maury County and Williamson County, the parties reached an agreement to extend the current arrangement for one year. City staff said the extension will provide time to evaluate long-term EMS solutions while maintaining continuity of care for residents and seamless coordination with Spring Hill Fire Department first responders. The resolution also authorizes Fitterer and City Administrator J. Cater Napier to represent the city’s interest in future negotiations regarding EMS service delivery and administrations. And now, Your Hometown Memorials, Sponsored by Oakes & Nichols Funeral Home…

structure.rationale: Unscripted press gaggle format with rapid topic-hopping and no deliberate structure — follows the chaotic rhythm of reporter questions rather than any editorial throughline.

relevance.rationale: Raw Air Force One press gaggle with Trump covering voter ID legislation, Venezuela opposition politics, Medicare GLP-1 drug coverage, drug pricing policy, immigration and housing costs, and a Senate candidate scandal — all timely, specific political developments.

clipability.rationale: Several self-contained moments work as standalone clips — Trump on Nobel Prize, drug prices going from highest to lowest in the world, and immigration driving housing costs — each punchy enough to stand alone with minimal context.

card.tagline: Trump on Air Force One: Nobel Prize, drug prices, and Venezuela unfiltered
card.description: Raw July 8 press gaggle covers Medicare GLP-1 coverage, voter ID, India-Pakistan nuclear near-miss claims, and immigration's effect on housing costs.
card.reason: Because this episode captures Trump's unscripted positions on this week's policy debates

scored.description: Today, Explained is Vox's daily news explainer podcast. Hosts Sean Rameswaram and Noel King will guide you through the most important stories of the day.  Part of the Vox Media Podcast Network.

structure.rationale: The episode has a clear two-part structure (capabilities first, then risks/privacy) with an identifiable throughline, though ad breaks and a mid-segment promo interrupt the flow.

relevance.rationale: This episode covers AI smart glasses as a consumer technology product — a tech/gadget topic with no substantive political, legislative, or policy angle at its core.

clipability.rationale: Several self-contained moments work well as standalone clips, particularly the '30-minute battery life' exchange and the 'glorified headphones' verdict, which land without much setup.

card.tagline: Meta's smart glasses are out — but are they actually ready for real life?
card.description: A tech journalist who wears Meta smart glasses daily breaks down what the AI features can and can't do, from museum tours to 30-minute battery life.
card.reason: Because you've been curious about whether AI glasses are hype or reality

scored.description:
Welcome to the New Era Woman Podcast—
where purpose meets leadership and faith in real life.

This is a space for women who are healing, building, and becoming aligned. 

Each episode offers real conversations, biblical wisdom, and practical strategies to help you strengthen your inner world, hear God with confidence, and walk in peace, love, and emotional steadiness.

Whether you’re new to faith, returning to God, or seeking deeper discipleship, you belong here. 

Visit www.NewEraWoman.org and follow @newerawomanpodcast on IG to stay connected.

Follow us on Instagram at @newerawomanpodcast

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-new-era-woman-network--4771294/support.

Join Todd Herman on The Todd Herman Show offering unfiltered commentary on today’s critical issues. A former Seattle radio host and frequent Rush Limbaugh fill-in, Todd delivers sharp Christian conservative perspectives on politics and more. With a background at Microsoft and as a startup CEO, he blends real-world experience with fearless analysis. Subscribe for daily episodes, exclusive blogs, and show notes via Substack. Perfect for listeners seeking truth in a complex world.

scored.description: Real America’s Voice: Real News, Honest Views - Dive into a world of unfiltered news, in-depth analysis, and thought-provoking discussions featuring some of your favorite personalities. At Real America’s Voice, we cover a wide array of topics—politics, culture, and current events—through the lens of American values and freedom. We pride ourselves on delivering honest content that challenges mainstream narratives. Our passionate hosts are experts and commentators who offer unique insights on the issues that matter most to you. Whether you start your day with American Sunrise, follow breaking news in the War Room with Steve Bannon, gain political insights from Charlie Kirk, explore cultural commentary with Jack Posobiec, or tune-in for the latest with investigative reporter John Solomon and Just The News, you’ll find all your favorite programs on RAV right here on the RAV podcast, including coverage of our live events featured on the network. Join the Real America’s Voice community to stay informed, engaged, and inspired. Subscribe now and never miss an episode!

scored.feedTitle: The Daily Mojo with Brad Staggs
scored.description:
The Daily Mojo with Brad Staggs and Producer Ron Phillips is your first look each weekday, at the news, politics, faith, and absurdity that America is fast becoming. Brad and Producer Ron Phillips lens it all through a filter of truth, cynicism, and laughter (that’s getting tougher by the minute).

Our core value – We The People are at the heart of these United States and WE need to wrest our power back from the DC swamp dwellers. If you believe in freedom, personal responsibility, and individual rights, you may have found soulmates.

The Daily Mojo is life in the Conservatarian lane each weekday from 8-10a ET at TheDailyMojo.com, and wherever you get your favorite podcasts! Follow @RealBradStaggs @RealRonPhillips

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-daily-mojo-with-brad-staggs--3085897/support.

scored.description: Jim Webb Podcast—where real conversations meet sharp commentary. We dive into the latest trending topics, viral clips, and cultural debates, breaking them down with insight, honesty, and a touch of entertainment. Our goal is to cut through the noise, spark thought, and keep you engaged every step of the way. Hit that subscribe button and join the conversation today!

latestEpisode.title: The MOU is over! -  'Japan' Fires on USS Lincoln - Ukraine to manufacture Patriot Missiles

structure.rationale: There is a discernible throughline connecting multiple current events, but the host's commentary meanders between clips and topics without tight signposting or clear segment breaks.

relevance.rationale: The episode is sharply focused on active U.S.-Iran military escalation, sanctions policy, the Strait of Hormuz, and Ukraine weapons manufacturing — all timely, specific geopolitical developments with direct policy implications.

clipability.rationale: The host's analysis of Trump's 'mob-style' negotiating logic and the laws of armed conflict around civilian targeting are quotable standalone moments, though most require at least minimal setup from the surrounding clip.

card.tagline: Iran fires back, the MOU collapses, and Trump jokes about killing people
card.description: A military analyst breaks down escalating U.S.-Iran strikes, collapsed sanctions exemptions, and Ukraine's new Patriot missile manufacturing deal — and what it all means for the Strait of Hormuz.
card.reason: Because this episode covers the week's most dangerous geopolitical escalation

scored.feedTitle: The Right Stuff: A Blood Bowl Podcast
scored.description:
Yannie & Nathan talk about our in-store Blood Bowl League, Secret League! They go through each division, pick their favorites, and preview the week's matchups!

Hosts - David Yannarella & Nathan Starwalt
Audio Engineer - Scott Jensen 
Executive Producer - Wayne Knox

Don't forget to Like & Subscribe! Find all of our links here! https://linktr.ee/empiregamessc

structure.rationale: The show moves loosely between unrelated segments (rowing story, caller advice, ticket giveaway, gas prices) with minimal signposting or deliberate pacing typical of a live radio format.

relevance.rationale: This is a celebrity morning radio show covering entertainment gossip, a solo rowing record, a Fourth of July Venmo dispute, and BTS ticket giveaways — no meaningful connection to news or politics.

clipability.rationale: The Venmo fireworks caller segment is relatively self-contained and relatable, but most moments require the surrounding banter and radio context to land.

card.tagline: Should you pay a friend back for surprise Fourth of July fireworks?
card.description: Ryan Seacrest and crew debate a listener's post-party Venmo request for fireworks costs nobody agreed to split beforehand.
card.reason: Because this episode tackles a relatable summer social etiquette dilemma

scored.description:
Join the journey with David Knight, a true veteran in the realm of news and analysis, and become part of a community that values historical context, critical think, and spiritual insight in navigating the challenges of our era.  
LIVE every weekday morning at 9am Eastern where you'll find an honest, fresh perspective, not partisan cheerleading.  WIth a background as an electrical engineer, David is focused on the interplay of technology & liberty from a Christian perspective.

David has had decades of experience in the trenches of politics as an activist, organizer, candidate, reporter, analyst, and news anchor









Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-real-david-knight-show--5282736/support.

scored.description: Welcome to Furniture Industry News the premier podcast dedicated to executives navigating the ever-evolving world of furniture. In a landscape where the latest trends, market shifts, and innovative technologies can redefine success overnight, our podcast stands as your authoritative source for everything you need to stay ahead. Tune in to stay informed, inspired, and in step with the future of furniture. Furniture Industry News – where leaders come to learn, share, and shape the future of the furniture world.

Whether it works is still unproven, but it's a preview of how 87 00:03:41,770 --> 00:03:42,890 home retail could broaden. 88 00:03:43,370 --> 00:03:45,610 Let's shift to housing, because it's the backdrop for everything 89 00:03:45,610 --> 00:03:46,090 we do. 90 00:03:46,660 --> 00:03:48,820 Builder confidence slipped again in June, falling two points 91 00:03:48,820 --> 00:03:51,460 to 35 on the national association of Homebuilders and Wells 92 00:03:51,460 --> 00:03:52,660 Fargo Housing Market Index. 93 00:03:52,900 --> 00:03:55,820 To put that number in Context, anything above 50 means more builders 94 00:03:55,820 --> 00:03:57,020 see conditions as good than poor. 95 00:03:57,020 --> 00:03:59,140 So 35 is well into pessimistic territory. 96 00:03:59,220 --> 00:04:01,540 And this was the 14th straight month the reading has stayed below 97 00:04:01,540 --> 00:04:01,860 40. 98 00:04:02,020 --> 00:04:04,420 The last time it was this low for this long was during the foreclosure 99 00:04:04,420 --> 00:04:06,020 Crisis back in 2011 and 2012. 100 00:04:06,180 --> 00:04:09,380 Underneath that headline, the details tell the same story. 101 00:04:09,780 --> 00:04:13,920 The gauge of rose to 44, the Midwest held at 43, the 104 00:04:25,080 --> 00:04:28,880 south fell to 33 and the west fell to 27. 105 00:04:29,520 --> 00:04:31,520 Builders are responding with discounts. 106 00:04:31,760 --> 00:04:37,520 In June, 35% cut prices, up from 32% in May, with an average 107 00:04:37,520 --> 00:04:39,040 reduction of about 6%. 108 00:04:39,650 --> 00:04:44,250 And 62% used sales incentives, which marks the 15th straight month 109 00:04:44,250 --> 00:04:46,530 that figure has been at 60% or higher. 110 00:04:47,330 --> 00:04:49,650 The chairman of the association, Bill Owens,. scored.description:
Join the journey with David Knight, a true veteran in the realm of news and analysis, and become part of a community that values historical context, critical think, and spiritual insight in navigating the challenges of our era.  
LIVE every weekday morning at 9am Eastern where you'll find an honest, fresh perspective, not partisan cheerleading.  WIth a background as an electrical engineer, David is focused on the interplay of technology & liberty from a Christian perspective.

David has had decades of experience in the trenches of politics as an activist, organizer, candidate, reporter, analyst, and news anchor









Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-david-knight-show--2653468/support.

structure.rationale: The conversation meanders between sports diplomacy, political analysis, and candidate-specific commentary without clear segmentation, and the hosts frequently interrupt or redirect without deliberate pacing.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers timely 2026 midterm election dynamics, NRCC strategy, Democratic Party internal tensions, and specific races in New York, Colorado, and Maine, giving it clear news relevance, though the sports diplomacy opening dilutes focus.

clipability.rationale: LaHood's three-point Republican majority argument (candidate quality, resources, Democratic radicalism) is the most self-contained passage, but most exchanges require surrounding context to land and the crosstalk reduces standalone usability.

card.tagline: GOP's House majority strategist breaks down why Democrats are their own worst enemy
card.description: Illinois Congressman Darin LaHood, a key NRCC leader, argues socialist primary wins in New York and Maine are handing Republicans a 2026 midterm lifeline.
card.reason: Because this episode maps the Republican strategy for holding the House in November

scored.description: After many years on national television as a broadcaster for The Shopping Channel, Norm Murray has turned his attention to the people, events, and news from his own backyard – Peel Region. A Mississauga resident, Norm travels to various happenings across the GTA to find out – what IS “The Norm” in Peel? On-air, Norm invites conversation, as he engages in thorough discussion through live interviews. Notable interviews include: performers of The Living Arts Centre, Paramount Fine Foods Centre, and The International Centre – everyday people who entertain the community, specifically those who shy away from the “norm,” subsequently establishing a new “norm.” As he likes to say, “we’ve got a lot to talk about.”

latestEpisode.title: The Norm - July 7, 2026 - Ward 1 Update: Community, Growth, and the Future of Mississauga with Councillor Stephen Dasko

structure.rationale: The conversation follows a loose but identifiable throughline moving from regional council business to election season, though the pacing is conversational and meandering rather than deliberately structured.

relevance.rationale: The episode covers substantive local political content — Mississauga municipal elections, council voting mechanics, mayoral candidates, and regional policy motions — making it clearly on-topic for a News/Politics listener interested in Canadian municipal affairs.

clipability.rationale: Most exchanges require context about local figures and prior conversations to land, though Dasko's declaration that he's already campaigning regardless of competition offers one moderately self-contained moment.

scored.description: It's Okay, is a podcast about healing and growth. Each episode focuses on a different aspect of self-care, from improving mental health to exploring spirituality. We'll find ways to connect with ourselves and others to help us move through to a place of healing. Join us on our journey as we explore the power of resilience and the strength of community.

structure.rationale: The episode meanders between multiple unrelated stories with frequent personal digressions (watching TV with dad, cooking dinner) and no clear segmentation or throughline connecting the topics.

clipability.rationale: Most moments require surrounding context to land, and the host's frequent filler phrases and self-interruptions make it difficult to isolate a clean, standalone 30-45 second excerpt.

latestEpisode.title: Autopsy - The Death of Local Terrestrial Radio

clip.text: But you know what? 00:23:26.640 --> 00:23:30.079 All of that was preventable. 00:23:31.359 --> 00:23:46.480 And again, I wanted to talk about this since I left in 2015 because since I've lived where I live now, and people find out that I was in radio, one of the most common questions that I get even today is what's what's going on with radio? 00:23:46.720 --> 00:23:48.400 Does it exist anymore? 00:23:48.640 --> 00:23:50.240 What about these morning shows? 00:23:50.799 --> 00:23:52.559 What's going on with radio? 00:23:52.720 --> 00:23:56.400 It is nowhere near what it used to be, and it wasn't. 00:23:56.640 --> 00:24:03.599 So the thing that killed, not

transcript.scoringWindow: --> 00:22:08.000 It is almost impossible to win because again, you're competing with the rest of the nation. 00:22:08.079 --> 00:22:10.559 You're not competing with people in your community. 00:22:10.720 --> 00:22:14.319 You're competing with the entire competing with the entire nation. 00:22:14.559 --> 00:22:17.839 And that is just absolutely not going to work. 00:22:19.279 --> 00:22:31.839 And even the industry, you know, figured out that it was going to have to deal with massive layoffs, cost-cutting measures. 00:22:32.160 --> 00:22:36.319 Let's get back to the point that I brought up just a minute ago, the cost of a radio station. 00:22:36.480 --> 00:22:47.359 Back then, when all of this started, there was a simple formula that would determine the sale price of any one particular radio station in a particular market. 00:22:47.759 --> 00:22:52.720 And that formula was generally 12 times cash flow. 00:22:52.960 --> 00:23:13.279 So whatever your cash flow is in your radio station or your cluster, the sale price is going to be 12 times that amount, which means if you're the purchaser, you're the company buying these radio stations, that means you are so far in the red before you even close because you had to write a humongous check. 00:23:13.440 --> 00:23:15.519 So now you are going to have to look at things. 00:23:15.680 --> 00:23:18.000 You are going to have to look at cost-cutting measures. 00:23:18.160 --> 00:23:21.039 You're going to have to look at mass layoffs. 00:23:21.200 --> 00:23:24.000 You're going to have to increase commercial loads. 00:23:24.079 --> 00:23:25.440 And that's one of the biggest things. 00:23:25.599 --> 00:23:26.480 But you know what? 00:23:26.640 --> 00:23:30.079 All of that was preventable. 00:23:31.359 --> 00:23:46.480 And again, I wanted to talk about this since I left in 2015 because since I've lived where I live now, and people find out that I was in radio, one of the most common questions that I get even today is what's what's going on with radio? 00:23:46.720 --> 00:23:48.400 Does it exist anymore? 00:23:48.640 --> 00:23:50.240 What about these morning shows? 00:23:50.799 --> 00:23:52.559 What's going on with radio? 00:23:52.720 --> 00:23:56.400 It is nowhere near what it used to be, and it wasn't. 00:23:56.640 --> 00:24:03.599 So the thing that killed, not that local terrestrial radio is necessarily dead, but it's clearly on life support. 00:24:03.839 --> 00:24:05.839 And you know whose fault that is? 00:24:06.799 --> 00:24:08.880 The companies that did this. 00:24:09.680 --> 00:24:14.640 They did not think this through when they made these purchases. 00:24:14.799 --> 00:24:22.240 They started thinking on grander scales, on scales that would benefit the shareholders. 00:24:22.640 --> 00:24:24.240 That's what it was all about. 00:24:24.480 --> 00:24:37.680 They prioritized profit over anything and everything else, including people, community, neighborhoods, localism. 00:24:38.000 --> 00:24:44.240 They prioritized making sure that the shareholders got their share. 00:24:44.400 --> 00:24:50.880 And they were going to do whatever they had to do to keep that stock price up at a particular level. 00:24:51.039 --> 00:25:00.400 Now, one of the most common complaints that listeners had about terrestrial local radio up to that point were commercial loads. 00:25:00.640 --> 00:25:14.000 And believe me, as frustrated as you might have been at some time, or even maybe are now when you're watching even a TV station or you're listening to a radio station in the car, the amount of commercials is maddening. 00:25:14.079 --> 00:25:15.519 I was in radio for 30 years. 00:25:15.680 --> 00:25:18.559 I can't stand to listen to terrestrial radio anymore. 00:25:18.720 --> 00:25:22.000 They take a break and it just seems like these breaks go on forever. 00:25:22.160 --> 00:25:27.759 And it used to be radio commercials were made up of 60s and 30s. 00:25:27.920 --> 00:25:29.119 That's really what it was. 00:25:29.279 --> 00:25:45.839 And in most cases, outside of your what they call morning drive time, which is 6 to 10 a.m., outside of that, you're going to run probably three commercial breaks an hour and roughly four minutes worth of commercial units in that break. 00:25:46.000 --> 00:25:50.000 So when you're biding that up between 60s and 30s, it's really not going to be that much. 00:25:50.079 --> 00:25:53.200 And study after study has shown that listeners understand that. 00:25:53.359 --> 00:25:58.319 They know that's how radio and TV stations pay their bills, and they're willing to give you a little bit of commercial time. 00:25:58.559 --> 00:26:09.519 But what the industry decided they needed to do, because all the program directors basically revolted and said, playing 25 to 28 minutes an hour of commercials is ridiculous. 00:26:09.680 --> 00:26:14.240 We're going to lose our entire audience and our ratings are going to tank. 00:26:14.480 --> 00:26:15.440 And they did. 00:26:15.839 --> 00:26:20.720 And that's why the programmers all got together and said, look, we got to figure this out. 00:26:20.960 --> 00:26:27.599 Our thing was three breaks an hour with a maximum length of four and a half minutes. 00:26:27.759 --> 00:26:31.759 And that's when these companies decided, you know what we can do? 00:26:32.720 --> 00:26:39.440 We can start making 10-second commercials and 15-second commercials. 00:26:40.319 --> 00:26:43.839 Insert those into the commercial breaks. 00:26:44.160 --> 00:26:49.200 We'll be able to charge almost as much for those as we're charging for a regular spot. 00:26:51.440 --> 00:26:54.480 And we're going to get the deal of a lifetime. 00:26:55.119 --> 00:26:56.480 Didn't work out that way. 00:26:57.680 --> 00:26:59.039 Didn't work out that way. 00:26:59.599 --> 00:27:12.400 Because what you had is you had commercial breaks that consisted of maybe four or five elements, maybe four or five commercials, and then the break would end with some kind of a station promo or something like that. 00:27:12.559 --> 00:27:15.920 Well, now what you have, you still have the four-minute break. 00:27:16.160 --> 00:27:26.480 But instead of having four or five units to have to sit through, you've got 10, 12, 14, 16 units. 00:27:26.960 --> 00:27:43.599 And when you're listening to this in your car, at home, on your iPod, whatever, when you're listening to this, you're thinking, my God, this is the longest commercial break ever in the history of broadcasting. 00:27:43.839 --> 00:27:46.079 When time-wise, it isn't. 00:27:46.240 --> 00:27:48.640 It's the same amount that it's always been. 00:27:48.880 --> 00:27:52.319 But element-wise, yeah, it is crazy. 00:27:52.480 --> 00:27:56.400 And they also did studies when programmers started complaining about this. 00:27:56.559 --> 00:28:16.240 And the studies showed, and us programmers were begging our general managers and our VPs of programming to look at this information because what they found out was that listeners couldn't care less if a commercial is 10 seconds, 15 seconds, 30 seconds, or 60 seconds. 00:28:16.400 --> 00:28:18.880 All they know is that's a commercial. 00:28:19.039 --> 00:28:23.359 They view 10-second commercials the same way they view a 60. 00:28:23.519 --> 00:28:31.920 And when you've increased the number of elements from 5 to 13, not going to happen. 00:28:32.559 --> 00:28:34.319 Absolutely not going to happen. 00:28:34.559 --> 00:28:37.279 And the other thing, the advertiser. 00:28:37.839 --> 00:28:42.559 The advertiser gets completely hosed in that situation. 00:28:42.799 --> 00:28:54.319 Again, when you have a format where you've got three four and a half minute breaks per hour, which, and again, the number of elements might be four or five. 00:28:54.559 --> 00:29:06.079 Well, the problem came when the 10s and the 15s came into play because how would you like to be an advertiser and you're kind of skittish about spending money on advertising? 00:29:06.160 --> 00:29:12.240 You've never done it before, but you've got a radio salesperson in there all the time who's trying to convince you to do this, and you're thinking, I don't know. 00:29:12.319 --> 00:29:13.759 I don't know if it's going to work for me. 00:29:13.920 --> 00:29:15.759 Um, I'm going to have to see results right away. 00:29:15.839 --> 00:29:18.240 And then they have to be too, well, you know, it's going to take time. 00:29:18.319 --> 00:29:21.920 You know, it's going to have to get a little bit repetitive. 00:29:22.079 --> 00:29:24.559 You can't expect results right off the bat. 00:29:24.880 --> 00:29:32.559 But what they have done is they have sold these advertisers on the idea of 10s and 15s, which overall are cheaper. 00:29:32.799 --> 00:29:46.079 But what if you are an advertiser and you're so skittish about spending money on doing commercials and you are the second to last element in a 13-element break? 00:29:46.240 --> 00:29:51.119 Do you think that advertiser is getting his or her money's worth for their investment? 00:29:51.519 --> 00:29:52.799 Absolutely not. 00:29:53.279 --> 00:29:54.400 Absolutely not. 00:29:54.640 --> 00:30:00.799 And that's when they started doing things like sponsoring, you know, the studio that you broadcast in. 00:30:01.920 --> 00:30:09.839 Hey, you know, we're coming to you live this morning from the ABC Mechanic Garage and Wrecker Service. 00:30:10.319 --> 00:30:13.759 You know, for example, they don't, no, that's our station. 00:30:14.000 --> 00:30:15.359 We own the station. 00:30:15.599 --> 00:30:27.039 That advertiser does not own the station, but the perception could be that that advertiser has a stake in the radio station, which means they have a say in what's going on. 00:30:27.119 --> 00:30:30.240 And that was very, very frustrating for programmers to deal with. 00:30:30.960 --> 00:30:46.400 But the main thing that programmers dealt with was this corporate conglomerate landscape that for the local person was impossible to navigate. 00:30:46.559 --> 00:30:54.559 Because more often than not, you weren't in on the initial discussions for a new idea or something like this. 00:30:54.720 --> 00:30:59.200 Basically, they would get together in their corporate offices, they would decide this is what we're going to do. 00:30:59.279 --> 00:31:01.519 And then they send a memo to all the radio stations. 00:31:01.680 --> 00:31:04.720 Here's what you're going to do, here's how this promotion is going to work. 00:31:04.880 --> 00:31:08.880 And you're sitting there in your market thinking, man, I don't know if that's going to work. 00:31:09.200 --> 00:31:11.839 We just don't have that kind of a makeup here. 00:31:12.000 --> 00:31:16.319 The people who live here, this is not really who they are. 00:31:16.559 --> 00:31:18.319 But, you know, it doesn't matter. 00:31:18.559 --> 00:31:22.559 And so the local guys were ignored. 00:31:23.519 --> 00:31:27.599 And uh again, radio did this to itself. 00:31:27.920 --> 00:31:36.559 But when all the new technologies came in, and this ties into what I was talking about earlier: people asking questions about what happened to radio. 00:31:36.799 --> 00:31:40.240 Uh, did music streaming kill it? 00:31:40.480 --> 00:31:41.920 Did Spotify kill it? 00:31:42.079 --> 00:31:44.000 Did Pandora kill it? 00:31:44.160 --> 00:31:47.279 Um did Instagram kill it? 00:31:47.519 --> 00:31:47.920 What? 00:31:48.160 --> 00:31:58.160 No, those were great technologies that had huge potential for opportunities when they first came to fruition. 00:31:58.880 --> 00:32:11.200 But the problem was these companies, as excited as they were about the new technologies, knew that they were going to have to make a huge investment into these technologies. 00:32:11.359 --> 00:32:16.000 And they're going to have to try and recoup their cost almost immediately. 00:32:16.240 --> 00:32:30.240 So what they did was, again, much to the chagrin of local program directors everywhere, is that they created this system. 00:32:32.480 --> 00:32:39.680 I don't even know what else to call it, but you're being told this is what you're doing. 00:32:39.920 --> 00:32:45.039 You're not involved in the conversation, just do what we tell you to do. 00:32:46.240 --> 00:32:55.680 And that in radio, it that model can't work because radio was so special. 00:32:55.920 --> 00:32:57.839 Do you remember listening to the radio? 00:32:58.000 --> 00:33:11.440 It was kind of a neat experience, you know, to listen for your favorite song, or you would sit there with your cassette player, and your cassette player had an FM radio

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear analytical throughline — consolidation causes, commercial load failures, technology response — with identifiable segments, though pacing is conversational and occasionally repetitive.

relevance.rationale: This episode is a media industry retrospective about radio consolidation and commercial load strategy — entertainment/media business content with no substantive political, legislative, or policy angle at its core.

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear interview format with identifiable segment breaks, a logical progression from legal basics to enforcement gaps to reform proposals, though pacing is somewhat uneven and transitions rely on repetitive show-ID bumpers.

relevance.rationale: The episode addresses specific recent Austrian animal cruelty cases (Husky Buba, cat Schmotzer) and critiques current law, making it timely and substantive, though it is a niche legal/policy topic rather than top-tier political news.

clipability.rationale: The lawyer's explanation of why judges literally cannot sentence first-time offenders to prison under current law is a self-contained, quotable moment, and the international comparison segment also stands alone reasonably well.

card.tagline: Why no one in Austria goes to prison for torturing animals — explained
card.description: A criminal defense lawyer breaks down the legal loopholes that make Austria's animal cruelty law effectively toothless, citing two shocking recent cases.
card.reason: Because this episode exposes a specific legal failure behind this week's headlines

scored.feedTitle: Off the Record:  Portland Real Estate Insider
scored.author: Joe and Makayla
scored.description: Welcome to Off the Record: Portland Real Estate Insider, your ultimate source for all things real estate in Portland Metro! Join Joe and Makayla, as they take you on a thrilling journey behind the scenes of the bustling Portland Metro real estate market. From jaw-dropping success stories to nail-biting challenges, we spill the beans on it all, without holding back. Get ready to gain insider knowledge, uncover hidden gems, and discover the secrets of the trade that no one else will tell you. With our  expert insights, you'll be hooked from the very first episode. So, whether you're a seasoned investor, a first-time homebuyer, or just curious about the world of real estate, be sure to tune in to Off the Record: Portland Real Estate Insider. Don't miss out - hit that subscribe button now and join us on this thrilling ride!

And then, you know, within a matter of I don't know how long all of a sudden they get their vocal cords and it's like whoa, holy smokes. scored.author: Taylor Johnatakis
scored.description:
Peasants Perspective: A Voice from the Edge of Freedom  


Join Taylor Johnatakis, a self-proclaimed “peasant” turned podcaster, on an unfiltered journey through family, faith, and the fight for American ideals. From the depths of DC Jail—where he recorded during a 14-month sentence tied to January 6—to his triumphant return home after a Trump clemency in 2025, Taylor delivers raw, heartfelt commentary for the common man. Expect a mix of gritty storytelling, reflections on liberty lost and reclaimed, and timeless lessons drawn from his life as a septic designer, father, and reluctant rebel. Whether he’s reading Dr. Seuss to his kids or dissecting the state of the republic, Peasants Perspective is a bold, unpolished call to stay grounded amidst chaos. Subscribe for a front-row seat to a story that’s as real as it gets—no filter, no apologies.

clipability.rationale: The duct-tape-to-a-light-pole punchline is genuinely self-contained and amusing, but the segment is so short and breezy that most of it functions as setup for that one moment.

card.tagline: Mexican Batman is duct-taping bike thieves to light poles — and the cops approve
card.description: A Denver morning show's feel-good segment spotlights a Mexican vigilante who's been gifting duct-taped thieves to local police, complete with the stolen motorcycles.
card.reason: Because this episode delivers a quick, shareable feel-good story

Yeah, so the way that I see it as that Vantill in many ways is relying upon Kuiper's understanding of his spiritual sovereignty and developing it in terms of Christian philosophy rooted in God's word. structure.rationale: The interview follows a clear question-and-answer throughline moving from eschatology to sphere sovereignty to sins-vs-crimes to church-state separation, with identifiable segments, though pacing is uneven and the mid-episode ad break interrupts flow.

relevance.rationale: The episode is primarily theological and philosophical — a book discussion on Reformed political theology — with only incidental references to specific political events like Bill C9, keeping it in loosely relevant territory rather than substantive news/politics coverage.

clipability.rationale: A few quotable lines exist — 'there's not one square millimeter anywhere in all creation that Jesus does not cry mine' — but most exchanges require theological context to land, limiting standalone usability.

card.tagline: Should God's law govern the state? A Reformed theologian makes the case.
card.description: A deep-dive into sphere sovereignty, Kuyper, Van Til, and why one author argues separating God from the state is itself a religious act.
card.reason: Because this episode argues God's law should shape civil governance today

If you are even a casual boxing fan, you will want to be with us on the "Big Fight Weekend Boxing Podcast Feed."

structure.rationale: The episode moves methodically through individual fighters and bouts with a clear recap format, though pacing is conversational and segments blend together without hard breaks.

relevance.rationale: This is a boxing analysis podcast covering fight results, scorecards, and fighter matchups — sports entertainment content with no political or news dimension.

scored.feedTitle: Chicks on The Right - Conservative Politics & Culture Commentary
scored.author: Chicks On The Right - Conservative Commentary
scored.description:
Chicks on the Right is your daily dose of unapologetic common sense, humor, and sanity. Each weekday, Mock and Daisy break down politics, culture, and everyday absurdities with quick wit and relatable honesty. Whether they’re calling out hypocrisy or laughing through the madness, the Chicks offer conservative women (and plenty of men) a place to unwind, connect, and stay informed.

For more information, visit:
https://www.radioamerica.com/chicks-on-the-right/
https://heliosmedia.us/chicks-on-the-right/

structure.rationale: The episode moves through distinct topics (Vance/Israel, Candace Owens, Platner scandal, McConnell rumors, Sneako) with a recognizable talk-radio format, but transitions are loose and the dog tangent disrupts pacing.

relevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with current political figures and events — JD Vance's Israel comments, Americans attending Khamenei's funeral, and Candace Owens — giving it clear news/politics relevance, though the Sneako dog-rant segment drifts into culture-war commentary.

clipability.rationale: The JD Vance Israel rebuttal and the Jackson Hinkel funeral chant sequence are genuinely self-contained and punchy, though the Sneako dog digression requires too much setup to clip cleanly.

card.tagline: Americans chanting 'Death to America' at Khamenei's funeral — what now?
card.description: The Chicks dissect JD Vance's Israel rebuke, US citizens at Khamenei's funeral, the Platner scandal, and McConnell health rumors in one sprawling conservative culture-war roundup.
card.reason: Because this episode covers flashpoint conservative reactions to this week's news

scored.author: Amanda Sturgill
scored.description: Unspun is critical thinking about the news. Hear real examples, past and present, of newsmakers attempting to mislead you and understand how they manipulate the truth. Learn how to avoid being swayed by fake news and misinformation. Get Unspun, because you deserve the truth.

people 07:53.880 --> 08:02.290 [SPEAKER_02]: Now, the author's note quite correctly, but there are really big costs and communications if an organization distributes bad information. 08:03.432 --> 08:12.342 [SPEAKER_02]: And at the same time, there's been kind of an ongoing ethical question in the field about whether it's a good idea to use AI and if so, how can you use it? 08:13.123 --> 08:14.744 [SPEAKER_02]: Publishers have been kind of bullish on it. 08:14.824 --> 08:17.485 [SPEAKER_02]: I think with the idea that they can reduce their cost for staff. 08:18.645 --> 08:22.967

structure.rationale: The episode follows a clear journal-club format with an identifiable intro, framing of the research paper, and layered discussion of concepts like 'human in the loop' and 'knowledge decay,' though pacing is somewhat lecture-like and meandering in places.

relevance.rationale: The episode directly engages with a timely and substantive issue — AI's role in fact-checking and information integrity — with clear relevance to how news and political information is verified and consumed today.

clipability.rationale: Several standalone moments work well — particularly the 'someone can throw off lies a mile a minute and it takes hours to check those' line and the Ford Motor AI recall anecdote — but most insights require the surrounding setup to land fully.

card.tagline: Can AI fact-checkers actually be trusted — or are they making things worse?
card.description: Unspun breaks down new academic research on whether communication managers trust AI fact-checking tools, and why 'human in the loop' may be failing in practice.
card.reason: Because AI's role in shaping what you believe is the story right now
